title: Continuous Audio Thinking for Large Audio Language Models
summary: Large audio language models (LALMs) have shown impressive capabilities on diverse audio understanding tasks, ranging from speech transcription to music analysis. However, because LALMs are typically trained to produce text-aligned responses, their hidden states are progressively shaped for text generation rather than for preserving acoustic information. As a result, the diverse acoustic content that audio carries, such as phonetic detail, prosody, sound events, affect, and pitch, is lost along the way and difficult to leverage in the response. We introduce Continuous Audio Thinking (CoAT), a framework that equips audio language models with a continuous latent workspace for organizing acoustic information prior to response generation, grounded by distillation from audio experts. Within the thinking space, the model can utilize the rich acoustic information provided by expert distillation when generating its response. Furthermore, the proposed continuous thinking block can be processed in a single prefill, so CoAT does not require additional autoregressive decoding cost over the baseline. Across three LALMs, Qwen2-Audio, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, and Audio Flamingo~3, performance gains on a broad benchmark suite spanning audio reasoning, audio understanding, music classification, speech emotion, and speech transcription demonstrate the effectiveness of CoAT. Further analysis confirms that the auxiliary supervision propagates from the thinking positions to the model's textual responses.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Continuous Audio Thinking (CoAT), a framework that adds a continuous latent "thinking" space to large audio language models so they can better preserve and use rich acoustic information before generating text, improving performance on many audio-related tasks without extra decoding cost.
AI.motivation: Large audio language models (LALMs) are good at many audio understanding tasks but are trained primarily to output text. As information flows through the model, internal representations become increasingly optimized for text generation rather than for retaining detailed acoustic information like phonetics, prosody, sound events, affect, and pitch. This means that much of the rich acoustic content present in the input audio is lost and cannot be effectively leveraged when producing textual responses. The authors aim to address this mismatch by creating a mechanism that keeps and organizes acoustic information explicitly before text generation, so models can reason over audio more effectively.
AI.method: The authors introduce Continuous Audio Thinking (CoAT), which augments LALMs with a continuous latent "thinking" workspace specifically designed to capture and organize acoustic information prior to text generation. This workspace is realized as a continuous thinking block that sits between the audio encoder and the text decoder portions of the LALM. It is trained via distillation from specialized audio expert models, which provide rich supervisory signals about phonetic details, prosody, sound events, affect, pitch, etc. The expert knowledge is injected into the thinking positions so that the model’s hidden states there encode high-quality acoustic representations. Importantly, the continuous thinking block is processed in a single prefill step, not via extra autoregressive steps, so the method does not add decoding overhead at inference time. The framework is instantiated and evaluated on several existing LALMs, including Qwen2-Audio, Qwen2.5-Omni-7B, and Audio Flamingo 3.
AI.result: Applying CoAT to three different LALMs yields consistent performance improvements across a wide range of benchmarks, covering audio reasoning, general audio understanding, music classification, speech emotion recognition, and speech transcription. These empirical gains show that having a dedicated continuous acoustic thinking space, trained via expert distillation, leads to better utilization of audio information in the models’ outputs. Additional analyses show that the auxiliary supervision introduced at the thinking positions meaningfully influences and improves the textual responses, indicating successful propagation of audio-aware representations from the latent workspace into the final generated text.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Continuous Audio Thinking (CoAT) is an effective and efficient way to enhance large audio language models by providing a dedicated, expert-supervised latent space for organizing acoustic information before text generation. CoAT improves performance across diverse audio-related tasks without increasing autoregressive decoding cost and successfully transfers rich acoustic supervision from internal thinking representations to the final text outputs. This suggests that separating and enriching a continuous "thinking" phase for audio is a promising direction for advancing audio-language modeling and reasoning over complex acoustic content.

title: Redact or Keep? A Fully Local AI Cascade for Educational Dialogue De-Identification
summary: Educational dialogue is a valuable but sensitive resource for research: the same transcripts that capture authentic learning often capture personally identifiable information (PII) entangled with curricular content, where "Riemann" may refer to a real student or to a mathematical concept. Existing approaches force a tradeoff between governance and accuracy. Commercial Large Language Models (LLMs) can handle this ambiguity but require sending student data to third parties, while local named entity recognition (NER) systems preserve governance but over-redact curricular terms. We propose a fully local cascade framework that reframes de-identification from open-ended entity recognition to constrained privacy triage. A recall-first union proposer combines two lightweight encoders with deterministic rules to over-generate candidate spans; a context-aware reviewer then makes a binary Redact/Keep decision for each candidate using surrounding dialogue and speaker role. We evaluate three reviewer configurations against same-family LLM-only baselines and a commercial API on math tutoring transcripts from two large platforms. The strongest local configuration reaches 0.958 macro F1, compared with 0.767 for a same-family LLM-only baseline and 0.706 for the commercial API, while running entirely on a single laptop. On a targeted challenge set of curricular-personal name ambiguity, the same configuration degrades by only 0.03 F1 versus 0.19 to 0.25 for smaller reviewers. These results suggest that for educational de-identification, problem formulation matters more than model scale.

AI.tldr: They propose a fully local, two-stage de-identification pipeline for educational dialogue that reliably removes PII without over-redacting curricular terms, achieving higher F1 than both LLM-only and commercial API baselines while running on a laptop.
AI.motivation: Educational dialogue logs are highly valuable for learning analytics and AI in education, but they contain intertwined personally identifiable information (PII) and curricular content (e.g., a name that could be either a student or a mathematician). Current options either send data to third‑party LLMs (raising governance, privacy, and compliance concerns) or rely on traditional NER systems that aggressively redact, harming data utility by removing legitimate curricular terms. The authors want a way to preserve strict governance (fully local processing) while accurately distinguishing personal from curricular references, especially in ambiguous math tutoring contexts.
AI.method: They reframe de-identification as a constrained, two-stage privacy triage process instead of open-ended NER. First, a recall-first union proposer uses two lightweight encoders plus deterministic rules to over-generate candidate PII spans from the dialogue. Second, a context-aware reviewer considers each candidate span in its local conversational context (including surrounding text and speaker role) and makes a binary Redact/Keep decision. They instantiate three variants of the reviewer and compare them to (1) an LLM-only baseline from the same model family and (2) a commercial API, all evaluated on math tutoring transcripts from two large platforms. They also build a challenge set focused on ambiguous cases where curricular and personal names overlap.
AI.result: The best fully local cascade configuration achieves 0.958 macro F1 on the main de-identification task, outperforming a same-family LLM-only baseline (0.767 macro F1) and a commercial API (0.706 macro F1), while remaining efficient enough to run on a single laptop. On a challenge set specifically targeting curricular-personal name ambiguity, this strong configuration experiences only a 0.03 F1 drop, whereas smaller reviewer models degrade much more substantially (0.19–0.25 F1). This shows that the proposed formulation plus a sufficiently capable local reviewer yields state-of-the-art de-identification performance under strict governance constraints.
AI.conclusion: By casting educational de-identification as a cascade of recall-heavy candidate proposal followed by context-sensitive privacy triage, the authors can keep all computation local without sacrificing—and in fact improving—accuracy relative to LLM-only and commercial cloud solutions. Their findings indicate that how the problem is structured (union proposer + contextual reviewer) is more critical than simply scaling up model size, especially for subtle ambiguities between personal and curricular entities. This framework offers a practical, privacy-preserving path for using real educational dialogue at scale while complying with governance requirements.

title: SproutRAG: Attention-Guided Tree Search with Progressive Embeddings for Long-Document RAG
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems must balance retrieval granularity with contextual coherence, a challenge that existing methods address through LLM-guided chunking, single-level context expansion, or hierarchical summarization. These approaches variously depend on costly LLM calls during indexing or retrieval, limit context aggregation to a single granularity level, or introduce information loss through summarization. We present SproutRAG, an attention-guided hierarchical RAG framework that addresses this trade-off by organizing sentence-level chunks into progressively larger but semantically coherent units, using learned inter-sentence attention to construct a binary chunking tree. Unlike prior approaches that rely on external LLMs, fixed context expansion, or lossy summarization, SproutRAG learns which attention heads and layers best capture semantic document structure, enabling multi-granularity retrieval without additional LLM calls or compressed summaries. At retrieval time, SproutRAG uses hierarchical beam search to retrieve candidates at multiple granularities, capturing multi-sentence relevance beyond flat retrieval. The framework is trained end-to-end with a joint objective that improves both embeddings and tree structure. Experiments across four benchmarks spanning scientific, legal, and open-domain settings demonstrate that SproutRAG improves information efficiency (IE) by 6.1% on average over the strongest baseline. Code is available on https://github.com/AmirAbaskohi/SproutRAG.

AI.tldr: SproutRAG is a hierarchical RAG framework that builds an attention-guided binary chunking tree over sentence-level text to enable efficient multi-granularity retrieval without extra LLM calls or lossy summarization, improving information efficiency over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG systems struggle to balance fine-grained retrieval (good precision but poor coherence) with coarse-grained retrieval (better coherence but more noise). Prior solutions rely on expensive LLM-guided chunking, single-level context expansion, or summarization that loses information. The paper aims to design a retrieval framework that can flexibly access relevant context at multiple granularities, maintain semantic coherence, and avoid extra LLM cost or information loss.
AI.method: The authors propose SproutRAG, which first splits documents into sentence-level chunks and then learns a binary hierarchical chunking tree by using inter-sentence attention patterns from a transformer. The model learns which attention heads and layers best represent semantic structure, and groups sentences into progressively larger, coherent units. It is trained end-to-end with a joint objective that optimizes both the underlying embeddings and the learned tree structure. During retrieval, SproutRAG runs a hierarchical beam search over this tree to select candidate chunks at multiple levels of granularity, enabling multi-sentence and multi-paragraph relevance beyond flat retrieval.
AI.result: On four RAG benchmarks across scientific, legal, and open-domain tasks, SproutRAG achieves an average 6.1% improvement in information efficiency over the strongest baseline. This indicates that for a given budget of retrieved tokens, the method delivers more task-relevant information than competing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Attention-guided hierarchical organization of documents enables RAG systems to retrieve context at multiple granularities without relying on external LLMs or lossy summarization. By jointly learning embeddings and tree structure and using hierarchical beam search at query time, SproutRAG better captures document semantics and improves information efficiency across diverse domains.

title: Want Better Synthetic Data? Steer It: Activation Steering for Low-Resource Language Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become an effective tool for synthetic data generation, including for low-resource languages, where generated data can improve downstream task performance. Current best-performing approaches typically rely on few-shot prompting with target-language examples, which increases inference costs and may reduce diversity through lexical anchoring. In this work, we investigate activation steering as an alternative for low-resource synthetic data generation. We study two steering strategies: Language Steering, which targets the linguistic identity of a language, and Quality Steering, which captures well-formedness by contrasting human-written and backtranslated text representations. We evaluate these methods across four open-source LLMs, multiple layers, and 11 typologically diverse languages by generating sentiment and topic classification data and finetuning smaller classifiers. Steering is applied in both zero-shot and few-shot prompting settings and compared against non-steered counterparts. Our results show that steering on early layers consistently improves the diversity of generated data while often yielding stronger downstream model performance, particularly for low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper explores activation steering in large language models as a cheaper, more diverse alternative to few-shot prompting for generating synthetic data in low-resource languages, demonstrating that steering early layers improves data diversity and downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Few-shot prompting with target-language examples is currently the dominant way to generate synthetic data for low-resource languages, but it is costly at inference time and can reduce lexical diversity due to anchoring on the provided examples. The authors want a method that encourages the model to produce text in specific low-resource languages and of high quality, without relying heavily on explicit examples, to better support downstream tasks like sentiment and topic classification.
AI.method: The authors propose using activation steering—adding learned steering vectors to internal activations of LLMs—to control language and quality during generation. They design two steering strategies: (1) Language Steering, which learns activation directions representing the linguistic identity of a given language; and (2) Quality Steering, which learns directions that distinguish human-written from backtranslated text to promote well-formedness. They apply these steering vectors at different layers of four open-source LLMs when generating synthetic training data (sentiment and topic classification) for 11 typologically diverse languages, in both zero-shot and few-shot setups, and then fine-tune smaller classifiers on the resulting data.
AI.result: Across the evaluated LLMs and languages, steering applied to early layers consistently increases the diversity of generated synthetic data compared to non-steered baselines. In many cases—especially for low-resource languages—this more diverse synthetic data leads to improved performance of downstream sentiment and topic classifiers trained on it. The comparison against standard zero-shot and few-shot prompting shows that activation steering can match or surpass them while avoiding their higher inference cost and lexical anchoring issues.
AI.conclusion: Activation steering is a promising alternative to traditional prompting for synthetic data generation in low-resource languages. By steering early-layer activations toward desired language identity and text quality, models can produce more diverse and often more useful training data, improving downstream task performance. This suggests that control at the representation level, rather than via explicit examples in prompts, can be an effective strategy for low-resource NLP and may generalize to other controlled generation settings.

title: NAVI-Orbital: First In-Orbit Demonstration of a Zero-Shot Vision-Language Model for Autonomous Earth Observation
summary: As Earth Observation data generation outpaces downlink bandwidth and human-in-the-loop processing, a widening gap has emerged between onboard collection and actionable ground intelligence. This paper presents NAVI-Orbital, a software system deployed on a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) spacecraft. On April 16, 2026, NAVI-Orbital achieved what is, to the authors' knowledge, the first in-orbit demonstration of a vision-language model performing autonomous multi-modal inference entirely onboard. NAVI-Orbital uses a local vision-language model (Gemma 3) to classify each captured scene, produce a text description of its content and the relationships between its features, and respond to operator follow-up via natural-language dialogue. The system is re-tasked through plain-English prompts in place of conventional command sequences, and is orchestrated by a graph-based state machine (LangGraph) coordinating dedicated agents for detection and dialogue. Results across ground benchmarking (88.16% accuracy on the 7,960-image curated AID benchmark), Flatsat validation, and live in-orbit captures of newly acquired, previously unseen Earth imagery (including uncorrected YAM-9 imagery, processed onboard with hardware-accelerated GPU inference and no fine-tuning for the flight instrument) demonstrate the feasibility of running foundation models on satellite-class edge computers to invert the conventional acquire-then-downlink-everything bandwidth profile through semantic compression of Earth observations in-orbit.

AI.tldr: NAVI-Orbital is an onboard vision-language system that runs a foundation model on a LEO satellite to classify, describe, and converse about captured Earth imagery, enabling semantic compression instead of downlinking all raw data.
AI.motivation: Earth observation satellites are generating data faster than it can be downlinked or manually analyzed, creating a bottleneck between image acquisition and actionable intelligence. The authors aim to close this gap by moving more intelligence onboard so satellites can understand, filter, and summarize what they see before transmitting it, reducing bandwidth needs and latency for decision-making.
AI.method: The paper introduces NAVI-Orbital, a software stack deployed on a LEO spacecraft that runs a local vision-language model (Gemma 3). The system classifies scenes, generates natural-language descriptions of content and feature relationships, and supports interactive dialogue with operators. Tasking is done via plain-English prompts instead of traditional command sequences. A graph-based state machine (LangGraph) orchestrates specialized agents for detection and dialogue. The system is validated via ground benchmarking on the curated AID dataset, Flatsat tests, and an in-orbit demonstration using newly captured, previously unseen YAM-9 imagery, with GPU-accelerated inference and no fine-tuning for the flight sensor.
AI.result: On the ground AID benchmark, NAVI-Orbital achieves 88.16% accuracy on 7,960 images. It operates successfully on Flatsat hardware and in space, where it performs autonomous multi-modal inference entirely onboard a LEO spacecraft. It handles raw, uncorrected YAM-9 imagery and produces classifications and text descriptions, as well as interactive dialogue responses, using only the satellite’s edge compute resources.
AI.conclusion: Running foundation-scale vision-language models on satellite-class edge computers is practically feasible and can invert the traditional “collect-then-downlink-all” paradigm. By performing semantic understanding and compression of imagery in orbit, systems like NAVI-Orbital can significantly reduce required downlink bandwidth, enable natural-language re-tasking, and move toward more autonomous, responsive Earth observation missions.

title: JetFlow: Breaking the Scaling Ceiling of Speculative Decoding with Parallel Tree Drafting
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates autoregressive Large Language Models (LLMs) by drafting multiple tokens and verifying them in parallel, but it faces a scaling limitation: increasing the draft budget improves speed only when acceptance remains high and drafting overhead stays low. This ceiling has been difficult to break because prior head-based SD methods face a causality-efficiency dilemma. Autoregressive drafters produce path-conditioned candidates that are effective for tree speculative decoding with higher acceptance length, but their drafting cost grows with tree depth. Bidirectional block-diffusion drafters generate all positions in one pass, but their branch-agnostic marginals can form individually plausible yet mutually inconsistent trees, wasting budget and reducing acceptance. We propose JetFlow, a head-based SD framework that combines one-forward drafting efficiency with branch-wise causal conditioning. JetFlow trains a causal parallel draft head over fused hidden states from the frozen target model, producing candidate trees whose scores align with the target model's autoregressive factorization. This enables JetFlow to convert larger draft budgets into longer accepted prefixes and higher end-to-end speedup. Across math, coding, and chat benchmarks on dense and MoE Qwen3 models, JetFlow consistently outperforms bidirectional-head and tree-based SD baselines. On H100 GPUs, JetFlow achieves up to 9.64x speedup on MATH-500 and 4.58x on open-ended conversational workloads, with further latency gains demonstrated through vLLM integration under realistic serving loads. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/hao-ai-lab/JetFlow.

AI.tldr: JetFlow is a new speculative decoding framework that keeps the efficiency of one-pass drafting while preserving causal, branch-wise consistency, leading to higher token acceptance and much larger speedups than previous methods across math, coding, and chat benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speeds up autoregressive LLMs by drafting multiple tokens per step, but its benefits are limited by a trade-off: larger draft budgets only help if most drafted tokens are accepted and if the drafting overhead remains small. Existing approaches either (1) use autoregressive tree drafters that are well-aligned with the target model but become increasingly expensive as tree depth and branching grow, or (2) use bidirectional or diffusion-style drafters that are efficient but produce branch-agnostic, mutually inconsistent token proposals, reducing acceptance rates. The authors aim to overcome this causality-efficiency dilemma so that increasing the draft budget reliably translates into longer accepted prefixes and real end-to-end speedups in practical workloads.
AI.method: JetFlow introduces a head-based speculative decoding framework that adds a causal parallel draft head on top of a frozen target LLM. The key idea is to fuse the target model’s hidden states and train this draft head so that, in a single forward pass, it outputs a tree of candidate continuations whose probabilities factorize in the same causal (autoregressive) manner as the target model. This preserves branch-wise causal conditioning while avoiding the depth-dependent computational cost of fully autoregressive tree drafters. As a result, JetFlow can draft a large set of candidate tokens in one pass, yet the candidate scores remain consistent with the target model’s factorization, increasing the likelihood that the target model will accept long prefixes during verification. The framework is evaluated on dense and Mixture-of-Experts Qwen3 models and integrated into vLLM for realistic serving tests.
AI.result: JetFlow consistently outperforms both bidirectional-head and tree-based speculative decoding baselines across math, coding, and conversational benchmarks on Qwen3 models. It converts larger draft budgets into longer accepted prefixes and higher realized speedups. On H100 GPUs, JetFlow achieves up to 9.64× end-to-end speedup on the MATH-500 benchmark and up to 4.58× speedup on open-ended chat workloads. Additional experiments with vLLM integration show further latency improvements under realistic multi-request serving scenarios, demonstrating practical deployment benefits.
AI.conclusion: By aligning a parallel, one-pass draft head with the target model’s causal factorization, JetFlow breaks the traditional trade-off between drafting efficiency and causal consistency in speculative decoding. This design enables higher acceptance of drafted tokens and turns larger draft budgets into substantial, measurable speedups in real LLM applications. The method generalizes across task types and model architectures (dense and MoE), and the open-sourced code and models facilitate adoption and further research on scalable speculative decoding techniques.

title: CaVe-VLM-CoT: An Interpretable Vision-Language Model Framework
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) remain prone to hallucinations, producing fluent but visually unfaithful outputs. Existing chain-of-thought and retrieval-augmented methods only partially address this, as they neither enforce step-level citation grounding nor route verification failures back to retrieval for correction. We present CaVe-VLM-CoT, a modular reflection-based agentic-RAG framework that enforces evidence-grounded reasoning through a five-stage closed-loop pipeline: Extractor, Retriever, Solver, Citation Injector, and Verifier, in which detected ungrounded claims trigger structured feedback to the Extractor for targeted re-retrieval. Since no existing framework jointly measures retrieval quality, step-wise citation faithfulness, and cross-modal grounding, we propose a suite of 23 component-wise metrics across all stages, anchored by CaVeScore, a composite metric weighting accuracy, citation precision and recall, attribution, and evidence grounding. Without any architectural or prompt modifications, CaVe-VLM-CoT achieves 87.1\% accuracy and 56.6\% CaVeScore on ScienceQA , and 55.2\% accuracy and 35.7\% CaVeScore on MMMU (30 subjects).

AI.tldr: Introduces CaVe-VLM-CoT, a reflection-based agentic-RAG pipeline and new metrics to reduce and measure hallucinations in vision-language models via evidence-grounded chain-of-thought with citations.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models often hallucinate: they generate fluent answers that are not grounded in the input image or supporting documents. Existing techniques like chain-of-thought prompting and retrieval-augmented generation help but do not strictly enforce that each reasoning step is backed by explicit evidence, nor do they systematically correct hallucinations by looping back to retrieval. There is also no comprehensive evaluation framework that jointly assesses retrieval quality, reasoning faithfulness with citations, and cross-modal grounding. The paper aims to create both a method and a measurement suite to address these gaps.
AI.method: The authors propose CaVe-VLM-CoT, a modular, reflection-based agentic-RAG framework for VLMs. It consists of a five-stage closed-loop pipeline: (1) Extractor: identifies information needs or sub-questions from the original query; (2) Retriever: fetches relevant evidence (e.g., text or image regions) based on these extracted needs; (3) Solver: performs step-by-step reasoning (chain-of-thought) using the retrieved evidence; (4) Citation Injector: links each reasoning step or claim to specific retrieved evidence, enforcing explicit citations; (5) Verifier: checks whether each cited step is actually grounded in the evidence and consistent with the visual input. If ungrounded claims are detected, the Verifier sends structured feedback to the Extractor to refine queries and trigger targeted re-retrieval, closing the loop until a grounded solution is found or a limit is reached. On the evaluation side, they design 23 component-wise metrics spanning the pipeline, and define CaVeScore, a composite score that combines task accuracy, citation precision/recall, attribution correctness, and evidence grounding quality. The framework works with existing VLMs without changing their architecture or base prompts, acting as a wrapper agent around them.
AI.result: Applied to ScienceQA and MMMU (30 subjects), CaVe-VLM-CoT significantly improves performance while enforcing citation-grounded reasoning. On ScienceQA, it reaches 87.1% accuracy and 56.6% CaVeScore; on MMMU, it achieves 55.2% accuracy and 35.7% CaVeScore. These results show strong task performance along with measurable gains in evidence-grounded reasoning compared to baselines that lack the full closed-loop, citation-enforcing pipeline (though specific baselines and deltas are not detailed in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: CaVe-VLM-CoT demonstrates that a modular, reflection-based agentic-RAG wrapper can substantially improve both accuracy and evidence-grounded reasoning in vision-language models without modifying their core architectures. The closed-loop pipeline with explicit step-level citations and verification reduces hallucinations and enables fine-grained evaluation via a new suite of metrics, especially the composite CaVeScore. This establishes a practical framework for building more trustworthy, auditable VLM systems and a principled way to measure how well models ground their chain-of-thought in retrieved visual and textual evidence.

title: CoreMem: Riemannian Retrieval and Fisher-Guided Distillation for Long-Term Memory in Dialogue Agents
summary: Personalized dialogue agents require continuous long-term memory to maintain coherent interactions across multiple sessions. However, deploying these capabilities on consumer-grade hardware (e.g., 8 GB VRAM edge devices) introduces severe memory and compute bottlenecks. Existing systems typically rely on isotropic cosine similarity for retrieval and heuristic rules for context compression. These approaches lack a unified theoretical foundation, frequently suffering from the hubness problem in high-dimensional retrieval and syntactic fragmentation during compression. To overcome these limitations, we propose CoreMem, a resource-efficient edge-cloud memory architecture fundamentally unified by information geometry. First, Riemannian retrieval replaces cosine matching with a locally adaptive Fisher-Rao metric, effectively penalizing hub memories via Mahalanobis distance with O(Ndr) Woodbury acceleration for real-time search. Second, Fisher-guided discrete token distillation (FDTD) introduces a hierarchical sentence-to-token compression mechanism. It derives sensitivity scores from Fisher information traces, providing a principled compression-KL tradeoff augmented with explicit structural syntax protection. Evaluated on the LOCOMO and LongMemEval-S benchmarks, CoreMem achieves strong accuracy improvements, yielding substantial gains in Open-domain (+4.51 pp) and Temporal (+4.17 pp) reasoning. Extensive profiling confirms that CoreMem operates seamlessly within a strict 8 GB VRAM budget, successfully bridging the gap between resource-constrained edge devices and the demand for theoretically grounded, lifelong memory agents.

AI.tldr: CoreMem is a memory architecture for personalized dialogue agents that uses information geometry to provide efficient long-term memory retrieval and compression on 8 GB VRAM edge devices.
AI.motivation: Personalized dialogue agents need long-term memory across multiple sessions, but existing methods are inefficient on consumer hardware and rely on ad-hoc cosine similarity retrieval and heuristic compression. These lead to hubness issues in high-dimensional spaces and fragmented context after compression. A more principled, resource-efficient approach is needed.
AI.method: CoreMem introduces an edge-cloud memory architecture unified by information geometry. It replaces cosine similarity with Riemannian retrieval using a locally adaptive Fisher-Rao metric, realized as a Mahalanobis distance, and accelerates search via an O(N d r) Woodbury update. For compression, it proposes Fisher-guided discrete token distillation (FDTD), which computes Fisher information-based sensitivity scores to decide which tokens to keep, enforcing a compression–KL divergence tradeoff and preserving syntactic structure with hierarchical sentence-to-token compression.
AI.result: On the LOCOMO and LongMemEval-S benchmarks, CoreMem yields notable accuracy gains for long-term reasoning tasks, with improvements of +4.51 percentage points in Open-domain reasoning and +4.17 percentage points in Temporal reasoning, while staying within an 8 GB VRAM constraint.
AI.conclusion: CoreMem provides a theoretically grounded, information-geometric framework for both retrieval and compression in long-term memory for dialogue agents, addressing hubness and fragmentation while remaining deployable on resource-constrained edge devices. This bridges the gap between practical hardware limits and the need for lifelong, high-quality conversational memory.

title: Searching for Synergy in Shared Workspace Human-AI Collaboration
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Human-AI Co-Creativity. 13 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables
summary: Automated AI agents are increasingly capable, yet many scientific and professional tasks require human judgment and contextual expertise. We study shared-workspace human-AI teams, where AI agents and human collaborators must coordinate responsibilities before submitting a final answer. Using the Collaborative Gym environment with DiscoveryBench tasks, we examine when adding simulated human collaborators improves performance and when process loss turns additional collaborators into coordination overhead. Across 1,482 sessions, adding relevant collaborators can lower performance when teams lack structure to coordinate their contributions. We then evaluate scaffolding that combines shared group memory with simulated human-in-the-loop (HITL) gates, where selected actions require approval from a designated simulated participant. This scaffolding yields higher mean performance, most clearly in three-person teams, with clearer responsibility signals and stronger routing of expertise to team actions. Overall, how human-AI teams coordinate and integrate expertise matters as much as the capability available to them.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how coordination structure affects performance in shared-workspace human-AI teams and finds that scaffolding with group memory and human-in-the-loop gates improves team outcomes, especially in three-person teams.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become more capable, many real-world scientific and professional tasks still require human judgment and contextual expertise. However, simply adding more collaborators—human or AI—may not improve performance due to coordination challenges. The authors want to understand under what conditions additional (simulated) human collaborators help or hurt overall performance in human-AI teams, and how to structure collaboration so that expertise is effectively integrated rather than lost to process overhead.
AI.method: The authors use the Collaborative Gym environment with DiscoveryBench tasks to simulate shared-workspace human-AI teams. They systematically vary team compositions and collaboration structures across 1,482 sessions, adding simulated human collaborators with different relevance levels. They compare unstructured teams to teams using scaffolding that includes a shared group memory and human-in-the-loop (HITL) gates, where certain actions must be approved by a designated simulated participant. They then measure team performance and analyze when and why performance improves or degrades.
AI.result: The study shows that adding relevant simulated human collaborators can actually lower performance when teams lack sufficient structure to coordinate contributions, indicating significant process loss and coordination overhead. When the authors introduce scaffolding that combines shared group memory with HITL gates, performance improves on average, especially in three-person teams. This structure clarifies responsibilities and improves the routing of specialized expertise to the actions where it is most valuable.
AI.conclusion: Effective coordination structure is as important as raw capability in human-AI teams. Simply increasing the number of collaborators does not guarantee better outcomes and can even harm performance if roles and responsibilities are unclear. Scaffolding mechanisms—such as shared group memory and explicit human-in-the-loop approval gates—help align responsibility, reduce coordination overhead, and better leverage available expertise, leading to higher performance, particularly in multi-member teams like three-person groups.

title: VISUALSKILL: Multimodal Skills for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) approach human-level performance on standardised benchmarks but still struggle on long-horizon tasks and unseen software. Existing skill libraries address this with reusable skills, but represent the skill artifact as text only, despite the visual nature of GUI interaction. We propose VISUALSKILL: a hierarchical multimodal skill, tailored to each target application and organised as a central index over per-topic files, which the agent consumes through a load_topic MCP tool that fetches the relevant topic's text and figures on demand. We construct each skill with a two-stage pipeline that combines authored documentation with live-application UI exploration. On two CUA benchmarks, CUA-World and OSExpert-Eval, a Claude Code CLI agent backed by Claude Opus 4.6 reaches an average score of 0.456 with VISUALSKILL, a +15.3 point absolute lift over the no-skill baseline (0.303). Against a matched text-only skill that is generated from the same source content and differs from VISUALSKILL only in modality, VISUALSKILL yields a further +8.3 point absolute gain over the matched text-only skill (0.373 vs. 0.456), providing direct evidence that retaining visual figures in the skill artifact, rather than verbalizing them away, helps the agent both identify UI elements and verify workflow state after each action. Our code is available at https://github.com/XMHZZ2018/VisualSkills.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VISUALSKILL, a multimodal, visually grounded skill representation for computer-use agents, showing that preserving UI figures significantly boosts performance on long-horizon GUI tasks.
AI.motivation: Computer-use agents can match humans on standard benchmarks but fail on long, complex tasks and unfamiliar software, partly because existing skill libraries are purely textual and ignore the inherently visual nature of GUI interaction. The authors want to see whether explicitly encoding visual UI information into reusable skills can make agents more robust and effective across applications.
AI.method: They design VISUALSKILL, a hierarchical, multimodal skill format customized per target application. Skills are organized as a central index that points to per-topic files. Each topic file includes both text and visual figures that describe UI elements and workflows. An agent accesses these via a load_topic MCP tool that fetches only the relevant topic’s content on demand. Skills are constructed using a two-stage pipeline: (1) ingest and structure authored documentation; (2) augment it with exploration of the live UI to capture accurate visual context. They deploy a Claude Code CLI agent with Claude Opus 4.6 and compare three conditions on CUA-World and OSExpert-Eval benchmarks: no skills, a matched text-only skill (derived from the same content but with visuals verbalized away), and VISUALSKILL with full multimodal figures.
AI.result: On CUA-World and OSExpert-Eval, the baseline agent without skills scores 0.303 on average. Adding the matched text-only skill raises performance to 0.373. Using VISUALSKILL further improves the score to 0.456, a +15.3 point gain over the no-skill baseline and +8.3 points over the matched text-only skill. These results hold across both benchmarks, indicating that the visual component of VISUALSKILL contributes meaningful additional performance beyond what textual description alone can provide.
AI.conclusion: VISUALSKILL demonstrates that explicitly preserving and structuring visual information about GUIs inside reusable skills substantially improves computer-use agents’ ability to navigate long-horizon tasks and novel software. The gains over both a no-skill baseline and a content-matched text-only skill provide direct evidence that multimodal skill artifacts help agents more reliably identify UI elements and track workflow state. This suggests that future CUA systems and skill libraries should treat visual UI representations as first-class components rather than translating everything into text.

title: CEO-Bench: Can Agents Play the Long Game?
summary: Language model agents are becoming proficient executors at isolated, short-horizon tasks such as software engineering and customer service. Yet real-world challenges require a combination of sophisticated skills that remain largely untested in agents: (1) navigating long horizons amid uncertainty; (2) acquiring information in noisy environments; (3) adapting to a changing world; (4) orchestrating multiple moving parts toward a coherent goal. We introduce CEO-Bench, which evaluates these capabilities together by simulating a representative real-world task: operating a startup for 500 days. An agent manages pricing, marketing, budgeting, and many other aspects of a fictional company through a programmable Python interface, operating in the same environment and facing the same challenges as a human CEO. Success demands analyzing noisy, interconnected business databases, translating signals into sound strategy, and coordinating many decisions with programming. The strongest agents write sophisticated code that simulates customer cohorts to forecast future cash and mines negotiation history to uncover hidden customer preferences. Even so, most state-of-the-art models struggle in this environment. Only Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5 finish above the $1M starting balance, and neither consistently turns a profit. CEO-Bench takes a first step toward measuring the intelligence required to drive sustained, adaptive progress over time.

AI.tldr: CEO-Bench is a benchmark that tests language model agents on running a simulated startup over 500 days, stressing long-horizon, noisy, and adaptive decision-making skills.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations show language model agents can do short, isolated tasks (e.g., coding, customer service), but they do not test the composite skills needed for real-world, long-term, uncertain, and adaptive decision-making, such as that required to run a company. The authors want a benchmark that approximates these demands and measures whether current models can coordinate many interdependent decisions over time.
AI.method: They build CEO-Bench, an interactive simulation where an agent acts as the CEO of a fictional startup over 500 days. The agent uses a programmable Python interface to control pricing, marketing, budgeting, and other business levers. The environment includes noisy, interconnected business databases and changing market conditions. Performance is evaluated via financial outcomes such as ending cash balance and profitability, while also observing the strategies and code the agents write (e.g., customer cohort simulations, mining negotiation histories). Multiple state-of-the-art language model agents are run through this benchmark.
AI.result: The most capable agents learn to write nontrivial analytical code, such as simulating customer cohorts to forecast cash flow and extracting hidden customer preferences from negotiation logs. However, overall performance is weak: most state-of-the-art models fail to manage the startup profitably. Only two systems, Claude Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, end with more than the initial $1M balance, and even these models do not reliably produce sustained profits.
AI.conclusion: CEO-Bench exposes a gap between current language model capabilities and the kind of long-horizon, adaptive, and strategic intelligence required to run complex real-world operations. While some models show promising emergent behaviors in analysis and planning, consistent, profitable performance remains out of reach. The benchmark is proposed as an initial step toward systematically measuring and eventually improving agents’ ability to drive sustained progress in dynamic, uncertain environments.

title: LLM Parameters for Math Across Languages: Shared or Separate?
comment: 5 pages. Accepted at ACL Student Research Workshop (SRW) 2026. Code: https://github.com/luisavictor/math-across-languages Translated Datasets: https://huggingface.co/math-across-languages Webpage: https://math-across-languages.github.io
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit substantial cross-lingual variation in mathematical reasoning performance, but it remains unclear whether these differences reflect language-specific parameters or a shared mechanism that manifests differently by language. We present a cross-lingual mechanistic analysis of mathematical reasoning in LLMs, enabling us to localize and compare model parameters that support mathematical reasoning across languages. We find that the extracted math-associated parameters exhibit partial cross-lingual overlap, with the strongest overlap concentrated in intermediate model layers. We further observe that English consistently produces the largest set of math-relevant parameters, whereas lower-resource languages reveal smaller sets of relevant parameters. These results suggest that math-related behavior in multilingual LLMs is neither fully language-invariant nor fully language-specific, but instead exhibits partial cross-lingual parameter overlap with systematic language-dependent differences.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how mathematical reasoning in multilingual LLMs is represented across languages, finding that math-related parameters partially overlap across languages with systematic differences by language and layer depth.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs show different math reasoning performance in different languages, it is unclear whether this is due to distinct language-specific mechanisms or a shared underlying system expressed differently across languages. Understanding this has implications for model interpretability, fairness, and improving reasoning in low-resource languages.
AI.method: The authors conduct a cross-lingual mechanistic analysis of mathematical reasoning in multilingual LLMs. They identify and extract parameters associated with math reasoning in different languages and compare their overlap across layers and languages, focusing on how these parameters are distributed within the model.
AI.result: They find that parameters supporting math reasoning only partially overlap across languages, with the strongest cross-lingual overlap occurring in intermediate layers. English activations yield the largest set of math-relevant parameters, while lower-resource languages have smaller sets, indicating less robust or sparser math-related representations.
AI.conclusion: Mathematical reasoning in multilingual LLMs is supported by a mix of shared and language-specific parameters: it is neither fully language-invariant nor fully language-specific. Instead, there is partial cross-lingual parameter overlap, structured by layer depth and language resource level, which explains systematic differences in math performance across languages.

title: DeFAb: A Verifiable Benchmark for Defeasible Abduction in Foundation Models
comment: 33 pages, 14 figures, 23 tables. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb ; code and evaluation harness: https://github.com/PatrickAllenCooper/blanc
summary: A rule-based logic solver resolves every instance in our benchmark in under 50 microseconds with 100% accuracy; the best frontier language model reaches 65% at best and drops to 23.5% under rendering-robust evaluation (worst case over four surface renderings). We introduce DeFAb (Defeasible Abduction Benchmark), a dataset and generation pipeline that converts four decades of publicly funded knowledge bases into formally grounded instances for defeasible abduction: constructing hypotheses that explain anomalies by overriding defaults while preserving unrelated expectations. Because every hypothesis must pass polynomial-time checks for valid derivation, conservativity, and minimality, DeFAb makes logical rigor the instrument for measuring creativity and theoretical reasoning, scoring the disciplined construction of theory revisions rather than fluent but theory-destroying prose. The pipeline pairs taxonomic hierarchies (OpenCyc, YAGO, Wikidata) with behavioral property graphs (ConceptNet, UMLS) to produce 372,648+ instances across 33.75M materialized rules from 18 sources, in three levels with polynomial-time verifiable gold standards. Four frontier models do not reliably internalize defeasible reasoning: rendering-robust Level 2 accuracy is 7.8-23.5%; chain-of-thought variance (~36 pp) exceeds any inter-model gap; and a matched contamination control isolates a +19.4 pp Level 3 gap. We further release DeFAb-Hard (a 235-instance Level 3 difficulty variant; best model 53.3% vs 100% symbolic) and CONJURE (a kernel-verified transformative-creativity variant of 560 Lean 4/Mathlib instances whose gold answers are definitions the proof kernel did not previously contain, judge-free verifier; a pilot finds zero novel concepts). The same verifier doubles as an exact reward for preference optimization (DPO, RLVR/GRPO). Released under MIT at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PatrickAllenCooper/DeFAb.

AI.tldr: They build DeFAb, a large, formally grounded benchmark for defeasible abduction and show that simple symbolic logic solvers vastly outperform state-of-the-art language models on it.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test and measure models’ ability for defeasible reasoning and creative yet logically disciplined theory revision, going beyond surface-level language fluency and informal chain-of-thought.
AI.method: They automatically convert four decades of curated knowledge bases into defeasible abduction problems. The pipeline combines taxonomic hierarchies (OpenCyc, YAGO, Wikidata) with behavioral/property graphs (ConceptNet, UMLS), producing hundreds of thousands of instances in multiple difficulty levels. Each instance has gold-standard hypotheses that are checked in polynomial time for valid derivation, conservativity, and minimality. They also construct harder subsets (DeFAb-Hard) and a Lean 4/Mathlib-based variant (CONJURE) whose gold answers are new, kernel-verified definitions, and use the same verifier as an exact reward signal for preference optimization methods like DPO and RL-based approaches.
AI.result: A rule-based logic solver achieves 100% accuracy on the benchmark in under 50 microseconds per instance. In contrast, top frontier language models only reach about 65% at best, dropping to as low as 23.5% under more robust evaluation across different surface renderings. Rendering-robust Level 2 accuracy ranges from 7.8–23.5%, chain-of-thought introduces high variance (~36 percentage points), and contamination-controlled experiments still show a large (19.4 percentage point) performance gap on the hardest level. On DeFAb-Hard, the best model only gets 53.3% vs symbolic 100%; on CONJURE, a pilot detects no genuinely novel concepts generated by models.
AI.conclusion: Current frontier language models do not reliably internalize defeasible reasoning or the disciplined construction of theory revisions, even when they appear fluent or when using chain-of-thought. Symbolic logic systems still dominate on these tasks. DeFAb, DeFAb-Hard, and CONJURE provide scalable, formally verified benchmarks and exact verifiers that can be used both to evaluate and to train models (via precise reward signals), shifting focus from prose fluency toward logically grounded, creative theoretical reasoning.

title: Montreal Forced Aligner and the state of speech-to-text alignment in 2026
summary: The Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) was released in 2016 and has since become the most widely used tool for forced alignment in research and industry. In the decade since, MFA has undergone substantial development, including expanded coverage across more languages and dialects using larger open-source datasets, harmonized IPA dictionaries, model adaptation, cross-language phone remapping, and support utilities. This paper documents MFA 3.0's developments since version 1.0 and evaluates MFA's performance across English, Japanese, and Korean, benchmarked against classic and neural forced aligners. MFA 3.0 achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art performance across all four benchmark datasets with mean boundary errors below 15 ms. Adaptation and cross-language remapping are effective for languages outside MFA's training distribution, and pronunciation probability modeling and phonological rules provide gains in specific conditions.

AI.tldr: MFA 3.0 is a significantly improved, multilingual forced alignment toolkit that achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art alignment accuracy and adds powerful adaptation and cross-language capabilities.
AI.motivation: Forced alignment—precisely time-stamping phonetic or word-level transcriptions to speech—is essential in speech research and industry, but existing tools are limited in language coverage, ease of use, and performance consistency across languages and conditions. The original MFA became widely used, creating a need to document and systematically evaluate its major new developments, extend coverage to more languages and dialects, and test new techniques (e.g., model adaptation, cross-language phone remapping, pronunciation modeling) that could improve alignment, especially for under-resourced or out-of-distribution languages.
AI.method: The authors describe architectural and resource changes from MFA 1.0 to 3.0: expanded multilingual acoustic and pronunciation models built from larger open-source datasets, harmonized IPA-based pronunciation dictionaries, support for model adaptation and cross-language phone remapping, and added support utilities. They then empirically evaluate MFA 3.0 on four benchmark datasets in English, Japanese, and Korean, comparing its boundary error performance to both classic (likely GMM-HMM based) and neural forced aligners. They also perform targeted experiments on adaptation, cross-language remapping, pronunciation probability modeling, and phonological rules to assess their impact in various conditions.
AI.result: Across four benchmark datasets in English, Japanese, and Korean, MFA 3.0 achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art performance, with mean phone/word boundary errors under 15 milliseconds. Experiments show that model adaptation and cross-language phone remapping are effective strategies for aligning languages that were not directly seen during training. Additionally, incorporating pronunciation probability modeling and phonological rules yields measurable improvements in certain scenarios, indicating that linguistic knowledge can further refine alignment quality.
AI.conclusion: MFA 3.0 substantially advances the capabilities of the Montreal Forced Aligner, combining strong, competitive alignment accuracy with broader multilingual support and practical mechanisms for handling under-resourced and out-of-distribution languages. Its adaptation, cross-language remapping, and linguistically-informed pronunciation modeling make it a robust, general-purpose forced alignment toolkit for both research and industry applications.

title: Optimizing Lithium Production Decisions under Geological, Demand, and Pricing Uncertainties: A POMDP Framework for Multi-Objective Decision Making
summary: Decision making in lithium production is challenging, whether from an investor's perspective or a strategic production standpoint. Determining which mines to open and when to open them involves not only geological and price uncertainties, but also complexities around the choice of extraction method, from direct lithium extraction to hard rock mining. Prior work explored models of this problem and different methods to optimize mining decisions; these models did not account for uncertainty in pricing, uncertainty in demand, or different mining technologies to extract lithium. Incorporating different pricing models and extraction technology into these models enables more robust strategies for determining not only when and where to open a mine, but also which method of production to pursue. We frame the problem as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) and solve using belief state planning methods to get optimal decision making. In our study, we show that POMDP solvers outperform human inspired heuristics by dynamically adapting to shifting lithium price regimes (static, linear, exponential, and stochastic) through belief state planning and explicit uncertainty management. By optimally sequencing exploration, production, and technology choice, the framework achieves higher demand fulfillment and more balanced economic environmental outcomes over the projects lifetime in all different pricing and deposit scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper models lithium mine development as a POMDP to optimize when/where to open mines and which extraction technology to use under uncertain prices and demand, showing this approach beats heuristic strategies.
AI.motivation: Lithium is critical for energy storage, and producers/investors face difficult decisions about which deposits to develop, when to develop them, and which extraction technologies to adopt. Existing decision models ignore key uncertainties like future lithium prices, demand, and differing mining technologies, limiting their usefulness for robust, long‑horizon planning. A more realistic framework is needed to explicitly handle uncertainty and support better strategic choices that balance economic and environmental objectives.
AI.method: The authors formulate lithium mine planning as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), where the true state of the world (e.g., deposit characteristics, price regime) is imperfectly known. They use belief‑state planning methods to maintain and update probability distributions over hidden states, and compute policies that choose among exploration, production, and technology options (such as direct lithium extraction versus hard‑rock mining) over time. They test different lithium price dynamics (static, linear trend, exponential growth, and stochastic regimes) and compare solver‑derived policies with human‑inspired heuristic rules.
AI.result: Across a variety of price dynamics and deposit scenarios, policies derived from POMDP solvers outperform the human‑inspired heuristics. The POMDP approach better adapts to changing and uncertain price regimes and more effectively sequences exploration, production, and technology adoption decisions. This leads to higher demand fulfillment and improved balance between economic returns and environmental impacts over the project lifetime in all tested scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Modeling lithium mine development as a POMDP and solving it via belief‑state planning yields superior decision policies compared to heuristic approaches. Explicitly representing uncertainty in prices, demand, and technology options enables more flexible, adaptive strategies for when and where to open mines and which extraction methods to use. The framework supports improved long‑term outcomes—both economic and environmental—and provides a more robust basis for strategic planning in lithium supply development under uncertainty.

title: Possible or Definite? A Benchmark for Evaluating Diagnostic Uncertainty Preservation in Clinical Text
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for clinical text tasks such as summarization and revision. While most studies evaluate the fluency and coherence of LLM-generated text, whether LLMs correctly preserve diagnostic uncertainty remains underexplored. In clinical practice, phrases such as ``possible pneumonia'' communicate the strength of available evidence and directly guide decisions about follow-up testing and treatment. Altering these uncertainty expressions can change the clinical meaning entirely. In this paper, we systematically evaluated this problem in two steps. First, we constructed a benchmark of 1,200 clinical documents with 9,184 uncertainty annotations across five levels. Second, we evaluated three LLMs on this benchmark. Our results show that (1) LLMs preserve the original uncertainty cues poorly, often less than half the time; (2) LLMs struggle with nuanced distinctions between adjacent levels. This work reveals a failure mode not captured by standard evaluation metrics and provides implications for the safe deployment of LLMs in clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models often fail to preserve diagnostic uncertainty cues in clinical text, exposing a safety risk not reflected in standard metrics.
AI.motivation: In clinical documentation, expressions like “possible pneumonia” encode graded diagnostic uncertainty that directly impacts downstream decisions about tests and treatment. LLM-based summarization and rewriting tools are being introduced into clinical workflows, but existing evaluations mostly focus on fluency and coherence, not on whether models accurately preserve these nuanced uncertainty expressions. A systematic analysis is needed to understand whether LLMs distort or erase uncertainty, which could lead to inappropriate clinical actions and patient harm.
AI.method: The authors: (1) created a benchmark of 1,200 clinical documents, manually annotating 9,184 instances of diagnostic uncertainty across five ordered levels (e.g., from very tentative to highly confident formulations); and (2) used this benchmark to test three different large language models on clinical text tasks such as summarization or revision. They then measured how well the models preserved the original level and type of uncertainty in their outputs, with special attention to errors between adjacent uncertainty levels.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation showed that LLMs often failed to retain the original uncertainty cues, preserving them correctly in less than about half of instances. The models also had particular difficulty distinguishing and maintaining subtle differences between adjacent uncertainty levels, frequently upgrading or downgrading diagnostic certainty in their generated text.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, despite producing fluent and coherent clinical text, exhibit a critical failure mode: they do not reliably preserve diagnostic uncertainty expressed by clinicians. This limitation is not revealed by standard NLP metrics, which focus on surface quality rather than epistemic fidelity. The findings suggest that additional evaluation criteria and safeguards are necessary before integrating LLM-based tools into safety-critical clinical documentation workflows.

title: ForecastBench-Sim: A Simulated-World Forecasting Benchmark
comment: 15 pages, 5 main figures, 6 appendix figures. Spotlight presentation at Forecasting as a New Frontier of Intelligence / Workshop on AI Forecasting, ICML 2026
summary: Forecasting benchmarks for general-purpose AI systems usually inherit the constraints of the real world: outcomes resolve slowly, tail events are rare, and counterfactual questions are difficult to score. We introduce ForecastBench-Sim, a simulated-world forecasting benchmark built on game rollouts from Freeciv, a turn-based strategy game modelled on the Civilization series. Forecasters receive a fixed world report (a structured snapshot of the current game state) and answer questions about hidden future states; the benchmark then continues the simulation and scores forecasts. Because the world is simulated, the same setup can generate continuous or binary forecasting questions at arbitrary time horizons, paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal questions, and resolved examples of rare or disruptive outcomes. We describe the benchmark pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, and release artifacts, and report validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot. ForecastBench-Sim is intended to complement real-world forecasting benchmarks by providing controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ForecastBench-Sim, a forecasting benchmark based on simulated game worlds from Freeciv to evaluate probabilistic reasoning of AI systems and humans under dynamic conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing forecasting benchmarks are constrained by real-world limitations: outcomes can take a long time to resolve, rare tail events are scarce and hard to evaluate, and counterfactual or causal questions are difficult to score because we cannot observe alternate histories. The authors want a way to systematically and rapidly test forecasting and probabilistic reasoning, including edge cases and interventions, in a controlled yet complex environment.
AI.method: They build a benchmark on top of Freeciv, a turn-based strategy game, by taking structured snapshots of the in-game world (world reports) and posing forecasting questions about hidden future states. They then continue the game simulation to obtain ground-truth outcomes. The setup supports generating various types of questions—continuous or binary outcomes at arbitrary time horizons, paired intervention/counterfactual scenarios, and rare or disruptive events. They define a full pipeline: how scenarios are generated, what question families are used, how scoring is done, and what artifacts are released. They also run validation evaluations on AI models and an anonymized human pilot group.
AI.result: The benchmark can automatically generate a wide range of forecasting questions from simulated game rollouts, and these can be immediately resolved by advancing the simulation. The authors present validation slices showing that the benchmark is usable for evaluating models and humans, although details of performance are not given in the abstract. They successfully create a functioning pipeline, question taxonomy, and scoring protocol, along with initial empirical evidence that the benchmark behaves sensibly.
AI.conclusion: ForecastBench-Sim offers a complementary alternative to real-world forecasting benchmarks by leveraging a simulated world that allows immediate resolution, controllable difficulty, and systematic creation of counterfactual and rare-event scenarios. This makes it a practical tool for studying and comparing probabilistic reasoning and forecasting abilities of general-purpose AI systems and humans in dynamic environments.

title: PreUnlearn: Auditing Collateral Knowledge Damage Before Large Language Model Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning for large language models (LLMs) aims to remove specified knowledge while preserving the rest of the model's capabilities. However, the boundary between knowledge to forget and knowledge to retain is often unclear, since related and even distant information may be entangled in the model. In this paper, we study LLM unlearning from a data-centric perspective and measure how unlearning effects propagate from the forget set to same-domain and distant-domain knowledge. We find a consistent decay pattern: collateral damage is strongest near the forget set, weakens with semantic distance, but does not disappear at domain boundaries. We further ask whether such damage can be audited before unlearning is executed. We formulate forget-set auditing as a pre-unlearning prediction task and analyze which data features are most predictive of downstream damage. Our results show that interaction features between the forget set and evaluation set provide the strongest signals, suggesting that collateral damage is partly reflected in data geometry before model updates occur. These findings position forget-set auditing as an early warning tool for identifying risky unlearning runs and designing more reliable unlearning procedures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how forgetting specific data in LLMs unintentionally affects other knowledge, and proposes a way to predict such collateral damage before unlearning is performed.
AI.motivation: Machine unlearning tries to remove certain knowledge from LLMs (e.g., for privacy or legal reasons) while preserving the rest. In practice, models are highly entangled, so removing one set of knowledge can unintentionally damage related or even distant capabilities. There is limited understanding of how this collateral damage spreads across domains and whether it can be anticipated in advance. The authors aim to characterize this propagation behavior and develop tools to predict risky unlearning operations ahead of time.
AI.method: They adopt a data-centric view: rather than only analyzing model parameters, they examine how unlearning a specific "forget set" of data affects performance on other datasets spanning the same domain and more distant domains. They empirically measure performance decay as a function of semantic distance from the forget set. Then they frame "forget-set auditing" as a pre-unlearning prediction problem: using features of the data (especially interaction features between the forget set and evaluation sets), they learn or analyze which features best predict the degree of collateral damage that would result from unlearning.
AI.result: Experiments reveal a consistent decay pattern of collateral damage: it is strongest on data most similar to the forget set, diminishes with increasing semantic distance, but remains non-zero across domain boundaries. In the auditing study, they find that interaction features between the forget set and an evaluation set (capturing how the two sets relate in data/representation space) are the most informative predictors of downstream performance loss after unlearning. This indicates that the structure of the data itself encodes signals about what will be harmed by unlearning, even before any model updates are applied.
AI.conclusion: Collateral damage from LLM unlearning is structured and extends beyond the immediate target domain, decreasing with semantic distance but not vanishing entirely. Because this damage correlates strongly with pre-existing geometric relationships between the forget set and other data, it can be partially anticipated through "forget-set auditing" prior to unlearning. This positions auditing as a practical early warning mechanism to identify risky unlearning runs and guide the design of safer, more reliable unlearning procedures.

title: What Must Generalist Agents Remember?
summary: This paper develops a formal account of what generalist agents must store in memory in order to act near-optimally across multiple environments and goals. It shows that when two domains share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions, any uniformly near-optimal policy must induce distinct memory distributions at that bottleneck. The result yields a separation theorem: sufficiently successful agents cannot rely only on current state observations, but must preserve domain-relevant information in memory. The paper further shows that if an agent's memory contains enough information to estimate values for related goals, then that memory can be used to approximately reconstruct the agent's local transition dynamics. Together, these results characterize memory as the substrate that supports domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning for generalist agents.

AI.tldr: The paper studies what information a generalist agent must store in memory to act near-optimally across multiple tasks and environments, proving that successful agents need distinct memory states for different domains and can use memory to reconstruct local dynamics.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental role and necessary content of memory in generalist agents that must perform well across many environments and goals, and to provide a formal characterization of why simple reactive (memoryless) policies are insufficient.
AI.method: The authors use a formal, theoretical framework over multiple domains that may share observations but differ in optimal actions. They analyze policies, memory states, and observational bottlenecks to prove that near-optimality across such domains requires distinguishable memory distributions. They then show, via information-theoretic and value-estimation arguments, that sufficiently informative memory can be used to approximate local transition dynamics for related goals.
AI.result: They prove a separation theorem: any uniformly near-optimal agent facing domains that share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions must have memory states that differ across those domains at that bottleneck. They also prove that when memory stores enough information to support value estimation for related goals, it implicitly encodes enough structure to approximately reconstruct the agent’s local transition dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Memory is not just a convenience but a necessary substrate for generalist agents: it enables domain disambiguation when observations are ambiguous, supports approximate reconstruction of transition models, and thereby underpins planning across multiple goals and environments. Reactive agents that only use current observations are provably insufficient in the settings studied.

title: Towards Scalable Customization and Deployment of Multi-Agent Systems for Enterprise Applications
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems demonstrate strong performance on complex reasoning and task execution, enabling broad enterprise applications. However, production deployment remains challenging due to domain-specific customization requirements and high latency and inference costs in agentic workflows. We propose a unified framework for customization and efficient deployment of multi-agent systems in real-world settings. The first stage, Agentic Model Customization, combines continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and preference optimization to adapt a compact model to specialized domains while retaining strong agentic capabilities. The second stage, Inference Optimization, integrates speculative decoding and FP8 quantization with targeted calibration to enable cost-efficient serving with minimal quality loss. Across enterprise workloads, our framework enables rapid domain adaptation and achieves a 4.48x speedup in throughput while maintaining performance and improving robustness on long-tail scenarios.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework to customize compact LLM-based multi-agent systems for specific domains and deploy them efficiently using inference optimizations, achieving large throughput gains with minimal quality loss.
AI.motivation: While LLM-based multi-agent systems are powerful for complex reasoning and enterprise tasks, they are hard to deploy in production because each domain needs customization and current agentic workflows are slow and expensive to run. There is a need for a unified, practical approach that both adapts models to specialized domains and makes inference efficient enough for real-world use.
AI.method: The paper proposes a two-stage framework. Stage 1, Agentic Model Customization, uses a pipeline of continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and preference optimization to specialize a compact base model to a given domain while preserving multi-agent reasoning and coordination abilities. Stage 2, Inference Optimization, applies speculative decoding and FP8 quantization, together with targeted calibration procedures, to speed up inference and reduce cost without significantly degrading quality.
AI.result: On a set of enterprise workloads, the proposed framework allows models to be rapidly adapted to new domains and provides a 4.48x increase in serving throughput compared to a baseline, while keeping overall performance comparable and improving robustness on rare and long-tail cases.
AI.conclusion: A unified, two-stage approach that couples agentic model customization with advanced inference optimizations can make compact LLM-based multi-agent systems both domain-specialized and cost-efficient for production use, yielding substantial speedups without sacrificing performance and even improving robustness in challenging scenarios.

title: R2D-RL: A RoboCup 2D Soccer Environment for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robot soccer is a challenging testbed for multi-agent reinforcement learning because it combines partial observability, cooperative and adversarial interaction, sparse rewards, and long-horizon tactical behavior. RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation (RCSS2D) provides a mature robot-soccer platform, but its competition-oriented server-client architecture is difficult to use directly with modern Python-based MARL workflows. We introduce R2D-RL, a reinforcement learning environment that connects RCSS2D and HELIOS-based player clients to a Python MARL interface through shared-memory communication and cycle-level synchronization. R2D-RL supports full-field and scenario-based training with configurable opponents, Base discrete and Hybrid parameterized action spaces, action masks, expected possession value (EPV)-based reward shaping, and parallel execution. We provide front-goal scenarios and an 11-vs-11 full-field benchmark, together with baseline results.

AI.tldr: R2D-RL is a Python-friendly multi-agent reinforcement learning environment built on RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation, enabling modern MARL research with soccer-specific features and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing robot soccer platforms like RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation are powerful but difficult to integrate with modern Python-based MARL workflows due to their competition-oriented server-client architecture.
