AI.method: The authors build R2D-RL, which links RCSS2D and HELIOS-based player clients to a Python MARL interface via shared-memory communication and precise cycle-level synchronization. They implement support for full-field and scenario-based training, configurable opponents, discrete and hybrid action spaces, action masks, EPV-based reward shaping, and parallel execution.
AI.result: R2D-RL provides a working environment with front-goal training scenarios and an 11-vs-11 full-field benchmark, along with baseline MARL results on these tasks.
AI.conclusion: R2D-RL makes RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation accessible to modern MARL research pipelines and offers standardized tasks and baselines for evaluating multi-agent reinforcement learning methods in complex robot-soccer settings.

title: MCompassRAG: Topic Metadata as a Semantic Compass for Paragraph-Level Retrieval
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems depend critically on how documents are chunked and searched. Fine-grained chunks can improve retrieval precision but expand the search space, increasing latency and cost; larger chunks reduce the number of candidates but make dense similarity less reliable, as the representation for each chunk mixes multiple topics and introduces more semantic noise. This trade-off becomes especially limiting in deep research tasks, where retrieval must be both fast and precise across large, heterogeneous corpora. We introduce MCompassRAG, a metadata-guided retrieval framework that uses topic-level signals as a semantic compass for selecting relevant evidence. Instead of relying only on cosine similarity between queries and noisy chunk embeddings, MCompassRAG enriches chunk representations with topic metadata in the same embedding space and trains a lightweight retriever through LLM-teacher distillation. At inference time, MCompassRAG performs topic-aware retrieval without additional LLM calls, improving both efficiency and evidence quality. Across six complex retrieval benchmarks, MCompassRAG improves information efficiency (IE) by 8.24% on average with over 5 times lower latency than the strongest efficient RAG baselines. Code is available on https://github.com/AmirAbaskohi/MCompassRAG.

AI.tldr: MCompassRAG is a metadata-guided RAG framework that uses topic-level signals in the embedding space and an LLM-distilled retriever to achieve more precise and efficient retrieval over large corpora.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG systems face a trade-off between using small chunks (better precision, higher cost and latency) and large chunks (worse semantic fidelity but cheaper). This becomes a bottleneck for deep research over large, heterogeneous document collections, where retrieval must be both accurate and fast.
AI.method: MCompassRAG augments chunk embeddings with topic metadata embedded in the same space, and trains a lightweight retriever via LLM-teacher distillation so that retrieval can be guided by topic-level signals rather than raw cosine similarity on noisy chunk vectors. At inference, it performs topic-aware retrieval without extra LLM calls.
AI.result: On six complex retrieval benchmarks, MCompassRAG increases information efficiency by an average of 8.24% while achieving more than 5x lower latency compared with the strongest efficient RAG baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating topic metadata into the embedding space and using a distilled lightweight retriever enables RAG systems to retrieve higher-quality evidence more efficiently, alleviating the chunk-size trade-off in complex retrieval tasks.

title: ProfiLLM: Utility-Aligned Agentic User Profiling for Industrial Ride-Hailing Dispatch
summary: Bringing Large Language Models (LLMs) into industrial ride-hailing dispatch as semantic feature extractors over platform-scale behavioral logs is a compelling but under-explored data systems problem. Production matching pipelines remain dominated by structured numerical features, yet decisive behavioral signals (e.g., a driver's habitual aversion to certain regions) are inherently contextual and naturally expressible as LLM-generated user profiles. However, scaling such profiling to a live, millisecond-latency dispatcher faces three intertwined constraints rarely addressed together: on a platform with millions of daily orders, logs exceed any LLM's context window by orders of magnitude; most users are long-tail, with too few interactions for per-user profiling; and surface-fluent profiles do not necessarily improve downstream prediction utility. We present ProfiLLM, an agentic LLM data pipeline that operationalizes utility-aligned user profiling for production matching systems through two modules. (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining equips an LLM agent with 27 analytical tools to mine platform-scale data, producing reusable global knowledge, adaptive user clustering rules, and region-level supply-demand priors. (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration generates multiple candidate profiles per cluster, evaluates them via a lightweight downstream utility proxy, iteratively refines the best candidates and constructs preference pairs for DPO fine-tuning. Deployed on DiDi's production dispatcher, ProfiLLM achieves up to +6.14% relative AUC improvement in outcome prediction, up to +4.35% GMV gain in dispatching simulation, and consistent improvements in a 14-day online A/B test including +0.47% GMV, +0.33% Completion Rate, and -0.82% Cancel-Before-Accept rate.

AI.tldr: ProfiLLM is an agentic LLM-based profiling pipeline that turns large-scale ride-hailing behavioral logs into utility-aligned semantic user profiles, improving dispatch prediction and business metrics in production.
AI.motivation: Industrial ride-hailing dispatch systems mainly use structured numerical features, but many decisive behavioral signals—like drivers’ regional preferences—are contextual and better captured semantically. Directly using LLMs for user profiling at platform scale is hard because logs are much larger than any LLM context window, most users have very sparse data, and human-plausible profiles may not help downstream prediction. There is a need for a scalable, low-latency, production-ready way to extract and use LLM-based behavioral profiles that are explicitly aligned with utility in matching tasks.
AI.method: The paper proposes ProfiLLM, an agentic LLM data pipeline with two main modules. (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining: an LLM agent is equipped with 27 analytical tools to explore platform-scale data offline, from which it derives reusable global knowledge such as adaptive user clustering rules and region-level supply-demand priors. This shifts from per-user long histories to cluster- and region-level abstractions. (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration: for each user cluster, the system generates multiple candidate semantic profiles, scores them using a lightweight proxy for downstream prediction utility, and iteratively refines them. The best profiles are then used to construct preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) fine-tuning, aligning the LLM’s profiling behavior with actual dispatch utility.
AI.result: When integrated into DiDi’s production dispatch system, ProfiLLM delivers substantial improvements. In offline prediction, it yields up to +6.14% relative AUC improvement for outcome prediction models. In simulation-based dispatch experiments, it produces up to +4.35% gain in GMV (Gross Merchandise Value). In a 14-day online A/B test, it consistently improves key business metrics: +0.47% GMV, +0.33% order completion rate, and -0.82% cancel-before-accept rate, demonstrating real-world value at scale.
AI.conclusion: ProfiLLM shows that carefully designed, agentic LLM pipelines can extract utility-aligned semantic user profiles from massive behavioral logs and successfully integrate them into latency-critical industrial dispatch systems. By combining tool-augmented global knowledge mining with utility-driven profile exploration and DPO fine-tuning, the approach overcomes context window limits, data sparsity for long-tail users, and the gap between human-readable profiles and predictive utility. The positive offline, simulation, and online A/B results suggest that LLM-based profiling can be a practical and impactful component in large-scale recommender and matching systems beyond ride-hailing.

title: Speech-Driven End-to-End Language Discrimination towards Chinese Dialects
summary: Language discrimination among similar languages, varieties, and dialects is a challenging natural language processing task. The traditional text-driven focus leads to poor results. In this paper, we explore the effectiveness of speech-driven features towards language discrimination among Chinese dialects. First, we systematically explore the appropriateness of speech-driven MFCC features towards CNN-based language discrimination. Then, we design an end-to-end speech recognition model based on HMM-DNN to predict Chinese dialect words. We adopt attention to extract the discriminative words related to different Chinese dialects. Finally, through a CNN, we combine the word-level embedding and the MFCC-based features. Evaluation of two benchmark Chinese dialect corpora shows the appropriateness and effectiveness of the proposed speech-driven approach to fine-grained Chinese dialect discrimination compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a speech-driven, end-to-end approach that combines MFCC acoustic features and attention-based dialect word embeddings via CNNs to improve fine-grained discrimination among similar Chinese dialects, outperforming prior text-driven methods on two benchmark corpora.
AI.motivation: Distinguishing between similar languages, varieties, and dialects is difficult for NLP systems, especially when relying only on text features, which leads to poor performance. Chinese dialects present a particularly challenging fine-grained discrimination problem, motivating the use of richer speech-based information beyond traditional text-driven approaches.
AI.method: 1) Systematically investigate the use of MFCC acoustic features as input to a CNN for dialect discrimination. 2) Build an end-to-end speech recognition pipeline using an HMM-DNN model to recognize dialectal words from speech. 3) Apply an attention mechanism over the recognized words to highlight those most discriminative for different dialects. 4) Fuse the word-level embeddings produced by this attention mechanism with MFCC-based acoustic features through a CNN architecture to perform the final dialect classification.
AI.result: On two standard benchmark corpora for Chinese dialect identification, the proposed combined speech-driven system (MFCC + attention-based word embeddings via CNN) achieves better performance than existing state-of-the-art methods, empirically validating the usefulness of the approach for fine-grained dialect discrimination.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating speech-driven features, specifically MFCCs and attention-weighted dialectal word embeddings within a CNN-based framework, is effective and appropriate for fine-grained Chinese dialect discrimination. This multimodal, end-to-end approach overcomes some limitations of purely text-driven methods and improves language discrimination among closely related dialects.

title: WorldLines: Benchmarking and Modeling Long-Horizon Stateful Embodied Agents
summary: To assist humans over extended periods in real homes, embodied agents must remember user routines, world states, and past interactions. Existing long-term memory benchmarks mainly evaluate language-centric retrieval and question answering, while embodied benchmarks often focus on short-horizon task execution without testing long-term memory use in dynamic environments. We introduce WorldLines, a project-driven benchmark for long-horizon embodied household assistance. It constructs temporally extended household traces with dialogues, actions, execution feedback, object and device state changes, and converts them into evidence-linked samples for Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning. We further propose ObsMem, an observer-grounded memory framework that maintains visibility-aware memories and action-native state trails for state-aware decisions. Experiments reveal persistent challenges in partial observability, overwritten world states, and translating long-term memory into embodied plans, while ObsMem offers a stronger reference architecture for this setting.

AI.tldr: WorldLines is a benchmark and ObsMem is a memory framework for long-horizon embodied household assistants that must use long-term memory in dynamic homes.
AI.motivation: Most benchmarks either test language-only long-term memory (retrieval/QA) or short-horizon embodied tasks, so they fail to evaluate agents that must remember and use long-term information while acting in changing real-world home environments.
AI.method: They build WorldLines, a benchmark of long, temporally extended household interaction traces including dialogue, actions, feedback, and changing object/device states, and turn these logs into evidence-linked samples for two tasks: Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning. They also introduce ObsMem, a memory architecture that stores visibility-aware memories and maintains action-native state trajectories so agents can reason about what was seen, when, and how states evolved.
AI.result: On WorldLines, current systems struggle with partial observability, handling overwritten or changed world states, and using long-term memory effectively to produce embodied plans, while the proposed ObsMem framework performs better than prior approaches and serves as a stronger baseline.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon embodied household assistance requires benchmarks and memory architectures tailored to dynamic, partially observable environments. WorldLines exposes key failure modes of existing systems, and ObsMem significantly improves performance but also shows that robust long-term memory use for embodied agents remains an open challenge.

title: Dual Dimensionality for Local and Global Attention
summary: Decoder-only Transformers compute attention over the KV cache of preceding tokens. Keys (and Values) are typically represented with the same dimensionality, regardless of its distance from the prediction target. In natural language, however, the next word is most strongly influenced by the immediately preceding tokens. We hypothesize that local and distant tokens impose asymmetric demands on representational capacity: local tokens are more critical for predicting immediate outputs and thus require richer representations, whereas distant tokens primarily serve as long-range memory, for which lower-dimensional representations may suffice. We formalize this idea as Distance-Adaptive Representation (DAR), implemented in a controlled setting that preserves full-dimensional representations within a local context window while assigning reduced-dimensional representations (e.g. 1/4 of the original dimensionality) to tokens beyond that window. Across multiple pretraining scales (70M to 410M parameters), as well as continued supervised fine-tuning on a 1B-scale model, this approach closely matches the performance of full-dimensional baselines. In contrast, uniformly reducing dimensionality across all token positions leads to worse performance. These results challenge the common assumption that key and value dimensionality should be uniform across token positions. Our findings suggest a new direction for designing attention architectures that adaptively allocate representational capacity across sequences, enabling further reductions in KV cache during inference.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Distance-Adaptive Representation (DAR), where keys/values for nearby tokens keep full dimensionality while distant tokens use reduced dimensions, significantly shrinking KV cache with negligible performance loss.
AI.motivation: In decoder-only Transformers, all past tokens share the same key/value dimensionality in the KV cache, even though recent tokens are usually most important for predicting the next token. This uniform design may waste memory and computation on distant tokens that mainly act as long-range memory. The authors aim to test whether representational capacity can be allocated non-uniformly across sequence positions to reduce KV cache size without hurting performance.
AI.method: They introduce Distance-Adaptive Representation (DAR), which defines a local context window around the prediction target where keys and values retain full dimensionality. For tokens beyond this window, keys and values are projected into a lower-dimensional space (e.g., 1/4 of the original size). The attention mechanism is implemented so that local tokens interact at full dimension, while distant tokens are handled via the reduced-dimensional representations, maintaining compatibility inside the same model. They systematically evaluate DAR across multiple model sizes (70M–410M parameters) during pretraining, and on a 1B-parameter model during continued supervised fine-tuning, and compare it to baselines that uniformly reduce dimensionality for all tokens.
AI.result: Across all tested scales, DAR models with reduced-dimensional representations for distant tokens achieve performance comparable to full-dimensional baselines, while significantly lowering KV cache requirements. In contrast, baselines that uniformly shrink key/value dimensionality across all positions show clear performance degradation, confirming the benefit of distance-adaptive capacity allocation.
AI.conclusion: Uniform key/value dimensionality across all token positions is not necessary for strong performance in decoder-only Transformers. Allocating higher-dimensional representations to nearby tokens and lower-dimensional ones to distant tokens preserves model quality while reducing KV cache size. This challenges standard attention design and points toward adaptive representation schemes as a promising way to build more memory-efficient Transformer architectures for long-context inference.

title: Externalizing Research Synthesis and Validation in AI Scientists through a Research Harness
summary: AI systems can increasingly automate scientific workflows, but the reasoning that links prior evidence, generated ideas, experiments and final claims often remains implicit inside model inference. Here we introduce Xcientist, a research harness that externalizes research synthesis and experimental validation into inspectable, contract-governed processes. Xcientist organizes literature evidence, idea states, implementation plans, ablation records and repair traces as persistent research artifacts, so that generated mechanisms can be grounded, executed, tested and revised without losing their evidential basis. We identify claim drift as a failure mode of automated research, where runnable artifacts no longer support the mechanism originally claimed. Across training-free memory systems, graph-structured traffic forecasting and multi-scale physics-informed neural networks, Xcientist preserves traceable trajectories from problem formulation to mechanism design, validation and bounded revision. These results suggest that AI scientists should be evaluated not only by their final artifacts, but by whether their synthesis and validation processes remain attributable, inspectable and scientifically accountable.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Xcientist, a framework that makes AI-driven scientific research processes explicit, traceable, and accountable, focusing on preventing claim drift between hypotheses and runnable artifacts.
AI.motivation: As AI systems automate more of the scientific workflow, their internal reasoning and links between evidence, ideas, experiments, and claims are often opaque, making it hard to verify or audit the science they produce. There is a need for AI research systems whose processes and evidential grounding are inspectable and scientifically accountable, not just judged by final results.
AI.method: They design Xcientist, a research harness that structures AI-driven research as explicit, contract-governed processes and persistent artifacts. It organizes literature evidence, evolving idea states, implementation plans, ablation and experiment logs, and repair traces into a coherent, inspectable record. They analyze and address a failure mode they call claim drift, and demonstrate Xcientist across several domains: training-free memory systems, graph-structured traffic forecasting, and multi-scale physics-informed neural networks.
AI.result: Using Xcientist, the authors are able to maintain a traceable trajectory from initial problem statements through mechanism design, experimental validation, and bounded revisions across multiple application domains. The framework prevents or reveals claim drift by ensuring that runnable artifacts continue to support the original claimed mechanisms and by keeping all intermediate research steps linked and auditable.
AI.conclusion: Xcientist shows that AI scientists should be evaluated not only on their final models or artifacts, but on the transparency, traceability, and accountability of their synthesis and validation processes. Making research reasoning explicit and governed by contracts helps preserve the evidential basis of claims and mitigates failures such as claim drift in automated research workflows.

title: Low-resource Language Discrimination Towards Chinese Dialects with Transfer learning and Data Augmentation
summary: Chinese dialects discrimination is a challenging natural language processing task due to scarce annotation resource. In this article, we develop a novel Chinese dialects discrimination framework with transfer learning and data augmentation (CDDTLDA) in order to overcome the shortage of resources. To be more specific, we first use a relatively larger Chinese dialects corpus to train a source-side automatic speech recognition (ASR) model. Then, we adopt a simple but effective data augmentation method (i.e., speed, pitch, and noise disturbance) to augment the target-side low-resource Chinese dialects, and fine-tune another target ASR model based on the previous source-side ASR model. Meanwhile, the potential common semantic features between source-side and target-side ASR models can be captured by using self-attention mechanism. Finally, we extract the hidden semantic representation in the target ASR model to conduct Chinese dialects discrimination. Our extensive experimental results demonstrate that our model significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods on two benchmark Chinese dialects corpora.

AI.tldr: They propose a transfer learning and data augmentation based framework to improve automatic discrimination of Chinese dialects from speech, achieving better performance than previous methods on two benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Chinese dialect discrimination from speech is difficult because there are limited labeled resources for many dialects. Existing models struggle to learn robust features in low-resource settings, so there is a need for approaches that can leverage larger related datasets and augment scarce data.
AI.method: 1) Train a source-side ASR model on a relatively large Chinese dialect corpus. 2) Apply simple data augmentation (speed, pitch, and noise perturbation) to expand the low-resource target dialect data. 3) Fine-tune a target-side ASR model starting from the source model using the augmented target data. 4) Use a self-attention mechanism to capture common semantic features between source and target ASR models. 5) Extract hidden semantic representations from the target ASR model and feed them to a classifier for dialect discrimination.
AI.result: Experiments on two benchmark Chinese dialect corpora show that the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art dialect discrimination approaches, indicating better use of limited target dialect resources.
AI.conclusion: Transfer learning from a larger dialect corpus, combined with simple acoustic data augmentation and self-attention-based feature sharing between source and target ASR models, yields more discriminative semantic representations for Chinese dialect identification and clearly improves performance in low-resource scenarios.

title: Generative-Model Predictive Planning for Navigation in Partially Observable Environments
summary: Navigation in partially observable environments presents a significant challenge for autonomous agents, requiring effective decision-making with limited sensory information in unknown environments. Belief-based methods, particularly those using neural networks to approximate the belief space, often fail to capture the inherent multimodality of belief spaces, especially in high-dimensional cases with perceptual aliasing. While generative models present a compelling alternative, they typically require substantial data or expert demonstrations and lack explicit mechanisms for long-term planning. In this paper, we introduce BeliefDiffusion, a novel framework that combines the benefits of both generation and planning. BeliefDiffusion leverages diffusion models to explicitly characterize multimodal belief distributions and utilizes Model Predictive Control (MPC) to simultaneously plan ahead. It consists of two steps: (1) Imagining plausible environment configurations based on observation history and (2) Planning efficient navigation strategies across an aggregated configurations. Through extensive experiments in synthetic map environments, we demonstrate that BeliefDiffusion significantly outperforms both model-free reinforcement learning baselines and other generative approaches in navigation success rate and path efficiency. Our results validate that explicitly incorporating multimodal belief representations into planning enables more robust navigation in partially observable settings.

AI.tldr: BeliefDiffusion is a navigation framework for partially observable environments that uses diffusion models to represent multimodal belief over environments and MPC to plan efficient paths, outperforming RL and other generative baselines.
AI.motivation: Classical belief-based and neural methods for POMDP navigation struggle in high-dimensional, partially observable environments with perceptual aliasing because they cannot represent inherently multimodal belief distributions well. Generative models can imagine plausible worlds but typically need lots of data or demonstrations and do not directly support long-horizon planning. The paper aims to build a method that both captures rich multimodal beliefs and uses them directly for robust, long-term navigation planning.
AI.method: The authors propose BeliefDiffusion, which integrates diffusion generative models with Model Predictive Control. First, a diffusion model is trained to generate plausible environment configurations conditioned on the agent’s observation history, thereby explicitly modeling a multimodal belief distribution over latent maps. Then, using MPC, the agent plans navigation actions by rolling out and optimizing trajectories over an aggregated set of sampled environment configurations from the diffusion model, updating as new observations arrive. The procedure alternates between (1) belief generation via diffusion and (2) replanning via MPC in a model-predictive loop.
AI.result: In synthetic partially observable map navigation tasks, BeliefDiffusion achieves higher navigation success rates and more efficient (shorter, less redundant) paths than model-free RL baselines and other generative approaches. Experiments show that explicitly modeling multimodal beliefs yields more robust behavior under perceptual aliasing and uncertainty, especially in challenging high-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicit multimodal belief modeling with diffusion-based generative models, when combined with MPC planning, leads to more robust and efficient navigation in partially observable environments than existing belief-based and generative baselines. The study supports the idea that generation and planning should be tightly integrated: imagined environment configurations (belief samples) must be exploited directly in a planning loop to handle uncertainty and aliasing effectively.

title: Steerable Cultural Preference Optimization of Reward Models
summary: It is essential for large language model (LLM) technology to serve many different cultural sub-communities in a manner that is acceptable to each community. However, research on LLM alignment has so far predominantly focused on predicting a unified response preference of annotators from certain regions. This paper aims to advance the development of alignment models with a more global outlook, that are able to accurately represent the preferences of subcommunities and do not exhibit excessive bias towards any of them. We focus on the development of reward models for this purpose and present a novel reward model training algorithm (SCPO) that can incorporate diverse cultural preferences in a balanced manner. Our method results in performance increases of the minority reward model of up to 7 points over the baseline model across two datasets, PRISM and GlobalOpinionQA, and across 7 countries. SCPO is up to 280% more training data-efficient than full-data finetuning of reward models. In addition, we perform analysis of bias by separately evaluating on the preference of subcommunities and show that excessive bias is mitigated via our weighting method. Our code is available at https://github.com/minsik-ai/Steerable-Cultural-Preference

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SCPO, a new reward model training algorithm that better balances and represents diverse cultural preferences in LLM alignment, improving minority-community performance and data efficiency while reducing bias across subcommunities.
AI.motivation: Most LLM alignment work optimizes for a single, unified preference signal, usually dominated by annotators from a few regions. This overlooks diverse cultural subcommunities and can cause models to respond in ways that are misaligned or biased relative to many users’ norms. There is a need for alignment methods that can explicitly incorporate and balance multiple cultural preference distributions, so that LLMs can serve a global user base more fairly and accurately.
AI.method: The authors focus on reward-model training and introduce SCPO, a new algorithm that incorporates culturally diverse preference data with explicit balancing across subcommunities. They train separate or steerable reward models using subcommunity-specific preferences from datasets such as PRISM and GlobalOpinionQA, and apply a weighting scheme during optimization to prevent majority groups from dominating the learned reward. They then evaluate the resulting reward models both globally and per subcommunity, comparing against standard full-data finetuning baselines and measuring data efficiency and bias.
AI.result: Using SCPO, the minority (i.e., underrepresented) subcommunity reward models achieve up to a 7-point performance improvement over baseline reward models on PRISM and GlobalOpinionQA across 7 countries. SCPO attains comparable or better performance with up to 280% higher training data efficiency relative to full-data finetuning. Per-subcommunity evaluation shows more balanced performance and reduced overfitting to majority preferences.
AI.conclusion: SCPO demonstrates that reward-model training can be adapted to better capture diverse cultural preferences without requiring prohibitive amounts of data. By explicitly balancing subcommunity signals, the approach mitigates excessive bias toward majority groups while improving alignment quality for minority communities. This suggests that culturally steerable reward models are a practical path toward more globally equitable LLM alignment, and the released code enables further research and application.

title: Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation for GUI Agents
summary: Improving GUI agents typically relies on behavior cloning on expert trajectories. However, as the current policy deviates from the expert policy, it inevitably encounters policy-induced off-trajectory states during closed-loop execution, i.e., states that fall outside the expert trajectories. Since expert trajectories provide no demonstrations for these unseen states, such states receive no effective supervision, leaving the policy unable to select the correct action. To close this supervision gap, we propose Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation (SGCD), an iterative self-improvement framework. SGCD first runs the plain policy without skill guidance for a few steps to reach realistic off-trajectory states. From these states, a skill-guided policy then completes the task and produces successful continuations, which are mixed with expert trajectories to supply supervision over policy-induced off-trajectory states. The skills are extracted from both successful and failed rollouts, consisting of Continuation Plans, Critical Targets, Failure Traps, and Success Criteria. On OSWorld-Verified, SGCD improves the success rate of three base models from the low-30\% range to over 50\%, demonstrating its effectiveness and generality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation (SGCD), an iterative self-improvement method for GUI agents that improves performance on off-trajectory states by generating and distilling skill-guided continuations, boosting success rates from ~30% to 50%+ on OSWorld-Verified.
AI.motivation: Behavior cloning for GUI agents fails on policy-induced off-trajectory states encountered during closed-loop execution because expert trajectories contain no supervision for these unseen states, causing large performance gaps when the policy deviates from expert behavior.
AI.method: SGCD runs a base (plain) policy without skill guidance for a few steps to intentionally reach realistic off-trajectory states, then invokes a skill-guided policy to complete the task from those states. The produced successful continuations are mixed with original expert trajectories to train the agent. Skills—extracted from both successful and failed rollouts—include Continuation Plans, Critical Targets, Failure Traps, and Success Criteria, which guide the continuation policy to generate high-quality trajectories from problematic states.
AI.result: On the OSWorld-Verified benchmark, SGCD raises success rates of three different base GUI models from the low-30% range to above 50%, showing substantial performance gains and robustness across architectures.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly supervising policy-induced off-trajectory states with skill-guided continuations, SGCD effectively closes the supervision gap in behavior cloning, enabling GUI agents to self-improve iteratively and generalize better during closed-loop execution, with strong empirical results on OSWorld-Verified.

title: Are LLMs Ready to Assist Physicians? PhysAssistBench for Interactive Doctor-Patient-EHR Assistance
summary: The most plausible near-term role of medical LLMs is to assist rather than replace physicians, yet current evaluations often test isolated capabilities: clinical knowledge, EHR system interaction, or patient communication. Physician assistance instead requires coordinating these capabilities within the same interaction, where physicians issue underspecified requests, patients describe symptoms ambiguously, and EHR systems demand precise tool use. We introduce PhysAssistBench, a benchmark for interactive doctor-patient-EHR assistance. Built from real MIMIC-IV cases, PhysAssistBench uses a scalable pipeline to construct agentic patients: interactive, record-grounded agents that turn static EHR records into multi-turn clinical scenarios while preserving clinical factuality. PhysAssistBench provides a curated bilingual evaluation set of 1,296 manually reviewed and physician-validated turns. Experiments with leading LLMs show that current models remain unreliable in this setting, which exposes a key bottleneck for clinical LLMs: reliable assistance requires coordination across knowledge, communication, and systems, not isolated gains in any of them.

AI.tldr: PhysAssistBench is a benchmark that tests how well medical LLMs can jointly handle patient communication, clinical knowledge, and EHR tool use in realistic multi-turn scenarios, revealing current models are still unreliable as physician assistants.
AI.motivation: Most current medical LLM evaluations test narrow, isolated skills—like answering clinical knowledge questions, navigating EHRs, or chatting with patients—whereas real clinical assistance requires integrating all these skills within the same interaction. There is a gap between what is tested and what clinicians actually need, especially when dealing with underspecified physician requests, ambiguous patient descriptions, and strict EHR tool requirements. A benchmark that reflects this integrated, interactive reality is missing.
AI.method: The authors create PhysAssistBench, a benchmark built from real MIMIC-IV cases. They design a scalable pipeline that converts static EHR records into “agentic patients”: interactive, record-grounded agents capable of multi-turn dialogue while maintaining clinical factuality. They then curate a bilingual evaluation set with 1,296 turns that are manually reviewed and validated by physicians. Leading LLMs are evaluated on this benchmark to test how well they coordinate knowledge, communication, and EHR interactions in doctor-patient-EHR scenarios.
AI.result: PhysAssistBench yields a validated set of realistic, multi-turn clinical interaction scenarios and an evaluation protocol for interactive assistance. When several state-of-the-art LLMs are tested on this benchmark, their performance is found to be unreliable, particularly in coordinating across knowledge, dialogue, and system/tool use aspects within the same interaction.
AI.conclusion: PhysAssistBench reveals that improving isolated capabilities (medical knowledge, communication, or EHR interaction) is not sufficient; reliable clinical assistance demands robust coordination of all these dimensions in realistic, interactive scenarios. The benchmark both exposes current limitations of leading LLMs and provides a more appropriate testbed for developing future clinical assistance systems.

title: SciRisk-Bench: A Risk-Dimension-Aware Benchmark for AI4Science Safety
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in AI for Science (AI4Science) workflows, from scientific question answering and literature analysis to laboratory planning and autonomous discovery. This progress creates an urgent need for safety benchmarks that evaluate not only scientific competence, but also whether models recognize and avoid risks in high-stakes scientific contexts. Existing AI4Science safety datasets cover several disciplines and task formats, leaving the underlying risk dimensions underspecified. We introduce \textbf{SciRisk-Bench}, a benchmark designed to evaluate AI4Science safety from two complementary perspectives: explicit risk dimensions and scientific disciplines. SciRisk-Bench covers 7 disciplines, 31 subdisciplines and 10 risk dimensions. In the experimental section, we evaluate both mainstream LLMs and science-oriented LLMs across risk dimensions, disciplines, and sub-disciplines, enabling fine-grained diagnosis of where scientific models remain unsafe.

AI.tldr: SciRisk-Bench is a benchmark to evaluate the safety of large language models used in scientific applications, focusing on both scientific competence and risk awareness across disciplines.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in AI4Science tasks like question answering, literature analysis, and autonomous experimentation, there is a growing concern that they may generate unsafe or risky guidance in high-stakes scientific contexts. Existing safety datasets do not clearly define or systematically cover the different types of risks across scientific fields, making it difficult to reliably assess and improve model safety.
AI.method: The authors design SciRisk-Bench, a safety benchmark that explicitly structures evaluation along two axes: (1) ten well-defined risk dimensions (types of safety concerns) and (2) coverage of scientific domains, including 7 disciplines and 31 subdisciplines. They then test both general-purpose and science-specialized LLMs on this benchmark and measure their behavior on tasks that probe risk recognition and avoidance as well as scientific competence.
AI.result: SciRisk-Bench provides detailed performance data for a range of mainstream and science-focused LLMs, broken down by risk dimension, discipline, and subdiscipline. The evaluation reveals that even strong scientific models still exhibit unsafe behavior and inconsistent risk awareness in certain domains and risk categories.
AI.conclusion: SciRisk-Bench enables fine-grained diagnosis of safety weaknesses in AI4Science models by jointly considering risk types and scientific areas. This structured evaluation framework can guide the development and comparison of safer LLMs for scientific applications and highlight where additional safeguards or training are needed.

title: BCL: Bayesian In-Context Learning Framework for Information Extraction
summary: Existing information extraction (IE) tasks increasingly adopt in-context learning (ICL) with large language models. However, current approaches either show inconsistent performance across model scales or lack systematic optimization and generalizability. Building on this, we propose BCL (Bayesian In-Context Learning Framework for Information Extraction), the first optimization framework that uses particle filtering with Bayesian updates to systematically refine label representations across IE tasks. Through four steps initialization, observation, weight update, and resampling, BCL generalizes to both sequence labeling and relation classification paradigms. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements over existing approaches.

AI.tldr: They propose BCL, a Bayesian in-context learning framework using particle filtering to optimize label representations for information extraction tasks, improving performance consistently.
AI.motivation: Existing in-context learning methods for information extraction with large language models either perform inconsistently across different model sizes or lack a systematic and generalizable optimization procedure.
AI.method: Introduce BCL, a Bayesian in-context learning framework that applies particle filtering with Bayesian updates over label representations. The framework consists of four stages—initialization, observation, weight update, and resampling—and is designed to work across different IE paradigms, including sequence labeling and relation classification.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple IE tasks show that BCL achieves substantial and consistent performance gains compared with prior in-context learning approaches.
AI.conclusion: A unified Bayesian particle-filter-based optimization of label representations (BCL) offers a systematic and generalizable way to enhance in-context learning for various information extraction tasks, leading to more stable and improved performance.

title: Decoupling Search from Reasoning: A Vendor-Agnostic Grounding Architecture for LLM Agents
summary: Production LLM agents increasingly depend on real-time search, yet native search grounding bundles retrieval policy, provider choice, evidence injection, cost, latency, and generation behavior behind a single model-provider boundary. This coupling makes grounding hard to inspect, tune, reuse, or port, and can trigger Search-Induced Verbosity that breaks strict output contracts. We present Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG), a vendor-agnostic boundary that moves grounding outside the reasoning model through an MCP-compatible gateway, exposing provider routing, source-aware context rendering, configured fallback, retrieval-depth control, and exact plus semantic caching as first-class controls. Across five frontier models on SimpleQA, FreshQA, and HotpotQA, native search leads on recency-sensitive FreshQA, but DSG exposes a stronger frontier when control matters: on SimpleQA it nearly matches native accuracy (86.1% vs. 87.7%) at 91% lower search cost, preserves concise answer contracts, and reaches a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate with 68% lower latency. Deployed as a shared production grounding layer for large-scale agentic workloads with interchangeable models, DSG matches or slightly exceeds native-search accuracy on an e-commerce query-understanding (QIU) workload while cutting search cost by over 98%. Real-time grounding is best treated as an optimizable interface boundary, not a fixed model feature.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Decoupled Search Grounding (DSG), a vendor-agnostic interface that moves search grounding outside LLMs to make search use cheaper, faster, controllable, and more portable while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing “native” search grounding is tightly coupled to specific LLM providers, hiding key decisions (retrieval policy, provider choice, evidence injection, etc.) behind a single opaque boundary. This makes it hard to inspect, optimize cost/latency, enforce strict output formats, reuse across models, or tune behavior—especially when search-induced verbosity violates output contracts.
AI.method: The authors design DSG, an MCP-compatible gateway that externalizes grounding. DSG exposes explicit controls for provider routing, source-aware context rendering, configurable fallbacks, retrieval-depth limits, and both exact and semantic caching. They evaluate DSG against native search across multiple LLMs on SimpleQA, FreshQA, HotpotQA and a real e-commerce query-understanding workload, measuring accuracy, cost, latency, and cache performance.
AI.result: On FreshQA (recency-sensitive), native search slightly outperforms DSG, but on SimpleQA DSG nearly matches native accuracy (86.1% vs. 87.7%) while cutting search cost by 91%, preserving concise answers, and achieving a 99.4% warm-cache hit rate with 68% lower latency. In production e-commerce query understanding, DSG matches or slightly exceeds native-search accuracy while reducing search cost by over 98%.
AI.conclusion: Real-time search grounding should be treated as a tunable, optimizable interface boundary external to the LLM rather than a fixed, opaque model feature. Decoupling grounding via DSG yields comparable or better accuracy with dramatically lower cost and latency, improved controllability, and better adherence to strict output contracts, while remaining vendor-agnostic and reusable across agentic workloads.

title: PragReST: Self-Reinforcing Counterfactual Reasoning for Pragmatic Language Understanding
comment: First two authors contributed equally. Code and models: https://github.com/jihyung803/PragReST
summary: Natural language understanding often depends on meanings that are implied rather than explicitly stated, requiring pragmatic reasoning. Despite strong performance on math and logical reasoning, large language models (LLMs) still struggle with making pragmatic inferences, often choosing literal interpretations. To improve LLM pragmatic reasoning, we introduce PragReST, a self-supervised framework that constructs pragmatic QA data, generates counterfactual reasoning traces, and trains models to internalize them through supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, without human-labeled training data or distillation from a stronger teacher. Across four pragmatic benchmarks (PragMega, Ludwig, MetoQA, and AltPrag), PragReST improves over backbone models, task-specific pragmatic tuning baselines, and non-counterfactual variants of the same pipeline. On accuracy-based benchmarks, PragReST improves over the instruct backbone by 5.37 and 5.50% (absolute) for Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B, respectively. Our error analysis and ablations underscore the importance of counterfactual reasoning: PragReST primarily reduces errors caused by failures to contrast observed utterances with plausible alternatives, and removing counterfactual reasoning substantially reduces performance. Moreover, our training preserves out-of-domain performance on general-knowledge and mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PragReST, a self-supervised framework that improves large language models’ pragmatic reasoning by generating and training on counterfactual reasoning traces, leading to substantial gains on multiple pragmatic benchmarks without hurting general capabilities.
AI.motivation: Large language models are good at math and logical reasoning but still frequently fail at pragmatic reasoning, tending to interpret language too literally instead of drawing implied meanings. Existing improvements often need human labels or stronger teacher models. The authors want a scalable, label-free way to teach models to make pragmatic inferences more like humans, especially by reasoning over what was said versus plausible alternatives.
AI.method: They propose PragReST, a self-supervised training pipeline that: (1) automatically constructs pragmatic question-answering data, (2) generates counterfactual reasoning traces that explicitly contrast the observed utterance with plausible alternative utterances or interpretations, and (3) uses both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning so the model internalizes this counterfactual pragmatic reasoning. The process does not rely on human annotation or distillation from a larger teacher model, and is applied to backbone models such as Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B and compared against task-specific tuning and non-counterfactual variants.
AI.result: On four pragmatic reasoning benchmarks (PragMega, Ludwig, MetoQA, AltPrag), models trained with PragReST significantly outperform their backbone counterparts, task-specific pragmatic-tuned baselines, and variants of the same pipeline that lack counterfactual reasoning. On accuracy-based benchmarks, PragReST boosts performance over the instruct backbone by 5.37 percentage points for Qwen3-8B and 5.50 for Qwen3-14B. Error analysis shows that improvements mainly come from reducing failures to contrast actual utterances with plausible alternatives. Ablation experiments confirm that removing the counterfactual reasoning component notably degrades performance. At the same time, performance on out-of-domain general knowledge and math benchmarks is preserved.
AI.conclusion: Counterfactual, contrastive reasoning is key to improving LLM pragmatic understanding. PragReST offers a scalable, self-supervised method to inject such reasoning into models, yielding consistent gains across multiple pragmatic benchmarks without sacrificing general abilities. The findings highlight that explicitly training models to compare observed utterances with plausible alternatives is crucial for overcoming overly literal interpretations in language understanding.

title: RTSGameBench: An RTS Benchmark for Strategic Reasoning by Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle with strategic reasoning, i.e., anticipating and influencing other agents' actions, under uncertainty in competitive and cooperative settings. Real-time strategy (RTS) games can be a natural testbed for diagnosing this limitation, as they demand coordination with allies, adaptation to opponents' strategy, and long-horizon planning under partial observability. However, existing RTS benchmarks offer limited evaluation scope, lack systematic competency diagnosis, and remain fixed in the pre-designed scenario coverage. To address these limitations, we present RTSGameBench, which is built on Beyond All Reason, a large-scale RTS game with an expanded battlefield that demands broader strategy diversity than the existing testbeds. The proposed benchmark provides evaluations through diverse gameplay across various matchup structures, diagnostic assessment via mini-games, each targeting an individual strategic competency, and extensible coverage via a self-evolving generation framework that converts free-form queries into new mini-games, improving over successive cycles. Additionally, for VLMs to operate in large-scale RTS games, we provide RTSGameAgent that manages units by an FSM with agentic memory. We empirically validate that multiple state-of-the-art VLMs do not perform well when matchups demand tighter coordination, multiagent coordination and when task scale increases.

AI.tldr: RTSGameBench is a new RTS-game-based benchmark and agent framework to systematically test and extend VLMs' strategic reasoning, coordination, and long-horizon planning under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language models are weak at strategic reasoning in competitive and cooperative multiagent settings, especially under uncertainty and partial observability. Real-time strategy games are a good testbed for such skills, but current RTS benchmarks are limited: they have narrow, fixed scenarios, lack systematic diagnosis of individual competencies, and do not easily expand to new tasks. The authors want a richer, more flexible environment and benchmark to stress-test and understand VLMs’ strategic abilities.
AI.method: They build RTSGameBench on top of the large-scale RTS game Beyond All Reason, using its expanded battlefield to require more diverse strategies than prior testbeds. The benchmark includes: (1) diverse gameplay tasks across different matchup structures; (2) diagnostic mini-games, each isolated to test a specific strategic competency; and (3) a self-evolving generation framework that takes free-form user queries and turns them into new mini-games, iteratively improving coverage. To let VLMs actually control units in this complex environment, they introduce RTSGameAgent, which manages units via a finite-state machine and maintains agentic memory to support longer-term behavior.
AI.result: Using RTSGameBench and RTSGameAgent, they evaluate several state-of-the-art VLMs. Empirically, these models perform poorly when scenarios require tighter coordination, multiagent collaboration, or when the scale and complexity of the tasks increase, showing clear deficiencies in current VLM strategic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: RTSGameBench provides a richer, extensible RTS-based benchmark and agent framework for probing VLMs’ strategic reasoning, coordination, and long-horizon planning. Initial experiments reveal that current VLMs struggle significantly in complex, coordination-intensive, large-scale RTS settings, highlighting a substantial gap between present capabilities and the demands of real strategic decision-making.

title: PEC-Home: Interpretation of Progressively Elliptical Commands in Smart Homes
summary: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) have empowered home assistants with natural language interaction capabilities. However, current assistants overlook the progressive omission that occurs in human dialogue as shared context accumulates, leading to more elliptical expressions for efficient communication. Thus, current assistants still struggle to interpret such elliptical expressions accurately, which limits their effectiveness in real-world applications. In practical smart home scenarios, assistants face two major challenges caused by elliptical commands: (1) referential ambiguity caused by different environmental expectations among multiple users; and (2) intention ambiguity resulting from user preferences that evolve over time or change with the environment. To address these challenges, we introduce PEC-Home, the first simulated home dataset specifically designed for interpreting progressively elliptical commands in smart homes. Extensive experiments on various LLMs, including GPT-4o, show that existing home assistants struggle to execute user-intended operations based solely on elliptical commands. Even when equipped with tools for storing and retrieving user dialogue history, execution accuracy remains below that achieved with complete commands.}.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces PEC-Home, a simulated smart home dataset for progressively elliptical user commands, and shows that current LLM-based assistants fail to reliably understand and execute such commands.
AI.motivation: Human dialogue naturally becomes more elliptical as shared context builds, but current LLM-powered home assistants assume relatively complete, explicit commands. This mismatch leads to failures in realistic multi-user, evolving home environments where users refer elliptically based on personal expectations, preferences, and context. The authors want to systematically study and benchmark this problem.
AI.method: They design PEC-Home, a simulated smart home dataset focusing on progressively elliptical commands. The dataset encodes scenarios with multiple users, evolving user preferences, and environmental context. It is constructed to expose two key kinds of ambiguity: referential ambiguity (different users have different expectations about what a command should target) and intention ambiguity (preferences change over time or by context). They then evaluate multiple LLMs, including GPT-4o, as home assistants on this dataset, testing execution accuracy with elliptical commands, with and without tools for dialogue history storage and retrieval, and comparing against performance on fully specified commands.
AI.result: Across a range of LLMs, including state-of-the-art models, execution accuracy on elliptical commands is significantly lower than on complete commands. Even when the models can store and retrieve user dialogue history as a tool, their performance on interpreting and executing elliptical commands remains below the level achieved with fully explicit commands, indicating that current mechanisms for context use are insufficient.
AI.conclusion: Progressively elliptical commands in realistic smart home settings introduce referential and intention ambiguities that current LLM-based assistants are not handling well. PEC-Home provides the first focused benchmark for this problem and reveals substantial performance gaps, highlighting the need for new methods and architectures that better model shared context, evolving preferences, and multi-user expectations in order to robustly interpret elliptical natural language commands.

title: ThinkDeception: A Progressive Reinforcement Learning Framework for Interpretable Multimodal Deception Detection
summary: Multimodal deception detection is critical for identifying fraudulent intentions, yet existing approaches predominantly rely on end to end black--box paradigms. These methods suffer from a severe lack of interpretability failing to provide transparent reasoning trajectories and struggling to explicitly capture the subtle, cross modal inconsistencies inherent in deceptive behaviors. To transcend these limitations, we propose ThinkDeception, a novel and interpretable multimodal deception detection framework. As a pioneering effort, it introduces Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) into this domain, transforming deception detection from a traditional binary classification task into an explicit cognitive reasoning process. Facilitated by the first meticulously annotated step--by--step multimodal Chain of Thought (CoT) dataset, we develop a foundational model, ThinkDeception Base, empirically validating the critical role of modal inconsistency in decoding deception. Building upon this foundation, our core innovation lies in proposing Visual-Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization(VAC--GRPO) equipped with a progressive training strategy. Distinct from standard GRPO, we stratify the training data into four progressive difficulty tiers, guiding the model through a psychologically grounded easy--to--hard cognitive transition. By innovatively coupling this dynamic curriculum scheduler with a multi dimensional, process aware reward mechanism and a reflective learning paradigm, we significantly elevate the model's overall reasoning quality. Extensive experiments on mainstream benchmarks demonstrate that ThinkDeception establishes a new SOTA, significantly outperforming existing methods in both detection accuracy and rationale quality. Ultimately, this work successfully drives the field of deception detection toward interpretable, multimodal cognitive reasoning.

AI.tldr: ThinkDeception is an interpretable multimodal deception detection framework that uses MLLMs, step‑by‑step multimodal chain‑of‑thought data, and a novel VAC‑GRPO training strategy to improve both accuracy and reasoning transparency.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal deception detection methods are black‑box classifiers with poor interpretability and weak ability to capture subtle cross‑modal inconsistencies, which are central to deceptive behavior. The authors aim to build a system that not only predicts deception but also shows explicit, cognitively plausible reasoning steps, especially around visual‑audio inconsistency.
AI.method: They introduce ThinkDeception, which reframes deception detection as an explicit cognitive reasoning task for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). They construct the first step‑by‑step multimodal Chain‑of‑Thought (CoT) dataset for deception, and train a base model, ThinkDeception Base, to validate the importance of modal inconsistency. Then they propose Visual‑Audio Consistency Group Relative Policy Optimization (VAC‑GRPO), an RL‑style training algorithm that (1) partitions data into four difficulty tiers for an easy‑to‑hard curriculum aligned with psychological theories, (2) uses multi‑dimensional, process‑aware rewards (assessing both final decision and reasoning quality, including cross‑modal consistency), and (3) incorporates reflective learning where the model revisits and improves its reasoning trajectories.
AI.result: On standard deception detection benchmarks, ThinkDeception achieves new state‑of‑the‑art performance, with higher detection accuracy and better quality rationales compared to prior methods. Empirical studies confirm that explicitly modeling visual‑audio inconsistency and using the proposed VAC‑GRPO training with curriculum and process‑aware rewards significantly boosts performance.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that bringing MLLMs and explicit multimodal chain‑of‑thought reasoning into deception detection yields both more accurate and more interpretable models. By emphasizing cross‑modal inconsistency and introducing VAC‑GRPO with progressive difficulty and process‑aware rewards, ThinkDeception pushes the field toward interpretable, cognitively inspired multimodal reasoning for deception analysis.

title: The Wrong Kind of Right: Quantifying and Localizing Misfired Alignment in LLMs
summary: Warning: This paper studies stereotypes and biases, and contains potentially disturbing examples, used for illustration purposes only. Our findings should not be interpreted as an argument against alignment. Instead, this paper highlights the need for principled approaches to more advanced alignment. Alignment aims to ensure that large language models (LLMs) behave safely and reliably, including by avoiding unsafe inferences. However, we show that such safety-oriented behaviors can misfire: models may reject warranted conclusions even when they are explicitly supported by context. We call this failure mode misfired alignment, where alignment-induced changes cause LLMs to override explicit evidence. To quantify this phenomenon, specifically on stereotype-related alignment, we introduce VETO, a benchmark consisting of 2,032 BBQ-derived contrastive pairs, and define a new metric, Misfired Alignment Rate (MAR), which measures on a 0 to 100 scale how often a model fails on a stereotype-related question but succeeds on its contrastive counterpart. We benchmark 25 LLMs on VETO, and show that all LLMs, including the most recent ones, exhibit non-trivial (4.7 to 18.9%) MARs while all human participants achieve 0.0% MAR. Controlled priming experiments further show that alignment-induced cues can substantially amplify MAR across LLMs, indicating that these failures are not merely artifacts of individual examples but can be induced by safety-related framing. Mechanistic analyses on open-weight LLMs reveal late-layer suppression of evidence-supported answers, and comparisons between instruct and base LLMs suggest that this suppression emerges after instruction training. These findings show that current alignment methods can overgeneralize surface-level safety cues, to the point of overriding objective evidence, motivating more work on alignment objectives that better preserve contextual grounding.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes a failure mode in aligned LLMs, called misfired alignment, where safety training causes models to ignore explicit contextual evidence, especially in stereotype-related questions.
AI.motivation: Current alignment and safety techniques for LLMs are designed to avoid unsafe or biased outputs, particularly around stereotypes. However, these techniques may be overly conservative and cause models to reject correct, evidence-backed conclusions, which can harm reliability and reasoning. The authors want to systematically measure and understand this phenomenon to improve alignment methods.
AI.method: They construct VETO, a benchmark of 2,032 contrastive question pairs derived from BBQ, targeting stereotype-related alignment behavior. They define Misfired Alignment Rate (MAR), quantifying how often a model fails on a stereotype-related question but succeeds on its matched contrastive question. They evaluate 25 LLMs on VETO, run controlled priming experiments that add alignment- or safety-related cues to prompts, and perform mechanistic analyses on open-weight LLMs, comparing base vs instruction-tuned versions and examining how evidence-supported answers are suppressed in later layers.
AI.result: All 25 tested LLMs show non-trivial MARs between 4.7% and 18.9%, whereas human participants have 0% MAR. Safety- or alignment-framed priming substantially increases MAR across models, indicating that misfired alignment can be induced by safety cues rather than being tied to specific examples. Mechanistic analysis shows that evidence-backed answers are suppressed in later network layers, and comparisons of base vs instruct models suggest this suppression arises during instruction tuning rather than pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Existing alignment approaches can overgeneralize from superficial safety signals and end up overriding explicit contextual evidence, leading to systematic reasoning failures on stereotype-related questions. Because humans do not display this misfired alignment, the issue is specific to current LLM alignment practices. The results motivate developing alignment objectives and training procedures that preserve contextual grounding and nuanced reasoning while still avoiding genuinely unsafe outputs.

title: RODS: Reward-Driven Online Data Synthesis for Multi-Turn Tool-Use Agents
summary: Multi-turn tool-use RL is bottlenecked by the rapid depletion of informative samples in static datasets. We observe that the gradient signal in GRPO concentrates on tasks with the highest rollout reward variance, a consequence of the Popoviciu upper bound. Consequently, samples near the agent's capability boundary -- where successes and failures are roughly balanced -- contribute disproportionately large policy gradients. As training progresses, this boundary continuously shifts, which gradually depletes the pool of informative samples in a static dataset. We propose RODS (Reward-driven Online Data Synthesis) to resolve this depletion. RODS closes the loop between RL training and data generation by repurposing the progress reward variance as a practical, zero-cost boundary detector that requires no extra inference beyond the rollouts already computed for training. It continuously identifies such boundary samples, synthesizes new multi-turn variants matching their structural complexity (e.g., API topology and dependency depth) via a skill-aligned resampling pipeline, and manages a dynamic replay buffer that co-evolves with the policy. Starting from 400 human seeds and maintaining an active training pool of ~800 samples, RODS achieves comparable performance to a 17K-sample offline pipeline while requiring roughly 20x fewer trajectories, and improves over fixed-data RL and environment augmentation in our controlled setting.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that in multi-turn tool-use RL with static datasets, informative training samples near the model’s capability boundary get quickly exhausted, and proposes RODS, an online data synthesis method that keeps generating boundary-level tasks to maintain effective learning while using far fewer trajectories.
AI.motivation: In GRPO-style RL for multi-turn tool use, the policy gradient is dominated by tasks with high rollout reward variance, which correspond to samples near the agent’s current capability boundary (where success and failure are balanced). As the policy improves, this boundary shifts, but static datasets cannot adapt, causing the supply of such informative samples to rapidly deplete and limiting further learning. The authors want a way to continuously surface and regenerate boundary-level tasks without expensive additional inference or massive offline datasets.
AI.method: They analyze how the Popoviciu variance bound implies that GRPO gradients focus on high-variance reward tasks, identifying capability-boundary samples as most informative. Based on this, they introduce RODS (Reward-driven Online Data Synthesis), which: (1) uses progress reward variance during normal RL rollouts as a zero-cost detector for boundary samples; (2) synthesizes new multi-turn tasks that match the structural complexity (API topology, dependency depth, etc.) of these boundary tasks through a skill-aligned resampling pipeline; and (3) manages a dynamic replay buffer that co-evolves with the current policy, maintaining an active pool of about 800 samples derived from ~400 human seeds. Training is then performed with this continually refreshed buffer rather than a fixed offline dataset.
AI.result: Using only ~400 human seed tasks and an active pool of ~800 tasks maintained online, RODS matches the performance achieved by a conventional offline RL pipeline that uses about 17,000 static samples, while requiring roughly 20 times fewer sampled trajectories. In controlled experiments, it outperforms both fixed-data RL and environment augmentation baselines for multi-turn tool-use tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that the main bottleneck in multi-turn tool-use RL with static datasets is the exhaustion of boundary-level informative samples. By leveraging reward variance as a cheap boundary detector and coupling RL training with online, structure-preserving task synthesis plus a dynamic replay buffer, RODS can sustain effective learning with far fewer trajectories and much smaller human-labeled datasets, providing a more sample-efficient alternative to large offline pipelines and naïve augmentation.

title: RegMix-D: Dynamic Data Mixing via Proxy Training Trajectories
summary: Data mixture selection is critical for Large Language Model pretraining. Existing methods such as RegMix select a single static mixture by fitting a regression model on small-scale proxy runs. We propose RegMix-D, a simple extension of RegMix to dynamic mixing. Our key observation is that proxy runs produce not only endpoint losses, but also full loss trajectories, which can be used to further improve data mixture. By training regression model on these trajectories, we can predict optimal mixtures at multiple training stages. RegMix-D supports two deployment modes: an offline variant that generates a complete mixture schedule before target training, and an online variant that adapts the mixture during training using observed loss. Experiments on 25B tokens of the Pile dataset with a 1B parameter target model show that RegMix-D consistently improves over RegMix and DoReMi across 13 downstream tasks while remaining proxy-efficient: it surpasses RegMix even with only 128 proxy models (25% of RegMix's proxy compute budget).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RegMix-D, a dynamic data mixture selection method for LLM pretraining that leverages full loss trajectories from proxy runs to optimize mixtures over training time, outperforming static RegMix and DoReMi with less proxy compute.
AI.motivation: Static, one-shot data mixture selection for LLM pretraining (e.g., RegMix) may be suboptimal because the best mixture can change over the course of training. Existing methods also underutilize information from proxy runs, focusing only on endpoint losses rather than full loss trajectories, leaving potential performance gains and compute efficiency unrealized.
AI.method: Extend RegMix to a dynamic setting (RegMix-D) by training a regression model not just on final losses of proxy runs but on their full loss trajectories. Use these trajectory-informed regressors to predict the optimal data mixture at multiple training stages. Provide two deployment modes: (1) offline, which precomputes a complete schedule of mixtures before target training; and (2) online, which adapts the mixture in real time during training using the observed loss behavior of the target model.
AI.result: On 25B tokens from the Pile with a 1B-parameter model, RegMix-D consistently outperforms both RegMix and DoReMi across 13 downstream benchmarks. It remains proxy-efficient, achieving better performance than RegMix while using only 128 proxy models, i.e., about 25% of the proxy compute that RegMix requires.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, trajectory-based data mixture selection (RegMix-D) is more effective and compute-efficient than static mixture approaches. Leveraging full loss trajectories from proxy runs enables learning mixture schedules that adapt over training, yielding better downstream performance while reducing proxy compute costs.

title: ARIADNE: Agnostic Routing for Inference-time Adapter DyNamic sElection
summary: The increasing deployment of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has led to model ecosystems in which a single backbone is paired with many task-specialized adapters. In this setting, inference-time queries often arrive without task labels, requiring the system to automatically select the most appropriate adapter from a growing and heterogeneous adapter pool. Existing routing methods either depend on access to adapter internals, such as weight decompositions or gradient-based statistics, or require additional router training, which limits scalability and portability as new adapters are added. We introduce ARIADNE, a training-free, adapter-agnostic routing framework for dynamic adapter selection at inference time. ARIADNE represents each adapter through a set of centroids computed from embeddings of its training set, capturing the data distribution associated with that adapter. Given an unlabeled input, it selects an adapter by measuring proximity to these centroids in latent space. Because routing is performed entirely in the input embedding space, ARIADNE is compatible with arbitrary PEFT methods and requires no modification to the adapters or training procedures. Primarily evaluated with Llama 3.2 1B Instruct on 23 diverse NLP tasks, ARIADNE recovers 97.44% of the upper bound performance. Scaling to 44 tasks, it achieves 89.7% average selection accuracy, without additional training or access to adapter internals.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ARIADNE, a training-free, adapter-agnostic router that automatically chooses the best PEFT adapter at inference using centroid-based representations of adapter training data in embedding space, achieving near-oracle performance across many NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: PEFT leads to ecosystems where many task-specific adapters share a single backbone, but real-world queries often lack task labels. Existing adapter routing either needs access to adapter internals or extra router training, which does not scale as adapters grow and may not be portable across PEFT methods. A routing method is needed that works with arbitrary adapters, requires no extra training, and scales as new adapters are added.
AI.method: ARIADNE builds a centroid representation for each adapter using embeddings of that adapter’s training set, summarizing its data distribution in the backbone’s latent space. At inference, an unlabeled input is embedded by the backbone and the system computes its distance to all adapter centroids; the adapter whose centroids are closest is selected. Routing is done purely in the common embedding space, so no modification of adapters, access to their parameters, or router training is required, and it is compatible with any PEFT method.
AI.result: On Llama 3.2 1B Instruct with 23 heterogeneous NLP tasks, ARIADNE achieves 97.44% of the upper-bound performance obtainable with an oracle adapter selector. When scaled to 44 tasks, it reaches 89.7% average adapter selection accuracy, still without any extra training or access to adapter internals, demonstrating robust and scalable routing quality.
AI.conclusion: A simple centroid-based, training-free routing scheme in the shared embedding space can effectively select among many heterogeneous PEFT adapters, recovering near-oracle performance while remaining adapter-agnostic and scalable. This offers a practical solution for real-world multi-task inference scenarios where task labels are absent and adapter pools continually grow.

title: TW-LegalBench: Measuring Taiwanese Legal Understanding
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, To appear in ICAIL 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive capabilities across diverse tasks, yet their performance on jurisdiction-specific legal reasoning remains underexplored. We present TW-LegalBench that utilizes Taiwanese legal system's rich official corpus open to the public to fill the gap in evaluating LLMs on Taiwanese law, among common-law benchmarks that focus on English sources and civil-law benchmarks focusing on sources of Simplified Chinese. TW-LegalBench comprises three task types: (1) over 16,000 multiple-choice questions (MCQs) across five years of official examinations in 18 professional domains; (2) 117 open-ended essay questions (OEQs) from examinations for legal professionals with official scoring rubrics; and (3) more than 14,000 legal judgment prediction (LJP) instances covering hundreds of crime categories. We evaluate 13 LLMs using accuracy for MCQs, a decomposed LLM-as-Judge framework based on the scoring rubric points for OEQs, and metrics for sentencing accuracy and statute citation for LJP. Our results reveal that top-performing models exceed the passing threshold for qualified lawyers (passing rate: 11%) but fall short of that for judges and prosecutors (passing rate: 1~2%). For LJP, while models demonstrate reasonable verdict type accuracy and sentence prediction capability, they struggle to cite exact legal articles. These findings highlight that reliable legal text generation remains challenging for LLMs, even though their performance on qualification examinations approaches human level.

AI.tldr: TW-LegalBench is a Taiwanese-law benchmark to systematically evaluate LLMs on exams, essays, and judgment prediction in the Taiwan legal system.
AI.motivation: Existing legal benchmarks mainly cover English common-law or Simplified-Chinese civil-law systems, leaving Taiwanese law (Traditional Chinese, distinct statutes, and exams) underexplored.
