The authors want a jurisdiction-specific, large-scale, publicly sourced benchmark to assess how well LLMs handle real Taiwanese legal tasks and professional qualification standards.
AI.method: They construct TW-LegalBench using publicly available official Taiwanese legal corpora, creating three components: (1) >16,000 multiple-choice questions from five years of official exams in 18 professional domains; (2) 117 open-ended essay questions for legal professionals with associated official scoring rubrics; and (3) >14,000 legal judgment prediction items spanning hundreds of crime categories. They then evaluate 13 LLMs: MCQs via accuracy; OEQs via a decomposed LLM-as-Judge scheme that scores each rubric point; and LJP via verdict accuracy plus sentencing and statute-citation metrics.
AI.result: Top LLMs surpass the pass line for becoming qualified lawyers (where only about 11% of human test-takers pass) but still do not reach the much higher bar for judges and prosecutors (1–2% human pass rate). For judgment prediction, models can often get the verdict type and sentencing roughly right but frequently fail to identify and cite the precise statutory articles, indicating gaps in fine-grained legal grounding.
AI.conclusion: Despite near-human performance on some formal qualification-style tasks, LLMs are not yet reliable for nuanced, grounded legal reasoning and text generation in the Taiwanese context, particularly in statute citation and higher-level professional standards. TW-LegalBench provides a jurisdiction-specific benchmark to expose these limitations and support future improvements in legally robust LLMs.

title: Towards an Agent-First Web: Redesigning the Web for AI Agents
summary:
The World Wide Web was built on an assumption held for three decades: the primary consumer of web content is a human being. This permeates every layer; its access model presumes human visitors, its economics rest on human attention, and its content targets human perception. The rapid emergence of AI agents as intermediaries between humans and web content invalidates this assumption. Yet the web resists agents through blanket blocking, CAPTCHA-based exclusion, and economic models that treat agent access as extraction rather than legitimate interaction.
  This paper proposes a principled redesign across three layers. At the access layer, agents acting for humans should inherit equivalent access rights, governed by rate limiting and agent identification metadata in HTTP requests, analogous to browser headers, alongside a dual-layer architecture serving human-readable and agent-optimized content from the same domain. At the economic layer, we propose an intent-based tier framework grounded in the agent-as-human-proxy principle: an agent's economic obligation mirrors that of the human it represents. A token-based subscription model meters content in tokens rather than pageviews, alongside a commissioned content economy anchoring AI content production in human intentionality. At the content layer, we identify epistemic recursion, the self-referential loop in which AI-generated content is consumed by agents to produce further content, progressively detaching web knowledge from human ground truth. We propose the Agent Text Markup Language (ATML), a four-level human supervision tier model, and a cryptographic provenance chain to counter this threat.
  Together these constitute ten design principles for an agent-first internet, one in which agents are first-class citizens whose integration requires renegotiating the web's foundational social contract across access, economics, and content.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that the Web must be redesigned for AI agents as first-class users, proposing new access rules, economic models, and content standards so that agents can legitimately interact with and help sustain the internet.
AI.motivation: The Web was originally built for human users, assuming that humans are the primary consumers of online content. With the rapid rise of AI agents acting as intermediaries between humans and the Web, this assumption is breaking down. Current Web practices—such as blocking bots, using CAPTCHAs to exclude non-humans, and viewing automated access as exploitative—create friction and treat agents as illegitimate participants. Additionally, the growing volume of AI-generated content risks creating an epistemic loop where agents train on and reproduce their own outputs, drifting away from human-grounded truths. The authors are motivated to resolve these tensions and prevent degradation of the Web’s epistemic quality by rethinking the Web’s architecture, economics, and content practices for an agent-rich environment.
AI.method: The authors conceptually redesign the Web along three layers—access, economics, and content—under the guiding “agent-as-human-proxy” principle. At the access layer, they define a framework where agents acting on behalf of humans inherit the same access rights as their human principals, enforced via rate limiting and explicit agent-identification metadata in HTTP requests. They also propose a dual-layer architecture that delivers both human-readable and agent-optimized content from the same domain. At the economic layer, they introduce an intent-based tiering framework in which an agent’s economic obligations parallel those of the human it represents, implemented with a token-based subscription model that meters usage in fine-grained content tokens instead of pageviews. They further sketch a commissioned-content marketplace where AI content production is explicitly tied to human-defined goals. At the content layer, they analyze the problem of “epistemic recursion” in AI-generated content and propose the Agent Text Markup Language (ATML), which encodes four levels of human supervision, along with cryptographic provenance chains to track content origins and transformations. From these elements, they derive ten overarching design principles for an “agent-first” Web.
AI.result: The paper yields a structured proposal rather than empirical results. It produces: (1) a normative access-layer design where AI agents are recognized as first-class web citizens, with standardized identification and rate-limiting practices; (2) an intent- and token-based economic model aligning agent access costs with human intentions and usage, including a framework for commissioned AI content as a legitimate economic activity; (3) a content-layer specification that includes ATML for encoding degrees of human supervision and a cryptographic provenance mechanism to mitigate epistemic recursion. These are synthesized into ten formal design principles for building an agent-first internet.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that the Web’s long-standing human-centric assumption is no longer tenable in an era where AI agents mediate a substantial share of interactions. Treating agents as adversaries—through blocking, CAPTCHAs, and extractive economic framings—both mischaracterizes their role and fails to harness their potential. Instead, the Web should reconceive agents as human proxies and first-class participants. By restructuring access controls, economic models, and content standards—via mechanisms like agent-identification headers, token-based subscriptions, ATML, and cryptographic provenance—the internet can accommodate agents without sacrificing fairness, sustainability, or epistemic integrity. This reconfiguration represents a renegotiation of the Web’s foundational social contract to support an agent-first future.

title: LLMs Struggle to Measure What Distinguishes Students of Different Proficiency Levels: A Study of Item Discrimination in Reading Comprehension Assessment
summary: Item discrimination is a fundamental psychometric property of educational assessment, which measures whether an item meaningfully distinguishes students with higher proficiency from students with lower proficiency. While various existing works have explored whether large language models (LLMs) can estimate item difficulty, it remains unclear whether they can capture item discrimination. In this work, we evaluate 42 proprietary and open-weight LLMs in zero-shot settings using two complementary approaches: direct discrimination prediction, where models explicitly estimate an item's discrimination value from its content, and response-based Classical Test Theory (CTT) calibration, where LLM answers are treated as synthetic student responses to compute discrimination scores. Our results show that direct prediction yields weak alignment with human-calibrated discrimination: the best-performing model reaches only a Spearman correlation of 0.152. Response-based CTT calibration provides a stronger but still limited signal, with the all-persona synthetic respondent pool reaching a Spearman correlation of 0.241. These findings highlight item discrimination as an open challenge for LLM-based psychometric evaluation: current LLMs contain non-random discrimination-relevant signal, but they do not yet reliably capture how assessment items distinguish human students.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large language models can estimate item discrimination in educational assessments and finds that current models capture only weak, unreliable discrimination signals.
AI.motivation: While prior work has shown that LLMs can approximate item difficulty, it is unknown whether they can also capture item discrimination, a key psychometric property indicating how well test items distinguish between high- and low-proficiency students. Understanding this would clarify the suitability and limits of LLMs for test analysis and design.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 42 proprietary and open-weight LLMs in zero-shot settings using two approaches: (1) direct discrimination prediction, where models are prompted to estimate an item's discrimination parameter from its content, and (2) response-based Classical Test Theory (CTT) calibration, where model answers are used as synthetic student responses to compute item discrimination statistics. They then compare model-based estimates with human-calibrated discrimination values, primarily via Spearman correlation.
AI.result: Direct prediction shows very low alignment with human-calibrated discrimination, with the best model achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.152. The response-based CTT calibration approach performs somewhat better but remains limited: using all synthetic personas as respondents yields a maximum Spearman correlation of 0.241 with human discrimination values.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs do encode some non-random information related to item discrimination but fail to reliably approximate how test items differentiate human students. Item discrimination remains an unsolved and challenging dimension for LLM-based psychometric evaluation, indicating that current models are not yet suitable as dependable tools for estimating this property in educational assessments.

title: Analysing drivers and interdependencies in European electricity markets using XAI
summary: Electricity markets are inherently complex systems characterised by strong nonlinearities, high-dimensional interactions, and increasing interdependence across regions. While deep neural networks (DNNs) have demonstrated strong predictive capabilities for electricity prices, their lack of interpretability limits their usefulness for understanding the underlying drivers of price formation. This paper addresses this gap by combining DNN models with explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to analyse the determinants of electricity prices across 39 European bidding zones. We employ SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to quantify feature contributions and apply and extend SSHAP, an aggregation framework to improve interpretability in high-dimensional settings. The analysis identifies that renewable energy sources, particularly solar, play a disproportionately important role in price formation despite their lower share in total power generation. Gas prices remain a dominant and consistent driver across electricity markets, while interconnections significantly shape price dynamics, highlighting the strong interdependence of European electricity systems. In addition, a synthetic EU-wide electricity market is constructed to explore the counterfactual scenario of a fully integrated market with a single price.

AI.tldr: The paper uses deep neural networks combined with explainable AI (SHAP/SSHAP) to understand what drives electricity prices in 39 European bidding zones, finding that solar, gas prices, and cross-border interconnections are key determinants, and exploring a hypothetical fully integrated EU-wide power market with a single price.
AI.motivation: Electricity price formation in Europe is driven by complex, nonlinear, and interdependent factors across many regions. While deep neural networks can forecast prices well, they are often black boxes, offering little insight into the underlying drivers. Policymakers, market participants, and system planners need interpretable models to understand how renewables, fuels, and interconnections shape prices, particularly as the system decarbonizes and becomes more integrated. The paper is motivated by this need for high predictive accuracy combined with transparency and economic interpretability.
AI.method: The authors build deep neural network (DNN) models to predict electricity prices for 39 European bidding zones. To interpret these black-box models, they use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to quantify the marginal contribution of each input feature (e.g., generation by technology, fuel prices, interconnection flows) to predicted prices. They then apply and extend SSHAP, an aggregation framework that groups SHAP values to enhance interpretability in a high-dimensional feature space, making it easier to understand patterns across regions and variables. They also construct a synthetic EU-wide electricity market scenario to simulate a fully integrated system with a single common price and analyse it with the same tools.
AI.result: The analysis shows that renewable energy sources—particularly solar—have a disproportionately large impact on electricity prices relative to their share in total generation. Gas prices emerge as a strong and consistent driver of prices across nearly all European bidding zones, reflecting their role in marginal cost setting. Cross-border interconnections are found to significantly influence price dynamics, evidencing strong interdependence among European electricity markets. In the synthetic integrated market scenario, the framework is used to study how drivers and price formation would change under a single EU-wide price.
AI.conclusion: Combining DNNs with SHAP/SSHAP provides a powerful, interpretable framework for analysing electricity price formation in complex, interconnected markets. The findings underscore that solar generation, gas prices, and interconnection flows are central to European electricity price dynamics. The approach offers a way to maintain high predictive performance while gaining economic insight, supporting better-informed policy, planning, and market design decisions, including considerations around further market integration in Europe.

title: Morpheus: A Morphology-Aware Neural Tokenizer and Word Embedder for Turkish
summary: Turkish is agglutinative: meaning is carried by morphemes, yet the subword tokenizers that drive modern language models split words by corpus statistics, fragmenting semantically loaded suffixes and -- in the case of WordPiece and rule-based analyzers -- failing to decode their output back to the original text. This paper presents \textbf{Morpheus}, a neural morpheme-boundary model for Turkish that is at once a lossless, morphology-aware tokenizer and a word-embedding producer. A differentiable Poisson-binomial dynamic program turns per-character boundary probabilities into soft morpheme memberships during training and exact segments at inference, with no string normalization, so $\mathrm{decode}(\mathrm{encode}(w)) = w$ holds by construction. Because the model is neural, the same forward pass that tokenizes also emits a structured word embedding. Among reversible tokenizers -- the only ones valid for generation -- Morpheus attains the lowest bits-per-character ($1.425$), roughly doubles the gold morphological alignment of the subword family (MorphScore macro-F1 $0.61$ vs.\ ${\sim}0.32$), and uses ${\sim}19\%$ less GPU memory than 64K-vocabulary subword tokenizers. As an embedder, frozen Morpheus vectors lead on lexical retrieval (root-family MAP $0.85$) and same-root verification (ROC-AUC $1.00$), surpassing the multilingual retriever BGE-M3 and BERTurk; on context- and inflection-dependent tasks (NER, case/number probing) the heavier contextual encoders remain ahead -- a trade-off we attribute to Morpheus's root-centric geometry. Code: https://github.com/lonewolf-rd/TurkishMorpheus; model: https://huggingface.co/lonewolflab/Morpheus-TR-50K; interactive demo: https://huggingface.co/spaces/lonewolflab/morpheus-tr-demo.

AI.tldr: Morpheus is a neural, lossless, morphology-aware tokenizer and word-embedding model for Turkish that segments words into morphemes and produces structured embeddings, improving compression, morphological alignment, and certain lexical tasks over subword and large contextual models.
AI.motivation: Standard subword tokenizers like BPE and WordPiece are poorly suited for agglutinative languages such as Turkish. They split meaningful suffixes arbitrarily based on corpus statistics, harming morphological interpretability and downstream tasks that depend on correct morpheme boundaries. Moreover, many tokenizers are not reversible, meaning they cannot perfectly reconstruct the original text, which is problematic for generation. There is a need for a tokenizer that is both lossless (reversible) and explicitly morphology-aware, while also serving as an efficient word-embedding mechanism.
AI.method: The authors propose Morpheus, a neural morpheme-boundary model operating at the character level for Turkish. It predicts per-character morpheme boundary probabilities and uses a differentiable Poisson-binomial dynamic program to convert these probabilities into soft morpheme memberships during training, while yielding exact hard segments at inference. The design guarantees lossless encoding/decoding without string normalization, so encoding and decoding a word returns the original word exactly. The same forward pass that segments the word also generates a structured word embedding, making Morpheus both a tokenizer and an embedder. They compare its performance to reversible subword-based tokenizers and to strong embedding baselines including BGE-M3 and BERTurk across compression, morphological alignment, lexical retrieval, and contextual tasks.
AI.result: Morpheus achieves the lowest bits-per-character (1.425) among reversible tokenizers, indicating better compression. It nearly doubles the gold morphological alignment relative to subword tokenizers, with a MorphScore macro-F1 of 0.61 versus roughly 0.32, and uses about 19% less GPU memory than 64K-vocabulary subword tokenizers. As a frozen embedder, Morpheus provides state-of-the-art performance on lexical retrieval (root-family mean average precision 0.85) and same-root verification (ROC-AUC 1.00), outperforming BGE-M3 and BERTurk on these root-focused tasks. However, on context- and inflection-sensitive tasks such as named entity recognition and probing for case/number, larger contextual encoders still perform better.
AI.conclusion: Morpheus demonstrates that a neural, morphology-aware, and strictly reversible tokenizer can outperform standard subword approaches for Turkish in terms of compression efficiency, morphological faithfulness, and certain lexical-level tasks, while using less memory. Its root-centric embedding space is highly effective for root-related retrieval and verification but less competitive on fully contextual tasks, where heavier encoders still excel. This highlights a trade-off between morphological structure and contextual expressiveness, and positions Morpheus as a strong, efficient tool for morphology-focused applications in Turkish NLP.

title: Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics: A Formal Theory of Social Intelligence Emergence Through Long-Term Interaction
summary: Current conversational AI systems have made significant progress in language generation, personalization, and long-context interaction. However, most existing methods model social behavior through isolated components such as emotion modeling, memory retrieval, or persona conditioning, lacking a unified framework to explain the emergence of stable social relationships and social intelligence in long-term human-AI interaction.To address this, we propose the Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics Framework (HACD-H), a formal model of human-AI interaction as a self-organizing social cognitive system. HACD-H integrates emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency into a unified dynamical framework and introduces principles including multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics.We construct a conversational dataset with approximately 14,700 interaction turns and develop a theory-driven empirical evaluation framework. Results reveal a hierarchy of temporal persistence in social cognition, stable relational attractors, phase-transition-like developmental patterns, and a structured social cognitive energy landscape. Social intelligence shows a significant negative correlation with social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001), and interaction trajectories exhibit progressive energy reduction over time.These findings suggest that social intelligence emerges from long-term social cognitive coevolution rather than isolated conversational capabilities. HACD-H provides a unified theoretical foundation for modeling adaptive human-AI social interaction and developing socially intelligent AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HACD-H, a unified dynamical framework to explain how stable social relationships and social intelligence emerge in long-term human-AI interaction, supported by empirical conversational data.
AI.motivation: Existing conversational AI models treat social factors like emotion, memory, and persona as separate modules, lacking a coherent theory for how stable, intelligent social behavior emerges over long-term interactions. The authors aim to provide a formal, unified framework to explain and model the coevolution of human and AI social behavior over time.
AI.method: The authors design the Human-AI Coevolution Dynamics Framework (HACD-H), which formally models human-AI interaction as a self-organizing social cognitive system. The framework integrates emotional adaptation, relational organization, social memory, and personality consistency, and introduces concepts such as multi-timescale social cognition, relational attractors, trust basins, developmental phase transitions, and social cognitive energy dynamics. They build a conversational dataset of around 14,700 turns and a theory-driven empirical evaluation pipeline to test predictions of the framework.
AI.result: Empirical analysis using the HACD-H framework reveals: (1) a hierarchy in how long different aspects of social cognition persist over time; (2) stable relational attractors in interaction patterns; (3) developmental trajectories that resemble phase transitions; and (4) a structured landscape of social cognitive energy. They find a significant negative correlation between measured social intelligence and social cognitive energy (r = -0.391, p < 0.001), and observe that interaction trajectories tend to show decreasing social cognitive energy over time.
AI.conclusion: Social intelligence in human-AI interaction arises from long-term coevolutionary social cognitive dynamics rather than from isolated conversational skills. HACD-H offers a unified theoretical basis for analyzing, modeling, and ultimately designing socially adaptive and intelligent AI systems in extended interactions.

title: LegalWorld: A Life-Cycle Interactive Environment for Legal Agents
summary: Civil litigation is inherently a life-cycle process: what a lawyer drafts on day one constrains what unfolds at trial months later. Yet existing legal benchmarks evaluate isolated subtasks, and prior legal-agent simulators reinitialize each scenario from shared ground truth, leaving cross-stage causal dependencies unmodeled. We present LegalWorld, a life-cycle interactive environment that models Chinese civil litigation as a causally connected state chain of five stages (seven sub-scenarios), grounded in 75,309 paired Chinese civil judgments. We pair it with reusable infrastructure (local memory, global case memory, a Skill/Tool library) that keeps each dispute consistent across its full life cycle. Building on this environment, we construct LongJud-Bench to evaluate agent capability across all five connected stages. 18,992 ratings from 217 legal-background evaluators confirm that LegalWorld trajectories are procedurally faithful and role-consistent; and a capability-level cross-model evaluation reveals sharp divergences that aggregate scores cannot expose, with no single backbone leading across consultation, drafting, and courtroom advocacy. Detailed resources will be released publicly.

AI.tldr: Introduces LegalWorld, a life-cycle simulation and benchmark for Chinese civil litigation that models full-case causal dependencies across five stages using real judgment data.
AI.motivation: Existing legal NLP benchmarks and legal-agent simulators only test or simulate isolated subtasks and typically reinitialize each scenario from shared ground truth, failing to capture how early legal actions constrain later outcomes in a case’s life cycle. The authors aim to model and evaluate legal agents in a way that reflects the true, causally linked multi-stage nature of civil litigation.
AI.method: They build LegalWorld, an interactive life-cycle environment for Chinese civil litigation that encodes a case as a causally connected state chain spanning five stages and seven sub-scenarios. It is grounded in 75,309 paired Chinese civil judgments. The environment includes reusable infrastructure such as local memory, global case memory, and a Skill/Tool library to maintain consistency of each dispute across its full life cycle. On top of this, they construct LongJud-Bench, a benchmark that evaluates agent performance across all five connected stages. They collect 18,992 human ratings from 217 legal-background evaluators to assess the procedural fidelity and role consistency of the generated trajectories and to compare different backbone models’ capabilities across stages.
AI.result: Human evaluators confirm that trajectories generated within LegalWorld are procedurally faithful and that agent behaviors remain role-consistent. Cross-model evaluation at the capability level shows strong divergences between models across different stages of the litigation life cycle; models that perform well in one stage (e.g., consultation) may underperform in others (e.g., drafting or courtroom advocacy), and no single backbone model dominates across all stages. The benchmark also reveals differences that aggregate scores would obscure.
AI.conclusion: Modeling civil litigation as a causally connected life-cycle process yields a more realistic and discriminative evaluation of legal agents than isolated-task benchmarks. LegalWorld and LongJud-Bench provide an infrastructure and benchmark for assessing end-to-end capabilities in Chinese civil litigation, highlighting that different models excel at different stages and that comprehensive legal AI will require strengths across consultation, drafting, and advocacy. The authors plan to publicly release detailed resources to facilitate future research.

title: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
summary: To achieve deeper safety alignment for large language models (LLMs), recent efforts have studied how to push safety interventions earlier into the pretraining stage, primarily by filtering unsafe data or rewriting it into safer forms. We argue that pretraining-stage alignment should go beyond making the data safe: LLMs may compose seemingly benign knowledge and capabilities into unsafe behaviors. To this end, we propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, a pretraining-stage alignment method which regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring directly into language modeling, establishing a foundational capability that is subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training. Our experiments with 1.7B models pretrained on FineWeb-Edu show that Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces the success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Complementary to our real-world experiments, we also introduce a fully controlled synthetic environment, MedSafetyWorld, with a clear definition of safety and a reasoning structure under which models can easily generalize unsafe behaviors from safe data. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld further demonstrate a clear advantage of Safety Reflection Pretraining in preventing models from acting on unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data, compared with data filtering and rewriting. Taken together, our findings suggest that pretraining alignment should not only make the training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data.

AI.tldr: They add short, explicit safety self-reflection snippets directly into the pretraining corpus so models learn to internally monitor for harm, which improves robustness beyond just filtering or rewriting unsafe data.
AI.motivation: Existing pretraining-stage safety work mainly makes data safer (filtering or rewriting), but even purely safe data can let models learn capabilities that can be recombined into unsafe behaviors. The authors want an approach that shapes how models use knowledge, by building in self-monitoring and safety reasoning during pretraining itself rather than relying only on later alignment.
AI.method: They propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, where the pretraining data is regularly augmented with short safety reflections that explicitly discuss potential risks and safe behavior, teaching the model to integrate safety self-monitoring into its core language modeling. They train 1.7B-parameter models on FineWeb-Edu with and without this intervention, and also build a synthetic benchmark, MedSafetyWorld, where safety is crisply defined and models can easily infer unsafe actions from otherwise safe data. They run ablations in this controlled world comparing reflection pretraining to data filtering and rewriting baselines.
AI.result: On real-world 1.7B models, Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification and makes the models significantly more resistant to inference-time and finetuning-based attacks, compared to standard pretraining on filtered/rewritten data. In MedSafetyWorld, ablations show that models with safety reflections are much less likely to act on unsafe behaviors that can be generalized from safe data, and they outperform filtering and rewriting strategies on this dimension.
AI.conclusion: Simply making pretraining data safe is insufficient for robust alignment, because models can still generalize unsafe behaviors from seemingly benign content. Incorporating explicit safety reflections into pretraining better instills self-monitoring and safer behavior priors, complementing post-training methods. Pretraining alignment should thus aim to shape the behaviors models learn from safe data, not just sanitize the data itself.

title: Output Vector Editing for Memorization Mitigation in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models memorize and reproduce sequences from their training data, creating privacy, copyright, and security risks. Existing neuron-level mitigation methods equate editing with zeroing out neuron activations, but the activation only controls whether a neuron engages; the output vector is what writes to the residual stream and, through superposition, encodes multiple features. We propose output vector editing, a constrained-optimization weight edit that locates a small set of MLP neurons responsible for a memorized continuation and minimally modifies their output vectors to introduce a distractor in vocabulary space, redirecting their residual-stream contributions while leaving activations unchanged. Evaluating on four models from 360M to 7B parameters (SmolLM-360M, OLMo-1B, OLMo-7B, Llama2-7B), we center on OLMo-7B (whose open weights and pretraining corpus enable systematic mining) and mine 6831 memorized sequences, achieving up to 87.9% suppression. The 2.7$\times$ gap over zero ablation on the same located neurons shows the suppression comes from the output-vector edit, not localization alone. Four edit modes span a spectrum from aggressive suppression to minimal redirection; in ensemble they cover 96.5% of memorized sequences, while our recommended single-mode configuration reaches 81.5% with no catastrophic locality failures. We further identify a mechanistic boundary at ${\sim}14%$ of sequences unreachable by MLP-only editing; while these failures are not attention-driven overall, ablating the top contributing attention heads recovers 60--64% of them, with stronger recovery on continuations that copy tokens from the prefix, positioning attention as a complementary fallback rather than a primary mechanism. Edit mode ordering and the success-locality trade-off transfer across all four models, with success rates scaling with model size rather than family.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces output vector editing, a mechanistic intervention on MLP neurons in large language models that suppresses memorized training data continuations by minimally modifying neuron output vectors instead of zeroing activations, achieving substantially higher suppression with good locality and showing how far MLP-only edits can go before attention edits are needed.
AI.motivation: Large language models often memorize and regurgitate training data verbatim, posing privacy, copyright, and security risks. Existing neuron-level debiasing or unlearning methods typically treat editing as zeroing or ablating neuron activations, implicitly assuming that turning neurons off is the main control mechanism. However, MLP neurons in transformers contribute via their output vectors to a shared residual stream under superposition, meaning each neuron encodes multiple features. Simple activation ablation may thus be both ineffective and non-local, degrading model performance while failing to reliably suppress specific memorized continuations. There is a need for more precise, mechanistic interventions that target the specific contribution responsible for memorization while preserving the rest of the model’s behavior.
AI.method: The authors propose output vector editing, a constrained optimization technique that adjusts the output weight vectors of a small number of MLP neurons that are causally implicated in producing a memorized continuation. First, they locate neurons whose contributions to the residual stream are most responsible for generating a specific memorized sequence. Then, instead of zeroing the neurons’ activations, they keep activations fixed and minimally modify the corresponding output vectors in vocabulary space so that these neurons emit a distractor direction. This redirection reduces the model’s probability of the memorized continuation while maintaining locality. They design four edit modes spanning from aggressive suppression (large redirection of output vectors) to minimal redirection (small, targeted perturbations). They evaluate on four transformer LMs (SmolLM-360M, OLMo-1B, OLMo-7B, Llama2-7B), using OLMo-7B as the main testbed thanks to its open weights and accessible pretraining data that allow mining 6831 memorized sequences. They measure suppression rate (how often memorized continuations are no longer produced) and locality (absence of catastrophic side effects), and compare against neuron zero-ablation. They also analyze failures of MLP-only edits and examine complementary interventions on attention heads.
AI.result: On OLMo-7B, output vector editing achieves up to 87.9% suppression of memorized continuations, representing a 2.7× improvement over zero ablation of the same neurons, which confirms that the benefit comes from editing output vectors rather than simply locating influential neurons. Considering all four edit modes as an ensemble, they can successfully suppress 96.5% of the mined memorized sequences; a single recommended edit configuration attains 81.5% suppression with no catastrophic locality failures. They discover a mechanistic boundary: roughly 14% of memorized sequences are not fixable via MLP-only output vector editing. These resistant cases are not predominantly driven by attention overall, but when they ablate the top contributing attention heads, they recover 60–64% of those previously unreachable cases, especially when the continuation involves copying from the prefix. Across SmolLM-360M, OLMo-1B, OLMo-7B, and Llama2-7B, the relationship between edit-mode aggressiveness, success, and locality is qualitatively similar, and success rates improve with model size rather than being model-family specific.
AI.conclusion: Output vector editing offers a more precise and powerful way to mitigate training data memorization in large language models than traditional neuron activation ablation. By minimally redirecting the output vectors of a small set of causally implicated MLP neurons, the method substantially suppresses memorized continuations while preserving good locality, and its behavior generalizes across different model architectures and sizes. Nonetheless, there is an inherent limit to what MLP-only edits can achieve: a subset of memorized sequences requires interventions in attention, which acts as an effective fallback mechanism, particularly for copy-like behaviors. These findings deepen the mechanistic understanding of how memorization is implemented in transformers and suggest a practical, modular strategy: primarily use MLP output-vector edits, with targeted attention-head interventions for the residual hard cases.

title: User as Engram: Internalizing Per-User Memory as Local Parametric Edits
summary:
Personal memory in a language model is two problems: content and reasoning skill. The brain keeps the two apart (a sparse, local engram in the hippocampus for each episode, a slow neocortex for the shared skills that interpret it), so a new fact need not overwrite everything else. Most personalization today keeps a user's facts outside the weights, in a natural-language memory file or a retrieval index. When facts are written into the model instead, the standard recipe is the per-user LoRA adapter, which does the opposite of the brain, folding content and skill into one global weight delta. Writing a user's facts as a LoRA contaminates text unrelated to them; writing the same facts as local Engram rows leaves it mathematically untouched, resulting in a roughly 33,000x smaller memory footprint.
  We therefore propose User as Engram: store a user's content as surgical edits to the hash-keyed memory table of an Engram model, and carry the reasoning skill in one shared adapter. This layered design matches per-user LoRA's direct recall while delivering 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average, and never makes a single user worse at reasoning than the untouched base. The edit is a glass box: writing a fact switches on its lookup at exactly the trigger, adds the value the answer needs, leaves every other position unchanged to the last bit, and fails if written into the wrong layer. Because different users' facts land in disjoint hash slots, their edits compose: many users live in one shared table at once, stacking additively and losslessly, where a per-user LoRA, a single global weight delta, admits only one. Upon retrieval, a per-user Engram table does not grow with the population the retriever must search, so past ~100 facts it overtakes a retrieval pipeline on a 2.5x larger model.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new way to store personal user memories in language models using Engram-style keyed memory instead of per-user LoRA, cleanly separating content from reasoning skills.
AI.motivation: Current personalization methods either keep user data outside the model (retrieval, memory files) or embed it into the weights via per-user LoRA adapters. Both approaches have drawbacks: retrieval has search and scaling costs, and per-user LoRA entangles user-specific facts with general reasoning skills, leading to interference, contamination of unrelated behavior, and poor scalability across many users. The brain separates episodic content from shared skills, inspiring a similar architectural separation for LMs.
AI.method: Introduce 'User as Engram': represent each user's personal facts as local edits to a hash-keyed Engram memory table, while keeping general reasoning ability in a single shared adapter. Each fact is encoded as a precise, sparse key-value entry that only activates on its trigger pattern, leaving all other memory positions and behavior unchanged. Different users' facts are stored in disjoint hash slots, enabling additive, lossless composition in one shared table. The authors compare this to per-user LoRA and retrieval-based systems on direct recall and indirect reasoning tasks.
AI.result: User-as-Engram achieves comparable direct recall to per-user LoRA while delivering on average 5.6x higher accuracy on indirect reasoning tasks and never degrades a user's reasoning compared to the base model. Memory edits are provably local and non-interfering at the bit level. Because Engram tables are key-addressed and per-user retrieval does not scale with population size, beyond roughly 100 personal facts it outperforms a retrieval pipeline built on a model 2.5x larger. Memory footprint is also dramatically reduced: encoding facts as Engram rows yields around a 33,000x smaller footprint than storing them via LoRA weight deltas.
AI.conclusion: Separating personal content (as Engram-style key-value edits) from shared reasoning skill (in one global adapter) provides safer, more scalable, and more efficient personalization for language models than per-user LoRA or external retrieval. The architecture mirrors the brain's division between hippocampal episodic memories and neocortical skills, supports many users concurrently in a single model instance, preserves base reasoning ability, and significantly reduces memory and retrieval overheads while improving indirect reasoning performance.

title: Lost in a Single Vector: Improving Long-Document Retrieval with Chunk Evidence Aggregation
summary: Dense retrieval ranks one query vector against one document vector. On long documents, this interface can fail when a short but decisive span is weakened during document encoding before ranking. We study this failure mode as document-side early compression and introduce the Evidence Dilution Index (EDI) to measure how far a document-level representation falls below the strongest chunk-level evidence within the same gold document. Guided by this view, we propose DICE (Document Inference via Chunk Evidence), a training-free document-side strategy that splits documents into chunks, encodes them independently with a frozen model, and aggregates them back into a single vector while preserving the standard one-query-one-document interface. On LongEmbed, DICE improves retrieval across four backbones, with the largest gains on slices beyond 4k tokens: for Dream, Passkey >4k rises from 30.0 to 90.0 and Needle >4k from 23.3 to 74.0. Across 12,779 filtered samples, DICE yields lower EDI than the single-vector baseline in 92.8% of cases. These results establish document-level encoding as a practical and underexplored lever for long-document retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and quantifies a failure mode of dense retrieval on long documents and proposes a simple, training-free chunking-and-aggregation method (DICE) that preserves the 1-query-1-document interface while greatly improving long-context retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Dense retrieval typically represents each document as a single vector. For long documents, crucial short spans of evidence can get diluted or lost when compressed into this single vector, leading to missed relevant documents. Existing work focuses mostly on the query side or multi-vector retrieval, leaving the document encoding process itself underexplored as a lever for improving long-document retrieval.
AI.method: The authors formalize a failure mode called document-side early compression, where document-level embeddings underrepresent the strongest chunk-level evidence. They introduce the Evidence Dilution Index (EDI) to measure how much the full-document embedding falls short of the best chunk embedding within the same gold document. To tackle this, they propose DICE (Document Inference via Chunk Evidence): (1) split each document into chunks, (2) encode each chunk independently using a frozen dense retrieval model, and (3) aggregate chunk embeddings back into one document vector, maintaining the standard one-query-one-document retrieval interface. They evaluate DICE on the LongEmbed benchmark across multiple backbone encoders.
AI.result: On LongEmbed, DICE improves retrieval performance across four different dense retrieval backbones, especially for very long documents (>4k tokens). For the Dream backbone, Passkey >4k performance jumps from 30.0 to 90.0 and Needle >4k from 23.3 to 74.0. In a corpus of 12,779 filtered samples, DICE reduces evidence dilution in 92.8% of cases, as measured by a lower Evidence Dilution Index compared to the standard single-vector document representation.
AI.conclusion: Document-level encoding choices significantly affect long-document dense retrieval, and early compression in single-vector document representations can severely weaken decisive local evidence. The proposed DICE method, a simple training-free chunking and aggregation strategy, consistently improves long-context retrieval and reduces evidence dilution while preserving the convenient one-query-one-document interface. This shows that document-side encoding is a powerful and underexplored direction for advancing long-document retrieval systems.

title: TxBench-PP: Analyzing AI Agent Performance on Small-Molecule Preclinical Pharmacology
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) agents promise to accelerate drug discovery by compressing interpretation and decision-making loops, but practical deployment requires trusted evaluation on realistic program decisions. We introduce TherapeuticsBench Preclinical Pharmacology (TxBench-PP), a verifiable benchmark for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology and the first focused slice of a broader TherapeuticsBench effort across drug-discovery stages and therapeutic modalities. TxBench-PP tests whether agents can recover accurate conclusions from real-world assay data rather than memorized facts from literature. The benchmark contains 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, spanning mechanism-of-action (MoA) and pharmacodynamic (PD) reasoning, compound-target engagement, causal target validation, developability and safety, and translational efficacy. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers graded deterministically. Across 16 model-harness configurations, comprising 11 models and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The strongest configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi, passed 59.3\% of endpoint attempts (178/300; 95\% CI, 51.1-67.6), followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3\% (166/300; 47.0-63.6).

AI.tldr: TxBench-PP is a new, verifiable benchmark that tests whether AI agents can make correct preclinical pharmacology decisions from real assay data, showing that current leading models still fail frequently.
AI.motivation: AI agents are being proposed to accelerate drug discovery, but there is no trusted, realistic way to evaluate whether they can actually make correct decisions in preclinical pharmacology programs. Existing evaluations often test memorized knowledge or simplified tasks rather than realistic workflows, so the authors want a benchmark that reflects how agents would perform on real small-molecule preclinical decision-making.
AI.method: The authors construct TxBench-PP, a curated set of 100 evaluations derived from real-world preclinical pharmacology scenarios. Each evaluation is indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, and spans key reasoning domains such as MoA/PD reasoning, target engagement, causal target validation, developability, safety, and translational efficacy. Agents interact with the benchmark as if in a realistic coding/workflow environment: they receive workflow snapshots, inspect files, and must output structured answers. Grading is deterministic. They then test 16 model-harness configurations (11 underlying models) over 4,800 trajectories on these tasks.
AI.result: No model configuration was able to reliably recover the correct preclinical pharmacology decisions across the benchmark. The best-performing setup (Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi) correctly solved 59.3% of endpoints (178/300, with 95% CI of 51.1–67.6). The next best (GPT-5.5 / Pi) reached 55.3% (166/300, 95% CI 47.0–63.6).
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents, even state-of-the-art large models configured as program assistants, are not yet reliable for making preclinical pharmacology decisions from real assay data. TxBench-PP provides a rigorous, verifiable benchmark to track progress and stress-test future systems across the key decision types in small-molecule preclinical pharmacology.

title: RedactionBench
summary: Large Language Models are increasingly applied to sensitive domains that require redaction of personally identifiable information (PII). While redacting PII is a data cleaning prerequisite, existing benchmarks conflate extraction mechanics with privacy semantics. A public phone number is not equivalent to a phone number in a medical record. Whether information constitutes a violation depends heavily on who holds it, why, and in what context, fundamentally differentiating redaction from simple entity recognition. Grounded in contextual integrity, we introduce RedactionBench, a manually annotated benchmark comprising 200 diverse documents across 11 domains, mostly seeded from real-world sources. We also introduce R-Score, a novel character-level metric that treats semantically similar redactions equally and nullifies shallow formatting choices, such as varying masking styles for phone numbers. Evaluations across Named Entity Recognition models, entity extraction Small Language Models, and frontier models equipped with agentic tools demonstrate that contextual redaction remains an unsolved problem. A human evaluation with over 80 users on RedactionBench reveals a stark dichotomy in privacy perceptions. Annotators show consensus with target labels for mandatory redactions (89.4 percent) and safe text preservations (94.1 percent), but fail to agree on contextual redactions (47.7 percent). This variance demonstrates the subjective nature of contextual privacy and motivates R-Score, which decouples contextual ambiguity from strict precision. We compare 35 models across families and report their performance in redacting PII. Finally, we release RedactionBench to establish a baseline for future privacy-preserving systems, hoping to inspire efficient model design and standardized evaluations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RedactionBench, a benchmark and R-Score metric to evaluate contextual PII redaction by language models, showing that contextual privacy is subjective and current models struggle with it.
AI.motivation: Existing PII redaction benchmarks mostly test raw entity extraction, ignoring that privacy violations depend on context: who holds the information, purpose of use, and domain. A public phone number is treated the same as one in a medical record, which misrepresents real privacy risks and the task of redaction. There is a need for a dataset and evaluation method that capture contextual integrity and distinguish mandatory, contextual, and safe-to-keep information.
AI.method: The authors build RedactionBench, a manually annotated benchmark of 200 documents spanning 11 domains, primarily drawn from real-world sources, and label PII according to contextual privacy requirements (mandatory redactions, contextual redactions, safe text). They propose R-Score, a character-level metric that regards semantically equivalent redactions as equal and is robust to superficial differences like masking style. They evaluate a wide range of systems—traditional NER models, small LMs used for entity extraction, and frontier LMs with tools—on this benchmark. They also conduct a human study with over 80 annotators to quantify agreement on the different types of redaction decisions.
AI.result: Experiments show that all evaluated model families perform poorly on contextual redaction, indicating it is not a solved task. The human study reveals high agreement with the benchmark labels for clearly mandatory redactions (89.4%) and clearly safe text (94.1%), but low agreement for contextual cases (47.7%), confirming that people diverge significantly when context-dependent privacy judgments are required. The R-Score metric is designed to handle this ambiguity better by focusing on semantic adequacy of redactions rather than rigid token-level precision/recall.
AI.conclusion: Contextual PII redaction is fundamentally different from straightforward entity recognition and remains an open challenge for current language technologies. Because human judgments themselves are variable in ambiguous cases, evaluation must account for such subjectivity, which R-Score aims to do. RedactionBench and R-Score together provide a standardized baseline and measurement framework intended to guide the development and comparison of future privacy-preserving models and systems.

title: X+Slides: Benchmarking Audience-Conditioned Slide Generation
summary: Automatically generating slide decks from source documents is an important application of large language models (LLMs). Existing benchmarks primarily assess slide completeness and technical depth, while overlooking the target audience as a critical real-world factor. For instance, specialists demand rigorous proofs, whereas decision-makers prioritize actionable conclusions. To bridge this gap, we introduce X+Slides, a benchmark specifically designed for audience-conditioned slide generation. Built on a diverse corpus spanning 113 topics and seven presentation scenes, X+Slides employs a dynamic evaluation framework constructed from 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes. By assigning audience-specific utility weights to the same source-grounded probes, X+Slides reports four complementary metrics: Audience Coverage measures how much audience-essential information is conveyed, Domain-wise Coverage shows which information types are covered, Efficiency measures delivered utility per unit of attention cost, and Correctness verifies whether slide claims are supported by the source. Experiments on DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, and NotebookLM show that current systems can recover a substantial but still incomplete part of audience-essential information: at $τ_A=0.7$, DeepPresenter reaches a best Audience Coverage of 0.714, SlideTailor reaches 0.594, and the NotebookLM ablation reaches 0.853 while showing clear grounding differences. These results indicate that visual quality and broad topic coverage should not be treated as evidence support without source-grounded evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces X+Slides, a benchmark for evaluating how well LLM-based slide generators adapt content to different target audiences while staying grounded in the source document.
AI.motivation: Existing slide-generation benchmarks mostly measure completeness and technical depth but ignore that different audiences (e.g., specialists vs. decision-makers) value different kinds of information. This gap makes current evaluations poorly aligned with real-world presentation needs.
AI.method: The authors build X+Slides, a benchmark over 113 topics and 7 presentation scenarios. They create 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes (questions/assertions) and assign different utility weights to each probe for different audiences. Using these, they define four metrics—Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, and Correctness—to systematically evaluate audience-conditioned slide generation systems.
AI.result: Applying X+Slides to three systems (DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, and a NotebookLM ablation), they find that systems recover a substantial but incomplete portion of audience-essential information, with varying Audience Coverage scores (0.714, 0.594, and 0.853 at τ_A=0.7) and clear differences in how well the content is grounded in sources.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-based slide generators do not yet fully capture audience-essential information, and high visual quality or broad topical coverage should not be interpreted as evidence of faithfulness without source-grounded, audience-aware evaluation like that provided by X+Slides.

title: Beyond Scalar Scores: Exploring LLM-based Metrics for Clinical Significance Evaluation in Radiology Reports
summary: Reliable evaluation of generated radiology reports requires strict clinical accuracy, as omitted critical findings or mischaracterized radiographic observations can directly affect patient care. Existing metrics obscure this requirement by reducing report quality to a medically ungrounded scalar. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) possess rich medical knowledge, they likewise struggle to draw a reliable boundary between clinically significant errors and harmless variation. We study this boundary using ReEvalMed benchmark as testbed and evaluate metric-level clinical significance from detecting true clinical errors ("Discrimination") and tolerating insignificant variations ("Robustness"). Across 8 LLM evaluators under one-pass and two-pass settings, we identify a widespread discrimination bias: models effectively detect errors but also over-penalize harmless rephrasings. To mitigate this, we synthesize 4k report pairs and train lightweight interpretable metrics on Qwen3-8B and MedGemma-4B. Our trained metric sharpens the clinical significance boundary, surpassing 32B-scale medical LLMs and remaining competitive with proprietary models. Crucially, the more costly two-pass setting fails to consistently improve overall performance and mainly trades discrimination for robustness. These findings suggest one-pass trained metrics as the practical choice for cost-sensitive deployment, with two-pass inference reserved for settings where D-R balance is critical. We will release the dataset and metric.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves automatic metrics for checking clinical accuracy in generated radiology reports, focusing on distinguishing truly harmful clinical errors from harmless wording differences.
AI.motivation: Current automatic metrics for radiology report generation compress report quality into a single score that is not clinically grounded. They fail to explicitly capture the difference between clinically significant errors (e.g., missing a critical finding) and harmless linguistic variation. Even LLM-based evaluators, despite strong medical knowledge, do not reliably separate these two, which is dangerous for patient care and unhelpful for developing generation systems. A better, clinically meaningful evaluation metric is needed.
AI.method: The authors use the ReEvalMed benchmark to study how well different evaluators distinguish true clinical errors from insignificant variations. They define two dimensions: Discrimination (detecting real clinical errors) and Robustness (tolerating harmless variations). They systematically evaluate 8 LLM-based evaluators in one-pass and two-pass settings and observe a discrimination bias (good at error detection but over-penalizing benign rephrasings). To address this, they synthesize 4k pairs of radiology reports and train lightweight, interpretable metrics on top of Qwen3-8B and MedGemma-4B models, optimizing for a sharper boundary between clinically significant and insignificant differences.
AI.result: They find that all tested LLM evaluators tend to over-penalize harmless report variations while being good at detecting real errors, revealing a systemic discrimination bias. Their newly trained lightweight metrics, based on Qwen3-8B and MedGemma-4B, better align with clinical significance, surpassing the performance of larger 32B-scale medical LLMs and staying competitive with proprietary models. Additionally, they observe that the more expensive two-pass evaluation setting does not reliably improve overall performance; it mostly shifts the balance between discrimination and robustness rather than improving both.
AI.conclusion: One-pass, specially trained lightweight metrics can more accurately and efficiently evaluate clinical significance in radiology report generation than much larger or proprietary LLM evaluators. Two-pass inference is not generally cost-effective and should be reserved only for scenarios where finely tuning the discrimination–robustness balance is critical. The authors will release both their dataset and their metric to support further research on clinically grounded evaluation.

title: NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning
summary:
Neurosymbolic semantics is fragmented: classical, fuzzy, probabilistic and neural systems each define truth by their own inductive rules. NeSyCat, extending ULLER, subsumes them under a single inductive definition of truth, parametric in a strong monad and an aggregation structure on truth-values. NeSyCat has so far lacked an account of predicates and functions learned by neural networks.
  We provide NeSyCat Torch as the missing link and interpret computational symbols via neural networks, implementing the framework in probabilistic programming and tensor-based backends. We use the distribution monad for reference semantics and metric evaluation, and complement it by a monad for numerically stable, differentiable training: the lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring. For efficient training in batches, we furthermore employ a batch monad. The axioms are the source code: written once in monad-based do-notation, monadic bind performs marginalisation, lazily pruning unneeded branches.
  On MNIST addition, our HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy, while achieving nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog. However, unlike DeepStochLog, we stay in a uniform framework that applies to many first-order NeSy approaches. Namely, the construction is parametric in the monad; instantiating it with, e.g., the Giry monad extends the approach to continuous probability (working out a neural representation here is left for future work).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NeSyCat Torch, a neural-symbolic framework that unifies various semantics (classical, fuzzy, probabilistic, neural) under a single monadic definition of truth, and shows it can efficiently learn and interpret neural predicates/functions within first-order reasoning tasks like MNIST addition.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic systems currently have fragmented semantics: classical logic, fuzzy logic, probabilistic reasoning, and neural networks each define truth differently, making it hard to compare, combine, and systematically reason across them. The NeSyCat framework previously unified these semantics abstractly via monads and aggregation structures, but it lacked a concrete way to integrate neural network–learned predicates and functions into that unified semantics. This gap limited practical applicability to real neurosymbolic learning tasks.
AI.method: The authors extend NeSyCat by introducing NeSyCat Torch, which interprets symbols (predicates/functions) through neural networks in a probabilistic-programming-compatible, tensor-based implementation. They instantiate the general NeSyCat construction with specific monads: (1) the distribution monad for reference semantics and metric-based evaluation, (2) a lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring for numerically stable, differentiable training, and (3) a batch monad for efficient batched training. Using monadic do-notation, the axioms of the logic are implemented once; monadic binding then automatically performs operations such as marginalisation and lazy pruning of unnecessary branches. They implement this in HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch backends and test on the MNIST addition task.
AI.result: On the MNIST addition benchmark, NeSyCat Torch (in HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch variants) achieves higher speed and accuracy than Logical Tensor Networks (LTN) and DeepProbLog, and nearly matches the accuracy of DeepStochLog. These results are obtained while maintaining a single, uniform semantic framework rather than using ad-hoc or task-specific formulations.
AI.conclusion: NeSyCat Torch successfully fills the missing link between the abstract, unified semantics of NeSyCat and practical neural implementations of symbols, enabling learned predicates and functions to participate in a common first-order neurosymbolic framework. The monad-parametric construction makes the approach extensible: by swapping in other monads (e.g., the Giry monad) it can in principle handle continuous probability distributions and other semantics, suggesting a flexible foundation for a wide range of neurosymbolic systems. Future work includes developing suitable neural representations for such continuous probabilistic instantiations.

title: Beyond Reward Engineering: A Data Recipe for Long-Context Reinforcement Learning
summary: Long-context reasoning is an essential capability for large language models, particularly when they are deployed as autonomous agents that must reason over lengthy trajectories. Reinforcement learning (RL) has recently emerged as a dominant paradigm for improving this ability, yet existing work largely focuses on reward engineering while diverse training data remains scarce. We revisit this problem from a data-centric perspective and show that a simple yet effective data recipe alone, paired with a minimal outcome-based GRPO setup, suffices to substantially improve long-context reasoning. Our recipe targets three complementary task families -- retrieval, multi-evidence synthesis, and reasoning -- for which we construct and curate eight datasets totaling ~14K examples. Experiments on three models (Qwen3-4B/8B/30B-A3B) yield average gains of +7.2/+3.2/+6.4 points across seven long-context benchmarks, surpassing prior RL training sets. We further demonstrate that these gains transfer to agentic tasks, where continuing RL training on an agent-tuned model with our data recipe improves GAIA by +4.8 and BrowseComp by +7.0 points. We will release our datasets to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a data-centric recipe plus a simple RL setup that significantly improves long-context reasoning in LLMs and transfers well to agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing attempts to improve long-context reasoning in LLMs via RL focus mainly on reward design, while the diversity and structure of training data are under-explored and often inadequate. The authors aim to understand whether a carefully designed data recipe, with minimal RL machinery, can by itself substantially enhance long-context reasoning and better support autonomous agent use cases.
AI.method: The authors adopt a data-centric RL approach using a minimal outcome-based GRPO setup. They design a training recipe centered on three complementary task families—retrieval, multi-evidence synthesis, and reasoning—and build/curate eight datasets (~14K examples) aligned with these families. These datasets are then used to train Qwen3 models of different sizes (4B/8B/30B-A3B) via RL, and the resulting models are evaluated on a suite of long-context benchmarks and downstream agentic tasks.
AI.result: Across seven long-context benchmarks, the RL models trained with the proposed data recipe achieve average performance improvements of +7.2, +3.2, and +6.4 points for Qwen3-4B, 8B, and 30B-A3B, respectively, outperforming prior RL training sets. Furthermore, when continuing RL training from an agent-tuned model using the new data recipe, the authors observe gains of +4.8 points on GAIA and +7.0 points on BrowseComp, indicating strong transfer to agentic settings.
AI.conclusion: A well-designed, relatively small but diverse data recipe targeting retrieval, multi-evidence synthesis, and reasoning, combined with a simple outcome-based RL setup, is sufficient to significantly enhance long-context reasoning in LLMs and improve their performance as autonomous agents. This suggests that data design is a key lever for advancing long-context capabilities, and the released datasets can serve as a useful resource for future research.

title: Rethinking Reward Supervision: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation
summary: Post-training of reasoning language models is commonly driven by supervised distillation and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Distillation often relies on chain-of-thought annotations that are expensive to obtain and may themselves be noisy, incomplete, or partially incorrect; even when the final solution is correct, an imperfect rationale can interfere with learning. Reinforcement learning with verified rewards, on the other hand, typically compresses evaluative feedback into a scalar signal, obscuring which aspects of a response should be improved. We propose \textbf{Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation}, a framework that incorporates rubrics as structured, fine-grained feedback for on-policy self-distillation. Our method conditions the teacher model on criterion-level rubrics and uses it to provide token-level guidance on the student's own sampled trajectories. This design avoids treating a single reference rationale as the sole supervision target. Instead, rubrics specify what a strong response should satisfy, enabling more fine-grained credit assignment over the reasoning process than scalar reward optimization. We instantiate this framework with a two-stage pipeline that first learns to generate task-specific rubrics and then trains a rubric-guided reasoner. We evaluate on a diverse suite of science reasoning benchmarks and results show that rubric-conditioned self-distillation effectively converts rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance over the reasoning process, surpassing GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average.

AI.tldr: They introduce rubric-conditioned self-distillation, where a model uses fine-grained rubric criteria instead of single rationales or scalar rewards to guide its own reasoning-token learning, outperforming existing post-training methods on science reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training methods for reasoning LMs have shortcomings: supervised distillation depends on costly, often noisy chain-of-thought annotations, and RL with verifiable rewards collapses rich feedback into a single scalar, making it hard to know which parts of a response need improvement. The authors want a way to provide structured, fine-grained guidance over the reasoning process without relying on perfect rationales or opaque scalar rewards.
AI.method: They propose Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation. First, a model is trained to generate task-specific, criterion-level rubrics describing what a strong answer should satisfy. Then, a teacher model is conditioned on these rubrics and provides token-level guidance on the student model’s own sampled reasoning trajectories. Supervision is thus distributed across tokens based on rubric criteria, avoiding dependence on a single reference rationale and enabling finer-grained credit assignment than scalar reward optimization. The framework is implemented as a two-stage pipeline: rubric generation followed by rubric-guided reasoning training.
AI.result: On a broad set of science reasoning benchmarks, their rubric-conditioned self-distillation converts rubric-level criteria into token-level training signals for the reasoning process and achieves better performance than strong baselines, surpassing GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average.
AI.conclusion: Using structured rubric feedback as conditioning for self-distillation allows reasoning LMs to learn from fine-grained, criterion-based guidance rather than from single rationales or scalar rewards. This leads to more effective credit assignment during training and yields consistent performance gains over existing post-training methods on science reasoning tasks.

title: ScholarSum: Student-Teacher Abstractive Summarization via Knowledge Graph Reasoning and Reflective Refinement
summary: Abstractive summarization plays a crucial role in enabling efficient understanding of scientific literature, yet it inherently demands both linguistic fluency and factual faithfulness. Existing approaches often fail to reconcile these two requirements. Extractive methods rely on rigid sentence splicing that disrupts macro-level logical coherence, while large language model (LLM)-based generative approaches, despite mastering linguistic fluency, exhibit limited factual consistency. In this work, we propose ScholarSum, a hierarchical reflective graph-based framework that emulates a student-teacher writing process for fluent and faithful scientific summarization. ScholarSum first organizes the document into a hierarchical knowledge graph by segmenting it into semantically coherent units, whose multi-layered community structure captures global logic and macro-level themes. Guided by this global structure, the student generates an initial draft, which is subsequently refined through fine-grained evidence retrieval. To ensure factual consistency, a teacher-like reviewer then iteratively examines the draft, identifies unsupported content, and prompts targeted re-retrieval and rewriting until the summary meets rigorous quality standards. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ScholarSum significantly outperforms previous baselines in terms of both completeness and faithfulness. Our code is available at https://github.com/Xiaoyu-Tao/ScholarSum.

AI.tldr: ScholarSum is a student–teacher style, graph-guided framework that generates fluent and factually faithful scientific summaries by iteratively drafting and fact-checking against structured document evidence.
AI.motivation: Scientific abstractive summarization must be both fluent and factually correct, but current systems trade off between these goals: extractive methods preserve facts but break global coherence, while LLM-based generators produce fluent but often hallucinated content. There is a need for a method that jointly models a paper’s global structure and supports rigorous fact checking during generation.
AI.method: The authors build ScholarSum, which first segments a scientific paper into semantically coherent units and organizes them into a hierarchical knowledge graph with multi-layer community structure to encode global logic and themes. A “student” component uses this global structure to generate a draft summary. Then, a “teacher” reviewer iteratively inspects the draft to detect unsupported or weakly supported statements, triggers targeted evidence retrieval from the document, and guides selective rewriting. This reflective, graph-guided loop continues until the summary satisfies predefined quality and faithfulness criteria.
AI.result: Experiments on scientific summarization benchmarks show that ScholarSum achieves substantial gains over prior baselines on metrics of completeness (coverage of key content) and faithfulness (factual consistency with the source).
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical, reflective, graph-based student–teacher framework can effectively reconcile fluency and factual faithfulness in scientific summarization, outperforming existing extractive and LLM-based approaches. The released code enables further research and application of this approach.

title: Aligning Implied Statements for Implicit Hate Speech Generalizability with Context-Bounded Semi-hard Negative Mining
summary: Classifying implicit hate speech remains a challenge, as intent is often masked through insinuation and context rather than explicit slurs. Prior supervised contrastive approaches improve in-domain detection but can overfit surface cues and struggle to transfer across datasets. We propose ImpSH, a triplet-based framework that aligns posts with implied statements when available and uses context-bounded semi-hard negatives to focus learning on near confusions. We also examine AugSH, which forms positives via data augmentation. In controlled evaluations on IHC, SBIC, and DynaHate with BERT and HateBERT, ImpSH is a viable alternative to standard supervised contrastive baselines and often improves cross-domain performance under matched preprocessing and tuning budgets. Representation analysis using alignment and uniformity indicates tighter positive pairs with balanced global spread, and qualitative nearest-neighbor case studies illustrate typical false negatives under domain shift. These results demonstrate that aligning posts with their implied statements via context-bounded mining provides a more stable, bijective-like mapping to related insinuations, overcoming the volatility inherent in traditional clustering-based representation learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ImpSH, a triplet-based framework that aligns social media posts with their implied hateful statements and uses context-bounded semi-hard negatives to better detect implicit hate speech, improving robustness and cross-domain performance compared to standard supervised contrastive learning.
AI.motivation: Implicit hate speech is hard to detect because it relies on insinuation, context, and coded language rather than explicit slurs. Existing supervised contrastive learning methods help within a dataset but tend to overfit to superficial patterns and fail to generalize across different corpora. There is a need for representation learning methods that more faithfully capture the underlying hateful implication and transfer better across domains.
AI.method: The authors introduce two frameworks: (1) ImpSH, a triplet-based learning approach where each anchor post is aligned with its associated implied statement (when available) as a positive, while negatives are selected as context-bounded semi-hard examples—items that are close in representation space but not too easily separable. This encourages the model to focus on subtle, near-confusing distinctions in implicit hate. (2) AugSH, which constructs positive pairs through data augmentation instead of explicit implied statements. They evaluate both on implicit hate datasets (IHC, SBIC, DynaHate) using BERT and HateBERT encoders, under carefully controlled preprocessing and tuning conditions.
