They further analyze learned representations via alignment and uniformity metrics and conduct qualitative nearest-neighbor inspections under domain shift.
AI.result: ImpSH performs competitively with, and often better than, standard supervised contrastive baselines, particularly in cross-domain evaluations where generalization is crucial. Representation analysis shows that ImpSH achieves tighter positive pair alignment (posts are close to their implied statements) while preserving a balanced overall spread in the embedding space. Nearest-neighbor case studies highlight typical false negatives that emerge under domain shift, illustrating where and how the method succeeds or fails compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Aligning posts directly with their implied hateful statements and using context-bounded semi-hard negatives yields more stable and robust representations for implicit hate speech detection. This approach acts like a more bijective mapping between posts and their underlying insinuations, reducing the instability seen in traditional clustering-based contrastive methods and improving cross-domain performance. Augmentation-based positives are useful but generally less reliable than using explicit implied statements when available.

title: Approximate Structured Diffusion for Sequence Labelling
summary:
Sequence labelling, a core task of Natural Language Processing (NLP), consists in assigning each token of an input sentence a label.
  From a Machine Learning point of view, sequence labelling is often cast as a Linear-Chain Conditional Random Field (CRF) parametrised by a neural network.
  While this approach gives good empirical results, CRFs assume a finite decision span (eg label bigrams) which can limit their expressivity and hurt performance when long-range dependencies are required.
  We show we can leverage diffusion to train a CRF conditioned on an entire label sequence, with the caveat that the condition is on a noisy version of labels.
  We show experimentally that this method, in conjunction with approximate CRF inference, improves label accuracy with a 16.5% error reduction for POS-tagging.

AI.tldr: They enhance neural CRF sequence labeling by allowing conditioning on full label sequences via diffusion, improving long-range dependency modeling and reducing POS-tagging errors by 16.5%.
AI.motivation: Standard linear-chain CRFs in sequence labeling typically model only short label dependencies (like bigrams), which can be insufficient when labels depend on long-range context, limiting expressivity and performance.
AI.method: They introduce a diffusion-based training framework where the CRF is conditioned on entire label sequences, but with noise applied to the label sequence. Training incorporates this noisy conditioning along with approximate CRF inference to learn better sequence models that can capture longer-range dependencies.
AI.result: Experiments, particularly on part-of-speech tagging, show that this diffusion-conditioned CRF combined with approximate inference improves label accuracy, achieving a 16.5% reduction in error compared to standard baselines.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning CRFs on full (noisy) label sequences through diffusion is an effective way to extend their decision span and capture long-range dependencies, leading to tangible performance gains in sequence labeling tasks like POS tagging.

title: Efficient Financial Language Understanding via Distillation with Synthetic Data
summary: Large instruction-following models are powerful but costly to deploy, particularly in finance, where labelled data are limited by confidentiality and expert annotation cost. We present an efficient framework for financial sentiment analysis through distillation with synthetic data, transferring knowledge from a large instruction-tuned teacher to compact student models. The framework is designed for low-resource conditions, where a small set of real examples are collected and labelled by hand. The framework then clusters the examples and uses the clusters to select seeds for generating synthetic examples via structured few-shot prompting. Experiments show that clustering-based seed selection yields more representative synthetic data than random sampling, enabling compact models to achieve strong performance with minimal supervision. Notably, on a more complex and noisy text domain, the compact model trained on the complete synthetic-seed corpus even outperforms the teacher model, while remaining competitive on formal text. The framework provides a practical route toward resource-efficient domain adaptation in financial NLP with minimal human labelling effort.

AI.tldr: They propose a way to train small financial sentiment models using synthetic data distilled from a large instruction-tuned model, needing only few real labelled examples.
AI.motivation: Large instruction-following models work well but are expensive to run, and in finance it is hard to get enough labelled data due to confidentiality and expert labelling costs. There is a need for efficient domain adaptation methods that can leverage limited real data.
AI.method: Start with a small, hand-labelled financial dataset. Cluster these real examples and choose representative cluster seeds. Use structured few-shot prompts with these seeds to a large instruction-tuned teacher model to generate synthetic training data. Distill the teacher’s knowledge into compact student models using this synthetic corpus and compare clustering-based seed selection against random sampling.
AI.result: Clustering-based seed selection produces more representative synthetic data than random selection, leading to significantly better performance of compact student models under low-supervision conditions. In a complex, noisy text setting, a compact model trained on the full synthetic-seed corpus even surpasses the teacher’s performance, while staying competitive on more formal text data.
AI.conclusion: Carefully seeded synthetic data generation combined with distillation enables small models to learn strong financial sentiment analysis capabilities from very little human-labelled data. This framework offers a practical, resource-efficient approach to adapting NLP systems to financial domains with tight labelling constraints.

title: Improving Medical Communication using Rubric-Guided Counterfactual Recommendations
summary: Text-based telemedicine increasingly relies on lightweight patient feedback, however, such feedback primarily reflects perceived communication quality rather than medical accuracy. We introduce an LM-guided counterfactual recommendation pipeline that discovers and refines interpretable communication features such as tone, personalization, actionability and completeness in addressing patient concerns, without interfering with the medical content. These features are used together with patient-doctor interaction metadata to estimate positive feedback. At inference time, the system searches over low-cost ordinal feature changes and recommends minimal communication changes predicted to increase the probability of positive feedback, while independent auditor models test whether these gains generalize beyond the selection model. Across interactions, recommendations yield a mean +6.41% gain in predicted positive feedback probability under independent auditors, and are non-negative for 93.31% of recommendations. These results suggest that small, interpretable communication changes can capture most predicted gains while preserving the doctor's control over medical reasoning and final wording.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a system that improves patient satisfaction in text-based telemedicine by making small, interpretable changes to doctors’ communication style without altering medical content.
AI.motivation: In text-based telemedicine, patient feedback signals (like ratings or likes) mostly capture how patients feel about the communication style (tone, empathy, clarity) rather than the medical correctness of the advice. There is a need for methods that systematically improve communication quality—thereby improving feedback—while keeping the medical reasoning and content intact and under doctors’ control.
AI.method: The authors design an LM-guided counterfactual recommendation pipeline. First, they use language models to extract and refine interpretable communication features (e.g., tone, personalization, actionability, completeness) from doctor-patient messages. These features, along with interaction metadata, are used in a selection model to predict the probability of positive patient feedback. At inference time, the system searches over low-cost, ordinal changes to these features and recommends minimal edits to the doctor’s communication style that are predicted to increase positive feedback. Separate auditor models are then used to independently verify that the predicted gains from these recommendations generalize beyond the original selection model, guarding against overfitting or artefacts of a single model.
AI.result: On real telemedicine interactions, the recommendation system achieves an average increase of 6.41 percentage points in predicted positive feedback probability when evaluated by independent auditor models. Furthermore, 93.31% of the recommendations are non-negative, meaning they do not reduce predicted positive feedback, indicating robust benefits from the suggested communication changes.
AI.conclusion: Small, interpretable adjustments to how doctors communicate in text-based telemedicine—specifically in aspects like tone, personalization, actionability, and completeness—can capture most of the model-predicted gains in patient satisfaction without changing the underlying medical content. This preserves doctors’ control over medical reasoning and final wording while systematically improving predicted patient feedback, showing the promise of LM-guided, counterfactual recommendation systems for communication optimization.

title: Learning Robust Pair Confidence for Multimodal Emotion-Cause Pair Extraction
summary: Multimodal emotion-cause pair extraction (MECPE) requires reliable pair confidence over candidate pairs. Existing pair scorers commonly use pair-level cross entropy over valid candidates, which treats links mostly independently. This leaves the relative confidence geometry among competing causes under-constrained, allowing gold pairs to stay close to hard negatives or rely on incidental non-gold context. We study this vulnerability as pair-confidence brittleness and propose RPCL (Robust Pair Confidence Learning), a training-only framework for pair-confidence learning. RPCL encourages pair confidence to be both discriminative and stable: gold pairs are separated from row-wise hard negatives through a confidence-difference margin constraint, and clean pair predictions are aligned with predictions from a corrupted view where non-gold contextual utterance representations are partially corrupted. The original clean pair scorer and decoding pipeline are used unchanged at inference time. On ECF, MECAD, and MEC4, RPCL improves the three-seed mean Pair F1 over a matched base model by 2.58 to 2.83 percentage points in the full text-audio-video setting, and improves mean Pair AUPRC on all three datasets. Diagnostic analysis further shows larger gold-negative confidence gaps and lower margin-violation severity. These results suggest that explicitly shaping pair confidence is an effective training strategy for MECPE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RPCL, a training framework that makes emotion–cause pair confidence estimates more robust and discriminative, leading to better multimodal emotion-cause pair extraction performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal emotion-cause pair extraction models score candidate emotion–cause pairs using standard cross-entropy losses that treat each link largely independently. This leaves the relative confidence relationships between true causes and competing negative candidates weakly constrained. As a result, gold pairs can end up with confidence scores that are too close to hard negatives or influenced by incidental context, making the system brittle. The authors aim to explicitly address this pair-confidence brittleness and improve the robustness and reliability of pair scoring.
AI.method: They propose RPCL (Robust Pair Confidence Learning), a training-only framework that augments any existing pair scorer without changing its inference-time architecture. RPCL shapes the confidence geometry in two ways: (1) it imposes a confidence-difference margin between gold emotion–cause pairs and row-wise hard negative pairs, forcing a minimum gap in scores; and (2) it introduces a consistency constraint between predictions on clean data and on a corrupted view where representations of non-gold contextual utterances are partially corrupted. This encourages pair scores that are both discriminative (separating gold from hard negatives) and stable (robust to perturbations of non-essential context).
AI.result: On three MECPE benchmarks—ECF, MECAD, and MEC4—in the full text-audio-video multimodal setting, RPCL improves three-seed mean Pair F1 over a matched base model by about 2.58–2.83 points and also increases mean Pair AUPRC on all datasets. Diagnostic analyses show that RPCL enlarges the confidence gap between gold and negative pairs and reduces the severity of margin violations, evidencing more robust and better-structured pair confidence distributions.
AI.conclusion: RPCL effectively shapes the confidence landscape over candidate emotion–cause pairs, yielding more discriminative and stable pair scores. By enforcing margin-based separation from hard negatives and consistency under contextual corruption during training—while leaving inference unchanged—the approach improves MECPE performance and robustness across multiple multimodal datasets, indicating that targeted pair-confidence learning is a powerful strategy for this task.

title: SAGE: Stochastic Prompt Optimization via Agent-Guided Exploration
summary: Context engineering has emerged as a primary lever for improving AI systems without parameter updates. Recent work showing that textual gradients do not function as real gradients motivates treating automatic prompt optimization (APO) as black-box search. We introduce SPO (Stochastic Prompt Optimization), a framework for stochastic search over prompt space, and compare three strategies of increasing sophistication: error-informed random search, a genetic algorithm with evolutionary operators, and SAGE (SPO via Agent-Guided Exploration), a multi-agent pipeline with diagnostic code execution. Across three benchmarks, no single strategy dominates; effectiveness depends on the interaction of landscape structure with error type. We further deploy SAGE on a mental-health chatbot under a continuous optimization paradigm, where it compounds eight cycles of individually-noisy A/B tests into a statistically robust gain in next-day retention. We argue that coupling qualitative diagnosis with quantitative validation is what makes agentic optimization effective for open-ended task-oriented dialogue.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates stochastic black-box methods for automatic prompt optimization (APO), showing that different search strategies work better for different error landscapes and that an agentic, diagnosis-plus-testing approach can reliably improve a real mental-health chatbot’s user retention.
AI.motivation: Context engineering (prompting) is now a main way to improve AI systems without retraining, but recent work shows that textual “gradients” aren’t true gradients, so gradient-like prompt tuning is unreliable. This motivates treating automatic prompt optimization as black-box search over prompts and asking: which stochastic strategies work best, and how can we make them effective in real, open-ended applications like chatbots?
AI.method: The authors introduce SPO (Stochastic Prompt Optimization), a general framework for black-box search over discrete prompt space. Within this framework they instantiate and compare three strategies: (1) error-informed random search that randomly proposes prompt variants guided by observed errors; (2) a genetic algorithm that uses evolutionary operators such as mutation and crossover over a population of prompts; and (3) SAGE, a more sophisticated multi-agent system that performs diagnostic analysis (including running code) to generate targeted prompt edits, then validates them via A/B testing. They evaluate all three on three benchmarks and further deploy SAGE in a live mental-health chatbot, running eight iterative optimization cycles based on noisy next-day retention A/B tests.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks, the three SPO strategies show no universal winner; each works better depending on the structure of the prompt-performance landscape and the kinds of errors the model makes. In the real-world deployment, the SAGE-based continuous optimization process successfully aggregates eight rounds of noisy A/B tests into a statistically significant improvement in next-day user retention for the mental-health chatbot.
AI.conclusion: Automatic prompt optimization is best approached as stochastic black-box search rather than gradient-like optimization. Different stochastic strategies are preferable under different conditions, and coupling qualitative diagnostic reasoning (via agents and tools like code execution) with quantitative A/B testing yields robust improvements in challenging, open-ended dialog systems. Agentic, diagnosis-plus-validation approaches like SAGE can effectively and reliably optimize prompts in practical deployments.

title: As Easy as Rocket Science: Assessing the Ability of Large Language Models to Interpret Negation in Figurative Language
comment: 16 pages, 16 figures; for associated code and data see https://github.com/jrdowers/Negation-and-Fig-Lang; To be published in Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
summary: Figurative language and negation are two areas that challenge current language models, however, both are widely used throughout written and spoken language. Large language models (LLMs) are also widely used in everyday contexts where they cannot necessarily be tuned for a specific dataset. It is therefore essential to understand the ability of LLMs to correctly interpret text that includes both negation and figurative language. To investigate this, we develop a set of new annotations to an existing dataset of figurative language, and test a range of language models on the dataset. We find that the combination of negation and figurativeness can present a particular challenge, and that performance overall and across different negation types is particularly dependent on the prompt style used.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models handle text containing both negation and figurative language, using new annotations on an existing dataset, and shows that this combination is particularly challenging and highly sensitive to prompt style.
AI.motivation: Figurative language and negation are common in natural communication but difficult for current language models. Since large language models are now used broadly in real-world settings where they cannot always be fine-tuned, it is important to understand their ability to interpret text that involves both phenomena simultaneously, not just each in isolation.
AI.method: The authors augment an existing figurative language dataset with new annotations that specifically capture interactions between figurativeness and different types of negation. They then evaluate a range of large language models on this enriched dataset, systematically varying the prompts used to query the models in order to assess how prompt style affects performance.
AI.result: The experiments show that when negation co-occurs with figurative expressions, model performance drops compared to cases with figurativeness alone. The difficulty varies across different types of negation, and the accuracy of the models is strongly influenced by the way prompts are phrased, with some prompt styles leading to substantially better or worse results.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current large language models struggle especially with inputs that combine negation and figurative language, and that their behavior is fragile with respect to prompt design. This highlights a need for better modeling of these phenomena and for careful prompt engineering or further training if LLMs are to be reliably deployed in everyday contexts where such language is common.

title: SenFlow: Inter-Sentence Flow Modeling for AI-Generated Text Detection in Hybrid Documents
summary: Sentence-level AI-generated text detection (S-AGTD) for hybrid documents, where humans and LLMs co-author one text, faces two gaps: existing methods classify each sentence in isolation, discarding inter-sentence dependencies, and existing benchmarks omit the newest generation of generators. We construct MOSAIC, a benchmark of 16,000 hybrid documents over PubMed and XSum, generated by DeepSeek-V3.2 and Kimi K2 under stringent quality controls including a perplexity-consistency filter absent from prior benchmarks. We recast S-AGTD as structured prediction over the document sentence sequence and instantiate it as SenFlow, integrating graph-based inter-sentence propagation with linear-chain CRF decoding in a single document-level pass over a sentence graph. SenFlow reaches state-of-the-art performance on MOSAIC, with a +4.15 pp average Macro-F1 margin on cross-domain transfer, the hardest of three protocols of increasing difficulty. We further find that even after the perplexity filter equalizes overt cues, AI insertions retain a generator-dependent sentence-length gap that sentence-level detectors still exploit. Code and data: https://github.com/luojingkun22/SenFlow

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MOSAIC, a new benchmark for sentence-level AI-generated text detection in hybrid human-LLM documents, and SenFlow, a structured prediction model that leverages inter-sentence dependencies to achieve state-of-the-art performance, especially in cross-domain transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing sentence-level AI-generated text detection methods treat each sentence independently, ignoring contextual and inter-sentence relationships, and current benchmarks do not include outputs from the latest LLMs or strong quality controls, limiting their realism and robustness.
AI.method: They build MOSAIC, a 16,000-document hybrid benchmark over PubMed and XSum, with AI text segments produced by recent models (DeepSeek-V3.2 and Kimi K2) and filtered using a perplexity-consistency criterion. For modeling, they reformulate sentence-level detection as structured prediction over the document’s sentence sequence and propose SenFlow, which constructs a sentence graph to model inter-sentence propagation and applies linear-chain CRF decoding in a single document-level pass.
AI.result: SenFlow achieves state-of-the-art detection performance on MOSAIC, outperforming baselines by an average of 4.15 percentage points in Macro-F1 in the most challenging cross-domain transfer setting, and performs strongly across three evaluation protocols of increasing difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating document-level structure and inter-sentence dependencies significantly improves sentence-level AI-generated text detection in realistic hybrid documents, and even under perplexity-equalized conditions, detectable generator-specific artifacts such as sentence-length patterns remain exploitable by advanced detectors like SenFlow.

title: GraphPO: Graph-based Policy Optimization for Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become a standard paradigm for enhancing the capability of large reasoning models. RLVR typically samples responses independently and optimizes the policy using from final answers. This paradigm has two limitations. First, independently responses often contain similar intermediate reasoning steps, causing redundant exploration and wasted computation. Second, sparse final-answer rewards make it hard to identify useful steps. Tree-based methods partly address this problem by sharing prefixes and comparing branches from the same prefix to provide fine-grained signals. However, tree branches are still expanded independently. When different branches reach similar reasoning states, they cannot share information and repeat similar exploration. Moreover, tree-based methods ignore such dispersion and only perform local comparisons within separate branches, which can lead to higher variance in advantage estimation. To address this challenge, we propose GraphPO (Graph-based Policy Optimization), a novel RL framework that represents rollouts as a directed acyclic graph, with reasoning steps as edges and semantic states summarized from the reasoning paths as nodes. GraphPO merges semantically equivalent reasoning paths into equivalence classes, allowing them to share suffixes and reallocating budget away from redundant expansions to diverse exploration. Furthermore, we assign efficiency advantages to incoming edges and correctness advantages to outgoing edges, thereby improving inference efficiency while deriving process supervision from outcome. Theory shows that GraphPO reduces advantage-estimation variance and enhances reasoning efficiency. Experiments on three LLMs across reasoning and agentic search benchmarks show that GraphPO consistently outperforms chain- and tree-based baselines with the same token budgets or response budgets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GraphPO, a graph-based RL framework that represents model reasoning rollouts as a DAG to share equivalent reasoning paths, reduce redundant exploration, and improve reward/advantage estimation, outperforming chain- and tree-based RLVR baselines under equal budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods for large reasoning models either sample independent chains of thought or use tree structures. Independent sampling wastes compute by repeatedly exploring similar intermediate reasoning, and final-answer rewards are sparse, making it hard to credit specific reasoning steps. Tree-based methods partially address this by sharing prefixes and providing more fine-grained feedback, but they still expand branches independently, cannot share information when different branches reach semantically similar states, and only compare locally within branches, which increases variance in advantage estimates. A more global structure is needed to share information among semantically equivalent reasoning paths and to get lower-variance, more informative process-level supervision.
AI.method: The authors introduce GraphPO, a reinforcement learning framework that encodes multiple rollouts as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Nodes represent semantic reasoning states summarized from paths, and edges represent individual reasoning steps. Semantically equivalent paths are merged into equivalence classes so they share suffixes and exploration budget. The algorithm reallocates sampling away from redundant paths to more diverse regions of the reasoning space. For credit assignment, GraphPO defines two types of advantages: efficiency advantages assigned to incoming edges (how efficiently a state is reached) and correctness advantages assigned to outgoing edges (how likely steps from a state lead to correct outcomes). These advantages are estimated from outcome-based rewards but applied at the process level to update the policy. The authors provide theoretical analysis showing reduced variance in advantage estimation compared to chain- and tree-based approaches.
AI.result: Empirically, GraphPO is evaluated on three large language models across multiple reasoning and agentic search benchmarks. Under matched token or response budgets, GraphPO consistently achieves higher performance than standard chain-based RLVR and tree-based methods. The experiments show better reasoning accuracy and improved search/inference efficiency, validating that the graph representation and the split efficiency/correctness advantages lead to more effective reinforcement learning for reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: Representing reasoning rollouts as a DAG and merging semantically equivalent paths allows RL for large reasoning models to share information more effectively, cut redundant exploration, and obtain lower-variance, more fine-grained process supervision from outcome rewards. GraphPO’s graph-based policy optimization framework both theoretically and empirically improves advantage estimation and reasoning efficiency, leading to consistent gains over chain- and tree-structured RLVR baselines under the same computational budgets.

title: Beyond Tokenization: Direct Timestep Embedding and Contrastive Alignment for Time-Series Question Answering
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have given rise to time-series question answering (TSQA), which formulates time-series analysis as natural-language question answering. However, directly feeding raw numerical series into LLMs suffers from a tokenization bottleneck: Byte Pair Encoding fragments continuous values into unstable tokens whose embeddings lack meaningful metric structure, resulting in the loss of magnitude, scale, and trend information. Prior methods use patch-based encoders that split the series into fixed windows, locking in one granularity that breaks patterns and hides exact timesteps, through a separate module that rarely transfers across datasets with different lengths or sampling rates. To address this challenge, we propose CADE (Contrastive Alignment with Direct Embedding), a novel framework for TSQA built upon two key components: direct timestep embedding and semantic alignment. The proposed framework maps each timestep directly into the LLM embedding space through a point-wise linear encoder and MLP projector, preserving exact index-level access while eliminating the need for patching and padding. To further bridge the semantic gap between time-series and language representations, we introduce a novel one-directional supervised contrastive loss that aligns time-series embeddings with frozen class-name text anchors. Experimental results on the public Time-MQA benchmark demonstrate that our framework consistently improves performance across six TSQA tasks, outperforming both open-source and proprietary LLM baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CADE, a framework that directly embeds time-series timesteps into an LLM’s embedding space and aligns them with text via contrastive learning, significantly improving time-series question answering performance.
AI.motivation: Time-series question answering with LLMs is hindered by how raw numeric data is tokenized and encoded. Standard BPE tokenization breaks continuous values into unstable text tokens, losing essential numeric properties like magnitude, scale, and trend. Existing patch-based encoders mitigate this but force a fixed window granularity, obscuring exact timesteps and failing to transfer well across datasets with varying sequence lengths and sampling rates. There is a need for a representation method that preserves fine-grained temporal information and is semantically aligned with language representations within LLMs.
AI.method: The authors introduce CADE (Contrastive Alignment with Direct Embedding), a framework with two main components: (1) Direct timestep embedding: each timestep of the time series is mapped directly into the LLM’s embedding space using a point-wise linear encoder followed by an MLP projector. This removes the need for patching and padding and preserves exact index-level temporal access. (2) Semantic alignment: a one-directional supervised contrastive loss is used to align the learned time-series embeddings with frozen class-name text embeddings (anchors) produced by the LLM. This contrastive alignment narrows the semantic gap between numeric time-series features and natural-language concepts. The LLM backbone itself is kept fixed while the encoder/projector are trained to produce compatible embeddings.
AI.result: On the Time-MQA benchmark, which includes six TSQA tasks, CADE consistently improves performance compared to prior approaches. It outperforms both open-source and proprietary LLM-based baselines, indicating better generalization and effectiveness across diverse time-series question answering problems. The results empirically validate that direct embedding plus semantic contrastive alignment leads to more informative and LLM-compatible time-series representations.
AI.conclusion: Directly embedding timesteps into the LLM embedding space, combined with semantic contrastive alignment to text, yields superior representations for time-series question answering. CADE avoids the tokenization bottleneck and limitations of patch-based encoders, preserves fine-grained temporal structure, and transfers better across datasets. The approach provides a practical and effective way to integrate time-series data with LLMs for TSQA, and it may serve as a foundation for broader multimodal reasoning over numerical sequences.

title: G-IdiomAlign: A Gloss-Pivoted Benchmark for Cross-Lingual Idiom Alignment
summary: Idioms are difficult to transfer across languages due to their non-compositionality and weak surface-form grounding, making literal mappings unreliable. We present G-IdiomAlign, a gloss-pivoted benchmark where each idiom is anchored by an English gloss from Wiktionary. We further construct a high-confidence reference alignment set for reproducible evaluation. G-IdiomAlign supports two protocols: (1) a controlled Multiple-Choice Idiom Equivalence with typed distractors for error attribution; and (2) a Gloss-Contrastive Generation contrasting No-gloss and With-gloss inputs to isolate the effect of an explicit semantic pivot. Across diverse LLMs, a bias to literal translation is a dominant failure mode, especially when the target is a low-resource language. Glosses consistently improve Gloss-Contrastive Generation under an embedding-based semantic proxy, but performance remains modest, indicating substantial headroom in the open output space. Subsequent analysis on Qwen3-8B further suggests that cross-condition differences are concentrated more in attention heads than in layers, while better With-gloss generations coincide with stronger gloss anchoring.

AI.tldr: Benchmark paper introducing G-IdiomAlign, a gloss-pivoted dataset and evaluation protocols to study idiom translation in LLMs and the effect of explicit semantic glosses.
AI.motivation: Idiom translation is hard for machine translation and LLMs because idioms are non-compositional and not well grounded in surface forms, so literal word-by-word translations often fail. There is a lack of systematic, reproducible benchmarks that isolate and measure idiom-equivalence and the effect of providing explicit semantic explanations (glosses) during translation, especially for low-resource target languages.
AI.method: Create G-IdiomAlign, a multilingual benchmark where each idiom is linked to an English gloss from Wiktionary, serving as a semantic pivot across languages. Build a high-confidence reference set of idiom alignments for evaluation. Define two evaluation protocols: (1) Multiple-Choice Idiom Equivalence, in which models must pick the correct idiom translation among distractors of different types to enable fine-grained error analysis; and (2) Gloss-Contrastive Generation, where models generate translations in two conditions—without gloss and with gloss—to isolate the contribution of explicit semantic information. Evaluate multiple LLMs under these protocols, using an embedding-based semantic similarity measure for the open-generation setting. Perform a detailed model-internal analysis on Qwen3-8B, examining differences across attention heads and layers and their relation to gloss anchoring strength.
AI.result: LLMs across the board frequently default to literal translations instead of correct idiomatic equivalents, a problem that worsens when translating into low-resource languages. Providing glosses improves performance in the Gloss-Contrastive Generation protocol according to an embedding-based semantic metric, but overall generation quality is still only modest, revealing considerable remaining difficulty. The internal analysis of Qwen3-8B shows that the main cross-condition differences (No-gloss vs With-gloss) are localized more at the level of specific attention heads than entire layers, and that better translations in the With-gloss condition are associated with stronger attention to, or anchoring on, the gloss tokens.
AI.conclusion: G-IdiomAlign offers a controlled, reproducible way to evaluate idiom translation and the role of explicit semantic glosses in LLMs. Current models exhibit strong literal-translation bias and limited idiomatic competence, particularly for low-resource languages, even when given glosses. While glosses help, there remains significant headroom, and the observed attention-head-level differences and gloss anchoring provide concrete directions for future model analysis and improvement focused on better leveraging semantic pivots.

title: Enhancing Multilingual Reasoning via Steerable Model Merging
comment: 12 pages, 7 figures, 8 tables. Accepted by ACL2026 Findings
summary: Model merging is an effective technique for composing the capabilities of a multilingual model and a reasoning model. It has achieved promising generalization in multilingual reasoning tasks by aligning feature spaces of different models. However, the merged single model often fails to address the conflicts between source models, leading to suboptimal performance. In other words, the one-size-fits-all merging strategy may not align with the characteristics of different inputs which may require prioritizing certain models over others. To this end, we propose a Steerable Model Merging (ST-Merge) framework to modulate the contribution of each source model. To realize this idea, we introduce a gated cross-attention mechanism to weight or filter the two attended source models in an adaptive manner. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ST-Merge consistently outperforms multiple strong baselines on four multilingual reasoning benchmarks across 21 different languages.

AI.tldr: They propose a model-merging framework, ST-Merge, that adaptively weights multiple source models via gated cross-attention, improving multilingual reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing model merging combines multilingual and reasoning models by aligning their feature spaces, but a single, static merged model cannot resolve conflicts between the source models. Different inputs may need different emphasis on each source model, so one-size-fits-all merging leads to suboptimal performance, especially in multilingual reasoning tasks.
AI.method: They design Steerable Model Merging (ST-Merge), which modulates how much each source model contributes on a per-input basis. Concretely, they use a gated cross-attention mechanism that takes representations from two source models and adaptively weights or filters them. This mechanism lets the final representation prioritize one model or balance both, depending on the characteristics of the input.
AI.result: Across four multilingual reasoning benchmarks and 21 languages, ST-Merge consistently outperforms several strong model-merging baselines, indicating better generalization and handling of model conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, steerable combination of source models via gated cross-attention is more effective than static, uniform model merging for multilingual reasoning. By resolving conflicts between source models and tailoring their contributions to each input, ST-Merge achieves superior and more robust performance across many languages and benchmarks.

title: Sumi: Open Uniform Diffusion Language Model from Scratch
summary: Diffusion models have become a promising alternative to autoregressive models. Among these, uniform diffusion language models (UDLMs) permit any token to be updated at any step, in principle enabling more flexible generation. However, no UDLM has yet been pretrained from scratch at both large parameter scale and large token budget. Both autoregressive modeling and masked diffusion modeling already have capable models at scale that the community can study and build on; uniform diffusion has none. A scratch-pretrained UDLM at scale would provide a clean reference point for studying scaling behavior, generation dynamics, controllability, and trade-offs against established autoregressive and masked diffusion models. To this end, we introduce Sumi ("ink" in Japanese), a fully open 7B uniform diffusion language model pretrained from scratch on 1.5T tokens. Sumi performs competitively with autoregressive models trained at comparable token budgets on knowledge, reasoning, and coding benchmarks, while under-performing on commonsense benchmarks, where our education-heavy data mixture is a likely contributor. We release our model weights, checkpoints, and full training recipe, including a complete specification of the data mixture over publicly available corpora. We hope this release enables the community to study native uniform diffusion at scale and catalyzes work on its as-yet poorly understood aspects.

AI.tldr: They present Sumi, a fully open 7B-parameter uniform diffusion language model pretrained from scratch on 1.5T tokens, showing it can compete with similarly-trained autoregressive models and providing a clean benchmark for studying diffusion-based language modeling at scale.
AI.motivation: Uniform diffusion language models allow any token to be updated at any step, promising more flexible text generation than standard autoregressive models. However, there has been no from-scratch, large-scale UDLM to serve as a reference for scaling laws, generation dynamics, controllability, and comparisons with existing autoregressive and masked diffusion models. The authors aim to fill this gap so the community has a strong, open baseline for research on diffusion-based language modeling.
AI.method: They pretrain a 7B-parameter uniform diffusion language model, called Sumi, from scratch on a large 1.5T-token corpus using only publicly available data. The training recipe, including model architecture, diffusion setup, optimization details, checkpoints, and an explicit specification of the data mixture, is fully released to enable reproduction and further study.
AI.result: Sumi achieves performance comparable to autoregressive models trained on similar token budgets across knowledge, reasoning, and coding benchmarks, but lags on commonsense benchmarks. The authors attribute the commonsense shortfall partly to an education-heavy data mixture used during pretraining. The model, weights, and training artifacts are made fully available.
AI.conclusion: Sumi demonstrates that large, from-scratch uniform diffusion language models can be competitive with standard autoregressive models in several domains, though data mixture and training choices affect areas like commonsense reasoning. By releasing the full model and training pipeline, the authors provide a clean, large-scale reference point intended to spur systematic study of uniform diffusion language modeling, including its scaling properties, generation behavior, and practical trade-offs versus other paradigms.

title: Which Sections of a Research Paper Best Reveal Its Research Methods? Evidence from Library and Information Science
summary: Research methods are essential carriers of knowledge contribution in academic papers. Automatic multi-label classification of research methods can support knowledge services such as method retrieval, review generation, and research intelligence analysis. While existing studies primarily rely on titles and abstracts, abstracts often provide only limited methodological information, whereas utilizing full-text content faces challenges related to excessive length and information redundancy. Therefore, this paper proposes a segment combination strategy by partitioning the full-text content according to its physical postion. Using an annotated corpus of 1,954 full-text articles from three representative journals in Library and Information Science (JASIST, LISR, and JDoc), we evaluate the classification performance of various segments and their combinations across multiple models. Experimental results indicate that methodological information is distributed unevenly within the full-text content, with the middle-to-late and final segments exhibiting greater discriminative power. Furthermore, integrating bibliographic metadata with cross-segment combination strategies effectively enhances classification performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a segment-based strategy for multi-label classification of research methods using full-text journal articles, finding that middle-to-late and final segments plus metadata yield better performance than abstracts alone.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic research method classification mostly uses titles and abstracts, which lack detailed methodological information, while directly using full text creates challenges due to length and redundancy. There is a need to exploit full-text content more effectively for method retrieval, reviews, and research intelligence.
AI.method: The authors partition full-text articles into segments based on their physical positions in the document, then perform multi-label classification of research methods on individual segments and their combinations. They use an annotated corpus of 1,954 full-text articles from three Library and Information Science journals (JASIST, LISR, JDoc) and evaluate multiple models, also testing the integration of bibliographic metadata with cross-segment combinations.
AI.result: They find that methodological information is unevenly distributed across the article; middle-to-late and final segments provide more discriminative features for method classification than other parts. Combining multiple segments improves performance, and incorporating bibliographic metadata with cross-segment segment combinations further enhances classification accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Strategically selecting and combining specific full-text segments, particularly from the middle-to-late and final parts of articles, along with bibliographic metadata, is an effective way to improve automatic multi-label research method classification beyond what can be achieved using titles and abstracts alone.

title: Leadership as Coordination Control: Behavioral Signatures and the Recovery-Advantage Boundary in Multi-Agent LLM Teams
summary: Team science holds that leadership is contingent: it helps only under specific conditions, and capable, autonomous teams may need none at all. We ask the analogous question for multi-agent LLM teams: under what measurable conditions does process-level coordination control add value, and do those conditions match what team science predicts? We use behavioral signatures (majority lock-in, exploration, recovery from an incorrect round-0 consensus) and per-action ablations, clean because each controller is an explicit action set, not a monolithic prompt. We operationalize three classical leadership styles (transactional, transformational, situational) as controllers over a shared action vocabulary (explore, revise, accept, synthesize). A matched controller with the same actions but an arbitrary rule recovers no better than majority voting, so the theory-derived rule, not the vocabulary, does the work. Across four task regimes and three open-weight model families, no controller dominates by accuracy, as the contingency view predicts: transactional control matches a shared round-0 vote on all 12 (model, regime) combinations to within 1.3pp, and gains appear only on the one combination where the round-0 majority is unreliable (llama-4-scout social; situational +8pp over flat). A recovery-advantage account, tested with four boundary probes, says a controller beats plain interaction only where the round-0 majority is unreliable, the task is recoverable, and undirected interaction does not already repair it. These regions map onto contingency theory (leadership substitutes, path-goal redundancy, the situational readiness gap), so a largely null accuracy result is what the theory predicts, not a failure of the controllers. We read process-level coordination control as a contingency to be measured and theory-mapped, not a leaderboard to be topped.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when explicit process-level coordination (modeled on human leadership styles) actually helps multi-agent LLM teams, finding that benefits are contingent and mostly appear only when the initial majority answer is unreliable and not otherwise self-correcting.
AI.motivation: Team science suggests leadership is only useful under certain conditions; strong, autonomous teams may not need it. As LLMs are increasingly used in multi-agent setups, it is unclear when adding explicit coordination or control logic improves performance. The authors aim to identify measurable conditions under which such controllers add value and to test whether these conditions align with established contingency theories of leadership from human team research.
AI.method: The authors model three classic leadership styles—transactional, transformational, situational—as explicit controllers acting over a shared action vocabulary (explore, revise, accept, synthesize) in multi-agent LLM teams. They design behavioral signatures such as majority lock-in, exploration, and recovery from incorrect initial consensus, and run per-action ablation studies to isolate effects since controllers are explicit action sets rather than monolithic prompts. They compare controller-guided teams to baselines like majority voting and undirected interaction across four task regimes and three open-weight model families. They also include a “matched” controller with arbitrary rules but the same action vocabulary to test whether gains come from theory-derived rules versus the action set itself. Finally, they probe a recovery-advantage account with boundary cases to specify where controllers should or should not outperform plain interaction.
AI.result: No leadership-style controller consistently outperforms others across models and task regimes, consistent with contingency theory: transactional control closely matches the performance of a simple shared round-0 vote within about 1.3 percentage points across all 12 model-regime combinations. Meaningful accuracy gains emerge only in one setting where the initial majority is unreliable (llama-4-scout, social tasks), where a situational controller achieves about an 8 percentage point improvement over a flat baseline. The matched arbitrary-rule controller does not outperform majority voting, indicating that the benefit comes from the specific theory-informed control logic rather than the shared action vocabulary. The recovery-advantage probes support the claim that controllers help only when the initial majority is poor, the task allows correction, and unguided interaction does not already fix the mistake.
AI.conclusion: Explicit process-level coordination, framed as leadership-style controllers, should not be expected to universally increase accuracy in multi-agent LLM teams. Instead, its value is contingent: controllers help primarily in scenarios where the initial majority is unreliable, the problem is still recoverable, and unstructured team interaction does not correct the error. These conditions map closely to established constructs in contingency theories of human leadership (such as leadership substitutes and situational readiness). Thus, the largely null accuracy improvements are interpreted not as controller failure but as evidence that coordination control is a context-dependent tool to be carefully measured and aligned with theory, rather than a mechanism to universally outperform simple baselines like majority voting.

title: Dango: A Strictly L1-Only Large Language Model for Studying Second Language Acquisition
comment: 8 pages main text, 20 pages total including references and appendices
summary: We introduce Dango, a 1.8B-parameter large language model designed for controlled studies of L1-to-L2 (Japanese-to-English) transfer in second language acquisition (SLA). While previous studies have explored SLA in language models, they have predominantly relied on smaller or non-decoder models, limiting their ability to generate open-ended text and reducing their suitability as practical L2 simulators. We identify a key challenge when scaling models to this size: L2 contamination within the "monolingual" pretraining corpus used for L1 acquisition. To address this, we propose a filtering method to reduce premature exposure to English while preserving realistic, minimal exposure. We then fine-tune the model on LLM-generated L2-learning lessons to simulate the L2 acquisition process. Our evaluations confirm that Dango develops human-like L2 production patterns, outperforming both unfiltered and standard multilingual baselines. We release the model, data, and code to facilitate reproducible computational SLA research and learner-facing applications.

AI.tldr: Dango is a 1.8B-parameter Japanese L1 model carefully filtered to avoid early English exposure, then fine-tuned on synthetic English-learning lessons to study and simulate Japanese-to-English second language acquisition with human-like patterns.
AI.motivation: Existing work on second language acquisition (SLA) in language models mostly uses small or non-decoder architectures, which limits open-ended generation and usefulness as realistic L2 learners. Moreover, when scaling up to larger models, supposedly monolingual pretraining data often contains substantial L2 content (here, English), which contaminates the model’s L1-only stage and makes it hard to study controlled L1-to-L2 transfer. The authors want a realistic, controllable framework to study how a Japanese-native system acquires English and to build better L2-learning simulators.
AI.method: 1) Pretrain a 1.8B-parameter decoder-style LLM (Dango) as a Japanese L1 model. 2) Identify and mitigate L2 (English) contamination in the Japanese pretraining corpus by designing a filtering pipeline that removes most English while retaining a small, realistic amount of exposure. 3) After L1 pretraining, fine-tune the model on LLM-generated English-learning materials (L2 lessons) that mimic the experience of human Japanese learners acquiring English. 4) Evaluate Dango’s L2 production behavior against baselines: an unfiltered version of the model and standard multilingual models, analyzing whether the resulting L2 output shows human-like SLA patterns and improved learner simulation.
AI.result: The filtered, lesson-fine-tuned Dango model shows L2 English production patterns that more closely resemble human Japanese learners than both an unfiltered counterpart and standard multilingual baselines. It better captures characteristic SLA phenomena in L1-to-L2 transfer, indicating that controlling early L2 contamination and using targeted L2 lessons leads to more realistic L2 learner behavior in a large language model. The model, datasets, and code are released for the community.
AI.conclusion: Carefully controlling L2 contamination during L1 pretraining and then simulating an L2 learning phase via structured lessons enables a relatively large decoder LLM (1.8B parameters) to exhibit human-like second language acquisition patterns. Dango thus provides a practical, reproducible platform for computational SLA research and for building learner-facing applications, outperforming less controlled or generic multilingual approaches as an L2 learner simulator.

title: Language Models as Interfaces, Not Oracles: A Hybrid LLM-ML System for Pediatric Appendicitis
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can make clinical decision support more accessible by interpreting free-text documentation, but their direct use as diagnostic engines is limited by sensitivity to prompts, information order, and plausible but incorrect outputs. Structured machine-learning models offer more stable risk prediction, yet they require tabular inputs that are difficult to integrate with narrative clinical workflows. We present ClaMPAPP (Clinical Language-assisted Machine-learning Pipeline for Appendicitis), a hybrid system that uses an LLM as an interface rather than as the final decision-maker. ClaMPAPP extracts schema-constrained clinical features from note-like narratives, applies deterministic plausibility checks, and passes validated features to an XGBoost classifier trained on clinical, laboratory, and ultrasound variables. We evaluated ClaMPAPP on two independent pediatric appendicitis cohorts from German hospitals and compared it with end-to-end LLM baselines, including open-source and proprietary models. To preserve ground truth while testing free-text input, narratives were generated from structured electronic health records through template rendering and constrained LLM rewriting, with additional sentence-order permutation to assess positional robustness. ClaMPAPP achieved the strongest overall diagnostic performance in both internal and external validation while minimizing missed appendicitis cases, the key safety concern in acute triage. End-to-end LLMs showed unstable sensitivity-specificity trade-offs and greater degradation under narrative reordering. These results support an LLM-as-interface, ML-as-predictor design that separates natural-language usability from predictive inference and provides a more auditable pathway for clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: Hybrid system (ClaMPAPP) that uses an LLM only to extract structured features from clinical notes and then feeds them to an XGBoost model for appendicitis risk prediction, achieving better and more robust diagnostic performance than end-to-end LLMs.
AI.motivation: Directly using LLMs as diagnostic engines in clinical decision support is unreliable due to prompt sensitivity, order effects, and hallucinations, while traditional ML models need structured inputs that don’t fit well with narrative clinical workflows. The authors want a system that combines the usability of free-text interfaces with the reliability, stability, and auditability of structured ML predictors for pediatric appendicitis triage.
AI.method: They design ClaMPAPP, where an LLM parses note-like free-text narratives into a predefined clinical schema, applies deterministic plausibility checks on extracted features, and then uses an XGBoost classifier trained on clinical, lab, and ultrasound data to predict appendicitis risk. They generate realistic narratives from structured EHR data via templates plus constrained LLM rewriting, permute sentence order to test robustness, and evaluate ClaMPAPP versus multiple end-to-end LLM baselines (open-source and proprietary) on two independent German pediatric appendicitis cohorts (internal and external validation).
AI.result: ClaMPAPP outperforms all tested end-to-end LLM baselines in diagnostic accuracy on both internal and external validation cohorts and is particularly strong at minimizing missed appendicitis cases, which is critical for safe acute triage. End-to-end LLMs show unstable sensitivity–specificity behavior and are more adversely affected by reordering of narrative sentences, indicating poorer robustness to input variations.
AI.conclusion: Separating language understanding from risk prediction—using an LLM only as a structured feature extraction interface and a conventional ML model as the actual predictor—yields more accurate, robust, and auditable clinical decision support for appendicitis than using LLMs alone. This LLM-as-interface, ML-as-predictor architecture is a promising pattern for integrating free-text clinical workflows with reliable, safety-focused predictive models.

title: Trade-offs in Medical LLM Adaptation: An Empirical Study in French QA
summary: The development of large language models (LLMs) has led to an increased focus on their adaptation to specialized domains and languages, yet the effectiveness of domain adaptation strategies remains unclear. We present a study of medical domain adaptation using French medical question-answering (QA) as a case study. We compare continual pretraining (CPT), supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and their combination across three model families, multiple sizes, and three initialization types, explicitly disentangling adaptation effects from base model choice. We evaluate both multiple-choice (MCQA) and open-ended QA (OEQA) under greedy and constrained decoding using automatic metrics and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation. For MCQA, CPT+SFT most often achieves the best scores, but gains over SFT are small and frequently not statistically significant, making SFT a strong and cost-effective default. For OEQA, CPT consistently improves overlap-based metrics, while SFT often degrades generation quality; instruction tuning and CPT+SFT are preferred by LLM-based evaluation. Cross-lingual experiments further show effective transfer from French adaptation to English benchmarks. Overall, we provide practical guidelines for selecting adaptation strategies under computational constraints.

AI.tldr: Study comparing strategies to adapt large language models to French medical QA, assessing continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and their combination across tasks and decoding settings, and deriving practical guidelines.
AI.motivation: As LLMs grow, practitioners frequently need to adapt general models to specialized domains (like medicine) and languages (like French), but it is unclear which adaptation strategy—domain-adaptive pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, or their combination—is most effective and cost-efficient for different QA tasks and conditions.
AI.method: Use French medical question-answering as a testbed; evaluate three adaptation strategies (continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and CPT+SFT) across multiple model families, scales, and initialization types. Assess performance on both multiple-choice and open-ended QA, under greedy and constrained decoding. Use automatic overlap-based metrics and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations, and run cross-lingual transfer tests to English benchmarks.
AI.result: For multiple-choice QA, CPT+SFT generally gives the best scores, but improvements over SFT alone are often small and not statistically significant, making SFT a strong baseline. For open-ended QA, CPT reliably improves overlap-based metrics, whereas SFT alone can degrade generation quality; instruction tuning and CPT+SFT perform best according to LLM-judge assessments. French-domain adaptation also transfers effectively to English evaluation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Domain adaptation strategy should depend on task type and resource constraints: SFT is usually sufficient and cost-effective for multiple-choice medical QA, while CPT (possibly combined with SFT and instruction tuning) is preferable for open-ended QA quality. French medical adaptation yields benefits that transfer cross-lingually, and the study offers concrete guidance on choosing adaptation methods under limited computational budgets.

title: RECOM: A Validity Discrimination Tradeoff in Automatic Metrics for Open Ended Reddit Question Answering
summary: Automatic metrics are the default for evaluating LLM-generated text, yet a metric is quietly asked to do two jobs: tell genuine content alignment from surface coincidence (validity), and tell a better system from a worse one (discriminative power). On open-ended, opinion-driven question answering, the two are in tension. We introduce RECOM (Reddit Evaluation for Correspondence of Models), a contamination-free evaluation dataset of 15,000 r/AskReddit questions (September 2025), each paired with its authentic community replies, which postdate every evaluated model's training cutoff. Scoring five open-source LLMs (7--10B) against every reply each metric paired with a random-derangement noise floor we find that no metric does both jobs well. Cosine similarity separates real from random answers (Cohen's $d \approx 2$) but cannot rank the five models ($|d| < 0.1$); BERTScore precision appears to rank the models (raw $|d|$ up to 0.63), but once response length is controlled this collapses to $|d| = 0.09$ and its validity is weak ($d \approx 0.8$, versus cosine's $\approx 2$). Because every metric scores the same outputs, this validity--discrimination tradeoff is a property of the metrics, not the models, and we argue it stems from representation design. Three independent LLM judges reproduce the validity gap and likewise separate the five models only weakly. We recommend reporting metrics on both axes, with an explicit random-baseline floor. RECOM is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/recom-D4B0

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RECOM, a contamination‑free Reddit-based benchmark to separately measure validity and discriminative power of automatic metrics for open-ended LLM evaluation, showing that existing metrics cannot do both well and urging two-axis reporting with random baselines.
AI.motivation: Automatic metrics for LLM-generated text are widely used, but they are implicitly expected to serve two roles: (1) validity—distinguishing genuine alignment with human-written content from superficial similarity, and (2) discriminative power—reliably ranking models by quality. On open-ended, opinion-like question answering, these two roles conflict, and current benchmarks are often contaminated by appearing in training data. The authors want a clean setting where they can rigorously test whether common metrics truly distinguish real from random answers and whether they can meaningfully separate model quality.
AI.method: The authors build RECOM, a dataset of 15,000 r/AskReddit questions from September 2025, each paired with authentic community replies that postdate the training cutoff of all evaluated models, thus avoiding contamination. They evaluate five open-source 7–10B LLMs on these questions. For each metric, they compare model outputs to every authentic reply and compute scores. They also construct a random-derangement baseline (shuffled mismatched answers) to measure how well metrics separate real from random alignments. They analyze effect sizes (Cohen’s d) for (a) validity—distinguishing real vs random—and (b) discriminative power—separating the five models, including controls for confounds like response length.
AI.result: Cosine similarity in embedding space shows strong validity: it clearly separates real from random answers with a large effect size (Cohen’s d ≈ 2), but it fails to discriminate between the five models, with effect sizes |d| < 0.1. BERTScore precision seems to distinguish models at first (raw |d| up to 0.63), but once they control for response length this drops to |d| = 0.09, and its validity for real vs random is only moderate (d ≈ 0.8, far below cosine). Across metrics, none achieves both strong validity and strong model discrimination. Because all metrics are applied to the same model outputs, the observed tradeoff is attributed to the metrics themselves, likely due to how their text representations are designed. Three independent LLM-based judges replicate the weak validity–discrimination pattern: they also show a validity gap and only weak separation among models.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental tradeoff, in current practice, between a metric’s ability to validate genuine content correspondence and its ability to discriminate between models in open-ended, opinion-driven QA. Popular automatic metrics either separate real from random well but cannot rank models, or appear to rank models but rest on weak validity and confounds like length. This tradeoff arises from metric design, not the underlying models. The authors recommend that evaluation reports always include both a validity axis and a discriminative axis, plus an explicit random-baseline floor, rather than treating single scalar scores as trustworthy rankings. They release the RECOM benchmark to facilitate cleaner, contamination-free studies and better metric development.

title: DreamReasoner-8B: Block-Size Curriculum Learning for Diffusion Reasoning Models
summary: Block diffusion language models accelerate decoding through parallel block-wise denoising, yet whether they can be reliably scaled for long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning remains unresolved. To this end, we develop DreamReasoner-8B, an open-source block diffusion reasoning model, and conduct a systematic study of how training and inference block sizes affect long-CoT reasoning. Our analysis reveals a stark performance disparity: training with large block sizes yields remarkably poor reasoning, whereas small block sizes preserve effective reasoning. To bridge this granularity gap, we propose block-size curriculum learning, which gradually transitions training from fine-grained to coarse-grained block sizes, thereby overcoming this limitation and enabling strong reasoning performance that generalizes across diverse inference block sizes. On mathematical and code reasoning benchmarks, DreamReasoner-8B achieves results competitive with leading open autoregressive models such as Qwen3-8B. This work establishes a practical foundation for efficient, reasoning-capable diffusion language models. We release our model at https://github.com/DreamLM/DreamReasoner.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DreamReasoner-8B, a diffusion-based language model optimized for long chain-of-thought reasoning via parallel block-wise denoising, and shows that a block-size curriculum is key to maintaining strong reasoning while enabling efficient decoding.
AI.motivation: Block diffusion language models promise faster decoding by denoising text in parallel blocks instead of token-by-token, but it is unclear whether this approach can scale to tasks requiring long chain-of-thought reasoning. Existing work does not systematically study how the choice of block size during training and inference affects reasoning quality, especially for mathematical and code reasoning where long, precise CoT is critical. The authors aim to understand and overcome the limitations that prevent diffusion LMs from matching strong autoregressive models on such reasoning tasks.
AI.method: The authors build DreamReasoner-8B, an 8B-parameter open-source diffusion language model designed for reasoning. They systematically vary block sizes used in both training and inference to evaluate their impact on long-CoT performance. They observe that models trained directly with large block sizes suffer severe reasoning degradation. To address this, they introduce block-size curriculum learning: training starts with small, fine-grained blocks that preserve local reasoning structure and gradually shifts to larger, coarser blocks. This curriculum is intended to maintain reasoning ability while making the model robust to a wide range of inference block sizes and benefiting from parallel decoding. They then benchmark DreamReasoner-8B on mathematical and code reasoning datasets, comparing it to strong autoregressive baselines such as Qwen3-8B.
AI.result: Empirical analysis shows a clear gap between training with small versus large block sizes: large-block training leads to poor chain-of-thought reasoning, while small-block training retains strong reasoning. The proposed block-size curriculum successfully closes much of this gap, enabling DreamReasoner-8B to retain reasoning quality even when using larger blocks at inference. On math and code reasoning benchmarks, DreamReasoner-8B reaches performance competitive with leading autoregressive models like Qwen3-8B, while preserving the efficiency benefits of block diffusion decoding.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that naive scaling of block sizes in diffusion language models harms long chain-of-thought reasoning, but a carefully designed block-size curriculum can reconcile efficient parallel block-wise denoising with strong reasoning ability. DreamReasoner-8B shows that diffusion-based LMs can approach the reasoning performance of top autoregressive models on math and code tasks, providing a practical framework and open-source implementation for future research on efficient reasoning-capable diffusion LMs.

title: Enhancing Decision-Making with Large Language Models through Multi-Agent Fictitious Play
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have demonstrated great potential in solving tasks with execution complexity, by distributing subtasks across cooperative agents. However, this divide-and-conquer paradigm falls short on decision-making tasks that are also prevalent in the real world. These tasks require simultaneous reasoning from the stances of all involved stakeholders whose decisions are mutually dependent and thus cannot be solved in isolation. We characterize this challenge as stance entanglement, a form of decision complexity distinct from execution complexity. To address it, we propose Multi-Agent Fictitious Play (MAFP), a novel MAS paradigm that represents stakeholder stances as agents and formulates decision-making as an equilibrium-seeking process. Built on the game-theoretic principle of fictitious play, MAFP iteratively updates each agent's decision by best responding to the empirical mixture of other agents' past decisions. This enables agents to expose and address one another's weaknesses, progressively improving decision quality and robustness. We evaluate MAFP on challenging decision-making tasks that test the capability of deciding strategies for competitive scenarios prior to acting. MAFP outperforms both single-round and multi-round baselines on two complementary metrics, tournament strength and robustness, demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing stance entanglement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Multi-Agent Fictitious Play (MAFP), a game-theoretic multi-agent framework for LLMs that tackles decision-making problems with interdependent stakeholder stances (stance entanglement), improving robustness and performance over existing baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems work well for tasks that can be decomposed into independent subtasks (execution complexity), but perform poorly on decision-making problems where multiple stakeholders’ decisions are interdependent and cannot be solved in isolation. The authors identify this limitation as stance entanglement and aim to design a framework that can jointly reason over mutually dependent stances and reach robust, high-quality decisions in such settings.
AI.method: The authors propose Multi-Agent Fictitious Play (MAFP), a paradigm where each stakeholder’s stance is represented by an agent. Decision-making is formulated as an equilibrium-seeking process based on the game-theoretic concept of fictitious play. In MAFP, agents iteratively update their decisions by best responding to the empirical mixture (history) of the other agents’ past decisions. Through this iterative best-response process, agents successively uncover and address weaknesses in each other’s strategies, aiming to converge to more robust joint decisions.
AI.result: MAFP is evaluated on challenging decision-making tasks that require planning strategies for competitive scenarios before any actual action is taken. Compared with single-round and multi-round baseline methods, MAFP achieves better performance on two key metrics: (1) tournament strength, reflecting how well the resulting strategies perform in head-to-head competitive evaluations, and (2) robustness, capturing how stable and reliable the decisions are under variation or adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that stance entanglement is a key form of decision complexity that standard divide-and-conquer LLM-based MAS approaches do not address. By modeling stakeholder stances as interacting agents engaged in fictitious play, MAFP effectively tackles this complexity, yielding stronger and more robust decisions on competitive, multi-stakeholder tasks than existing single- and multi-round baselines.

title: Freeing the Law with LOCUS: A Local Ordinance Corpus for the United States
summary: Progress in legal AI increasingly depends on access to authoritative legal text at scale. Yet one of the most consequential layers of American law remains largely absent from existing machine-readable corpora: local ordinances. Local codes govern zoning, housing, business licensing, public health, noise, animal control, and many other domains of everyday regulation, but they are fragmented across vendor platforms designed for human browsing rather than bulk research access. We introduce LOCUS - the Local Ordinance Corpus for the United States - a comprehensive corpus and county-harmonized access layer for U.S. municipal and county ordinance codes. The raw corpus, available for release to researchers, represents nearly all publicly available municipal and county ordinance codes. The resulting raw corpus contains codes from 9,239 cities and counties. A smaller county-harmonized LOCUS access layer provides coverage for the largest 2,309 of 3,144 U.S. counties, accounting for a majority of the population. We use OCR to handle the myriad of document formats that have kept the law from being a public resource. We release the corpus with coverage metadata to support reproducibility, downstream legal AI research, and the incremental expansion of machine-readable access to local law. We train a collection of ModernBERT-based classifiers and scorers to facilitate analyzing U.S. local law among several dimensions, such as opacity and paternalism, that have not previously been studied at this scale. LOCUS-v1 and its derivative models are available at: https://huggingface.co/datasets/LocalLaws/LOCUS-v1

AI.tldr: The paper presents LOCUS, a large-scale, machine-readable corpus of U.S. local ordinances plus supporting models, to enable legal AI research on local law.
AI.motivation: Legal AI requires broad, machine-readable access to legal texts, but U.S. local ordinances—governing key everyday regulations—have been missing from existing corpora due to fragmentation, vendor lock-in, and non-ML-friendly formats. This gap hinders research on an important and consequential layer of law.
AI.method: The authors construct LOCUS by collecting nearly all publicly available municipal and county ordinance codes across the U.S., using OCR to convert diverse document formats into machine-readable text. They organize a raw corpus for 9,239 cities and counties and build a county-harmonized access layer covering the largest 2,309 counties. They add coverage metadata for reproducibility and train a suite of ModernBERT-based classifiers and scorers to analyze properties of local law, such as opacity and paternalism.
AI.result: The result is LOCUS-v1, a large corpus of local ordinances plus a county-harmonized access layer covering the majority of the U.S. population, along with pretrained ModernBERT-based models that classify and score aspects of local law. The dataset and models are released openly on Hugging Face with detailed coverage metadata.
AI.conclusion: LOCUS fills a major gap in machine-readable U.S. legal corpora by making local ordinances accessible at scale, enabling reproducible and large-scale legal AI research on local law, including novel dimensions like opacity and paternalism. It also provides an extensible foundation for further expansion of machine-readable local legal data.

title: Learning User Simulators with Turing Rewards
summary: Learning to simulate human users in interactive settings could advance the training of agent assistants, evaluation of personalization systems, research in the social sciences, and more. Existing approaches generally do so by training a large language model (LLM) to match a single ground truth response, either by maximizing the log probability or by using a similarity reward. We instead propose {Turing-RL}: a Turing-Test-based reinforcement learning approach for training user simulator models. {Turing-RL} uses a discriminative Turing reward with an LLM judge to score how indistinguishable a generated response is from the real user's given the user's history, and the user simulator LLM learns to produce responses indistinguishable from what the user could have said with such rewards. Across two different domains--conversational chat and Reddit forum discussion--we find that {Turing-RL} consistently outperforms baseline methods on both LLM and human evaluation metrics. Our study suggests that optimizing for indistinguishability, rather than response matching, is effective for learning user simulators.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Turing-RL, a Turing-Test-based reinforcement learning method to train LLM user simulators using a discriminative LLM judge reward, and shows it outperforms response-matching baselines in conversational and forum domains.
AI.motivation: Existing user simulators for training/evaluating interactive agents typically train LLMs to imitate a single ground-truth response, focusing on exact matching or similarity. This may not capture the diversity and indistinguishability of real human behavior. The authors want a better way to train simulators that behave like real users, useful for agent training, personalization evaluation, and social science research.
AI.method: They introduce Turing-RL, where a user-simulator LLM is trained via reinforcement learning with a Turing-Test-style reward. An LLM judge acts as a discriminator, scoring how indistinguishable the simulator’s generated response is from a real user’s response given the user’s history. The simulator’s policy is updated to maximize this discriminative Turing reward, encouraging it to produce responses that a judge cannot distinguish from real user utterances, instead of matching a specific ground-truth reply.
AI.result: On two domains—generic conversational chat and Reddit forum discussions—Turing-RL-trained simulators outperform baselines that optimize log-likelihood or similarity rewards. The gains are observed under both automatic LLM-based evaluations and human evaluations, indicating improved realism and fidelity of the simulated users.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing user simulators for indistinguishability from real users via a Turing-Test-style RL objective is more effective than standard response-matching approaches. Turing-RL provides a promising framework for training realistic user simulators that can better support interactive agent training and evaluation across domains.
